metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/All Functional Capabilities That tt-metal Provides to the Compiler_209326236.md

content: The Allocator maintains a view of on device memory across DRAM and SRAM (i.e. L1). It assigns addresses for Buffers and maintains a record of what is free or in-use. The allocator does not interact with on-device memory and is not responsible for copying data to/from the buffers.

- 1 [Architecture](#Allocator-Architecture)
 - 1.1 [Types of Allocators](#Allocator-TypesofAllocators)
 - 1.2 [Freelist Allocator](#Allocator-FreelistAllocator)
 - 1.3 [Lockstep Allocation](#Allocator-LockstepAllocation)
 - 1.4 [Alignment](#Allocator-Alignment)
 - 1.5 [Buffer Size Calculation](#Allocator-BufferSizeCalculation)
 - 1.5.1 [Step 1: Compute Total Buffer Size (in Bytes)](#Allocator-Step1:ComputeTotalBufferSize(inBytes))
 - 1.5.2 [Step 2: Calculate Page Size (in Bytes)](#Allocator-Step2:CalculatePageSize(inBytes))
 - 1.5.3 [Step 3: Calculate Buffer Size Per Bank](#Allocator-Step3:CalculateBufferSizePerBank)
- 2 [Memory Management](#Allocator-MemoryManagement)
 - 2.1 [SRAM Buffer and Circular Buffer](#Allocator-SRAMBufferandCircularBuffer)
- 3 [Memory Management Issues](#Allocator-MemoryManagementIssues)
 - 3.1 [Memory Issues](#Allocator-MemoryIssues)
 - 3.1.1 [Memory Exhausted](#Allocator-MemoryExhausted)
 - 3.1.2 [Circular Buffer Allocation Failures](#Allocator-CircularBufferAllocationFailures)
 - 3.1.3 [Memory Fragmentation](#Allocator-MemoryFragmentation)
 - 3.2 [Optimization Techniques](#Allocator-OptimizationTechniques)

- 3.1.3 [Memory Fragmentation](#Allocator-MemoryFragmentation)
 - 3.2 [Optimization Techniques](#Allocator-OptimizationTechniques)
 - 3.2.1 [Move Operation](#Allocator-MoveOperation)
 - 3.2.2 [Pre-allocation Strategies](#Allocator-Pre-allocationStrategies)
 - 3.2.3 [Efficient CB Allocation](#Allocator-EfficientCBAllocation)
 - 3.2.4 [Pitfall](#Allocator-Pitfall)
- 4 [References](#Allocator-References)

Different types of Buffer have its own Allocator. Currently there are **4 types of buffers** as illustrated in below table. The bank managers for those types are initialized once the device is launched.

| **Buffer Type** | **Purpose** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **DRAM** | Stores large tensors, weights, or data that exceed SRAM capacity. | Offers high capacity with higher latency and lower bandwidth compared to L1. |
| **L1** | Used for intermediate tensors, activations, and circular buffers. | On-chip SRAM with low latency and high bandwidth; limited in size. |
| **L1\_SMALL** | Stores control metadata for core-level operations. | Reserved region of L1; size buffer type is dynamic and can be set by user when initiate device via `l1_small_size`. Used in OPs like convolution and haloing [#link-to-doc]. |
| **TRACE** | [#todo] | [#todo] |

For both interleaved and sharded buffers, the allocator first determines how many bytes need to be allocated per bank. Managing allocations and deallocations is currently done using a **free list**.

!image-20250602-045455.png

We use a **first fit memory algorithm** to allow both top-down and bottom-up allocations in the same memory region. Buffers are able to specify whether they should be allocated in lower address space or higher address space. This enables Buffers of the same class to be grouped together without being fragmented by other Buffer classes.

- For example, user data Buffers are allocated bottom-up in DRAM while Program binaries are allocated top-down to avoid binaries fragmenting user data space.
- A use case for this in SRAM for L1 Buffers and Circular Buffers is described in below section. Based on this, while *best fit algorithms* reduce fragmentation for Buffers of the same class they don't account for fragmentation caused by mixing different classes.

!image-20250602-060701.png

**Example:** First Fit Memory Allocation in L1 Bank

!image-20250602-060701.png

**Example:** First Fit Memory Allocation in L1 Bank

- The diagram illustrates the **First Fit memory allocation algorithm** applied within an L1 SRAM bank. Allocation proceeds in a **top-down** manner, starting from higher memory addresses toward lower ones. In this example, three buffers (Buffer 0, Buffer 1, and Buffer 2) have already been allocated, leaving **three free blocks**: one at the top ("Free Block 0 - Head"), one in the middle ("Free Block 1"), and one at the bottom ("Free Block 2 - Tail").
- When trying to allocate **Buffer 3**, the allocator checks each free block from top to bottom. Although Free Block 0 is checked first, it is too small to hold Buffer 3, resulting in a **No Fit**. The search continues downward until Free Block 1 is found, which is large enough - this block becomes the **First Fit** for Buffer 3. As shown in the right side of the image, Buffer 3 is placed into Free Block 1, and the memory layout is updated accordingly.

This example demonstrates how **First Fit** can minimize search time by selecting the first available block that meets the size requirement, even if more optimal placements might exist further down.

> ![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/plugins/servlet/confluence/placeholder/error?i18nKey=editor.placeholder.error&locale=en_US&version=2 "Cannot invoke \"String.indexOf(int)\" because \"href\" is null") **Note:** This hybrid of top-down and bottom-up allocations can also be enabled by dividing up available memory into different memory pools that are located at higher/lower address spaces and then allocating Buffers of a particular class into their associated memory pool. This approach has not been explored in detail and would first require collecting data to appropriately size these memory pools and then consider dynamic resizing.

TT-Metal follows a lockstep allocation scheme across the banks. This means that *each bank reserves the same amount of space for a given buffer regardless of how many banks the data is in*. Lockstep allocation is illustrated in figure below

!image-20250601-083720.png

- In this example the first buffer, `b0`, has a single page of data of `X` bytes but all banks mark `X` bytes as being in use. The second buffer, `b1`, has `n+2` pages and the first page in each bank will be allocated at address `low_addr + X` with a total of `2Y` bytes being allocated in each bank. Lockstep allocation eases on device address calculation for pages.

> **Note:** this example shows allocating interleaved buffers but a similar scheme is followed for sharded buffers.

Allocations in DRAM and SRAM are done at `DRAM_ALIGNMENT`/`L1_ALIGNMENT` aligned addresses to ensure that reads from DRAM into SRAM do not violate NoC alignment constraints. When sizes in the buffer config do not respect this alignment, allocator pads each page or shard. This leads to some internal fragmentation in the allocated region.
This section explains how the allocator computes the **buffer size per bank** for a tensor, based on its **data type** and **memory configuration**.

section_title: Step 1: Compute Total Buffer Size (in Bytes)
content:
The total buffer size is calculated based on the **tensor volume** and the **element size of its data type**:

```
buffer_size_bytes = tensor_volume * element_size_byte(dtype)
```

| **Data Type** | **Element Size (bytes)** |
| --- | --- |
| `BFLOAT16` | 2 |
| `FLOAT32` | 4 |
| `INT32` / `UINT32` | 4 |
| `UINT16` | 2 |
| `UINT8` | 1 |
| `BFLOAT8_B` | 1 per datum + 4 × 16B shared exponent |
| `BFLOAT4_B` | 0.5 per datum + 4 × 16B shared exponent |

- Tensor shape: `[1, 256, 256, 3]`, dtype = `BFLOAT16`
- Flattened shape: `[65536, 3]`
- Tensor volume: `1 × 256 × 256 × 3 = 196,608`
- Element size: `2B` (BFLOAT16)

```
buffer_size_bytes = 196,608 × 2 = 393,216 B
```

These data types use a shared exponent block and **must be used with Tile Layout**.

| **BFP Type** | **Tile Size (32×32)** | **Total Bytes** | **Element Size** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `BFLOAT8_B` | 1024 data + 64 exponent | 1088 B | 17/16 bytes per element |
| `BFLOAT4_B` | 512 data + 64 exponent | 576 B | 9/16 bytes per element |

Page size depends on **tensor layout** and **element size**.

| **Layout** | **Page Shape** | **Page Size Calculation** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Row-Major Layout | `1 × W` (1 stick) | `page_size_bytes = W × element_size` |
| Tile Layout | `32 × 32` | `page_size_bytes = 32 × 32 × element_size` |

For `[1, 256, 256, 3]` (BFLOAT16):

- **Row-Major Layout**:

```
 page_size_bytes = 3 × 2 = 6 B
 ```

- **Tile Layout**:

```
 page_size_bytes = 32 × 32 × 2 = 2048 B
 ```

section_title: Step 3: Calculate Buffer Size Per Bank
content:
Tensors are distributed evenly across cores. Calculations use:

- `ALIGNMENT_BYTES = 16`

1. **Number of Pages**

```
 num_pages = floor(buffer_size_bytes / page_size_bytes)
 ```

2. **Pages Per Bank**

```
 num_equally_distributed_pages = ceil(num_pages / num_cores)
 ```

3. **Bytes Per Bank**

```
 size_bytes_per_bank = num_equally_distributed_pages × round_up(page_size_bytes, ALIGNMENT_BYTES)
 ```

Continue with the last example, assume `num_cores = 8`, we have:

DRAM buffers are allocated from low address to high address (bottom-up) whereas SRAM buffers are allocated from high address to low address (top-down). Data within the allocated region always grows from low to high address.

> !(blue star) In the context of **TT-Metal**, a **Circular Buffer** (CB) is a memory structure designed for efficient *data exchange and synchronization* between **RISCs**, either within the same Tensix core or across different Tensix cores. It is particularly useful in **concurrent** and **streaming workloads**, where **producer and consumer cores** operate at different speeds.

Unlike SRAM/DRAM Buffers, which are primarily used for data storage and transfer, **RISC cores operate directly on CBs**. The only operations that RISC cores can perform with SRAM/DRAM Buffers are **reading from** or **writing to them** - they cannot manipulate Tensor Buffers directly during execution. Circular Buffers, on the other hand, enable **real-time data movement and synchronization**.

SRAM buffers are allocated top down to avoid growing into **Circular Buffer** which are *typically* *not managed by the allocator* and are placed after the reserved region.

!image-20250601-095740.png

The lifetime of CBs depends on which context it is defined and is managed by different classes.

- Typically a CBs lifetime is tied to its corresponding **Program**(local circular buffer)with the **Program** being responsible for managing all CB addresses since its lifetime is within an Op.
- To reduce memory footprint, *a CB can share the same address space as a SRAM buffer* (global circular buffer). In this case the **Allocator** rather than the **Program** manages this CB's address and the lifetime of the CB extends across Ops.

!image-20250602-035500.png
> ![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/plugins/servlet/confluence/placeholder/error?i18nKey=editor.placeholder.error&locale=en_US&version=2 "Cannot invoke \"String.indexOf(int)\" because \"href\" is null") **Note:** before any **Program** is run on device there is a validation step in TT-Metal to ensure that CBs and SRAM buffers do not overlap to avoid memory fragmentation.

- **Local to a core**: These are confined to synchronization between a core's internal RISCs.
- **Volatile**: They are recreated for each program run and do **not preserve state** across program executions.

- **Persistent state across programs**: Unlike traditional buffers, global circular buffers persist across multiple program executions.
- **Used for inter-core communication**: These are designed to support **sender-receiver core mappings**, allowing data transfer and synchronization between different cores.
- **Tracked in L1 memory**: The read/write pointers are stored in L1 and must be manually synchronized to maintain state integrity.
- **API support**: Both Python (host-side) and kernel-side APIs exist to manage and interact with these buffers.

- Part of global circular buffers.
- Enable **sender cores** to transmit data to one or more **receiver cores** using APIs like:

 - `remote_cb_reserve_back()` (sender waits for space)
 - `remote_cb_push_back_and_write_pages()` (sender writes data)
 - `remote_cb_wait_front()` (receiver waits for data)
 - `remote_cb_pop_front()` (receiver signals data consumed)

!image-20250602-040038.png

---

When running inference on a model under **restricted memory constraints**, especially in **SRAM**, several memory-related issues can arise. These challenges can impact both performance and correctness if not handled properly.

- These occur when the system fails to allocate memory for a **Tensor Buffer** within the limited SRAM (L1) space *or even DRAM space if not handle properly*.
- **Typical cause**: The tensor or CB is too large for the remaining available space.

!image-20250602-063636.png

- Some operations require dedicated CBs for streaming or communication.
- Allocation may fail if CB overlaps with an existing tensor buffer.

!image-20250602-064844.png

- Even if the **total L1 memory is sufficient**, memory may be **fragmented** into small chunks scattered across banks.
- Fragmentation prevents large contiguous allocations and increases the chance of allocation failure.
- Causes: Frequent allocation/deallocation without compaction.

!image-20250602-072313.png

Move to shift tensor buffer 0 to the top (move essentially deallocates the input tensor and then re-allocates it). Now, tensor buffer 1 can fit in largest contiguous space

**Caveats**

- Move doesn't always help if there isn't enough space to shift tensor to (e.g. can basically be a ***no-op***)
- Moving from DRAM is bugged with fast dispatch (FD puts binaries in DRAM which could corrupt input tensor)

!image-20250602-084128.png

Dummy pre-allocations reduce move operations

- Pre-allocate a dummy tensor the size of tensor B before running ops
- Deallocate the dummy tensor before allocating tensor B
- No move op needed (better for perf)

!image-20250602-084604.png

- **Inplace OPs: Output tensor is allocated on the same space as input tensor**

 - Skips output tensor allocation and just returns the original input tensor
 - Input/output buffer is modified during OP execution
 - Input tensor data is now same as output tensor after OP is run
 - Good way to save space but might be difficult to make generic ops in-place
 - **Examples:** Softmax, eltwise, etc…
- **Overlapped CBs: Use the same space for different CBs for an OP**

 - Some OPs have different CBs for different purposes during an OP execution
 - But if the CBs aren't used at the same time, you can overlap them (i.e. occupy the same L1)
 - **Example:** Intermediate and output buffers for matmuls

Since bank offset for each tensor is the same, very small tensors can lead to very poor allocation of memory.

!image-20250602-085514.png

Alpamayo-R1 (AR1) is NVIDIA's vision-language-action (**VLA**) model for ADAS, announced at CES '26. This model combines the **Chain of Causation (CoC) reasoning** from LLMs with **trajectory planning** for complex driving scenarios. AR1 achieves superior results across tests: 12% higher planning accuracy in difficult cases, 35% fewer close encounters in closed-loop simulation, 45% better reasoning quality, and 37% improved reasoning–action consistency. It excels in **safety-critical long-tail scenarios** and offers a practical path toward Level-4 autonomous driving.

!image-20260126-071239.png

This document outlines key elements of the inference phase, including model architecture and dataflow, to prepare for deployment on our hardware. It also presents notable concepts such as training strategies and a Gaussian-splatting based simulation, which is a promising idea.

On the side, you might want to (re)visit these documents beforehand:

On the side, you might want to (re)visit these documents beforehand:

- SSR: Quick Glance at SSR
- Autonomous Driving System - A primer.pptx

Autonomous driving systems have shifted from traditional modular architectures to end-to-end (E2E) frameworks, increasingly adopted by industry. Unlike modular designs that separate perception, prediction, and planning with hand-crafted intermediate representations, E2E approaches map raw sensor inputs directly to vehicle motion or unicycle dynamic signals via jointly trained neural networks. This unified approach removes manual interfaces, enabling joint optimization and scalable data-driven policy learning.

!image-20260126-072707.png

Typical E2E models still suffer from data scarcity and blackbox issues, often removed to save computation. The problem worsens with **long-tail** and **safety-critical** cases, where sparse supervision and high-level reasoning pose challenges. Consequently, a significant gap prevents existing E2E models from achieving robust Level-4 autonomy with driving-specific reasoning.

> **Safety-critical long-tail scenarios** are **rare but highly dangerous** driving situations that fall outside the common patterns seen in everyday traffic. They include unusual combinations of events—like a pedestrian suddenly emerging from behind a large truck at night in the rain, or a stalled vehicle partially blocking an unlit highway—where data is scarce and small mistakes can lead to severe accidents.
>
> *"The chances are low, but never zero."*
>
> Because these edge cases are underrepresented in training data yet carry very high risk, they are especially challenging for end‑to‑end driving models and demand more robust perception, planning, and above all, **reasoning**, utilizing prior knowledge about the world to handle cases that belong to a different distribution*.*

Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) offer a promising way to close this reasoning gap. The scaling law shows that increasing compute and data makes models more powerful, or ~*more knowledge about the world*. Beyond built-in knowledge, recent frontier LLMs introduced a new paradigm: **inference-time reasoning** (the `thinking…` lines you see in ChatGPT).

!image-20260126-094218.png

To reach the final answer, these models generate intermediate *reasoning traces*, also called *chains of thought, or tree of thought*, mimicking human problem-solving strategies and offering key advantages:

1. *improved safety* via explicit counterfactual reasoning and runtime safety cross-checks and monitoring;
2. *better interpretability* through human-readable decision rationales;
3. *richer training signals* usable as verifiable rewards to boost long-tail performance.

For example: `perceive → detect pedestrian near crosswalk → predict intent → assess rear safety → plan smooth deceleration → apply brake → stop before crosswalk`.

**Leveraging VLAs, AR1 reframes autonomous driving as a natural language processing (NLP) problem with structured reasoning**, *causally grounded* in driving tasks, using structural knowledge: lane geometry, traffic rules, map priors, agent interactions, and dynamic constraints.

**TL;DR:** The AR1 is a modular VLA architecture, extending with action prediction for autonomous driving. It adopts a *flexible* and *modular* design philosophy.

> The architecture can adopt any off-the-shelf VLM backbone while incorporating domain-specific components for efficient vision encoding and real-time action decoding.

!image-20260126-094551.png

Given past observations up to time

, including multi-camera images and egomotion history, AR1 performs reasoning and predicts the ego vehicle's future trajectory

However, the downstream low-level vehicle controllers typically smooth trajectory outputs to ensure consistent and stable execution on-vehicle, instead of predicting waypoints directly, we use a control-based representation with unicycle dynamics. The trajectory is parameterized by control inputs

.

Cosmos-Reason serves as the **central reasoning engine** of Alpamayo-R1. Conceptually, it plays the same role as a general-purpose VLM/LLM backbone, but is **explicitly optimized for causal, structured reasoning under driving constraints**, rather than generic conversational or QA tasks. In the AR1 architecture, Cosmos-Reason sits between the vision encoder and the trajectory decoder, acting as the module that *interprets*, *reasons*, and *decides* before any concrete action is produced.

!image-20260127-043344.png

A key design choice is that **reasoning is treated as a first-class output**, not a hidden intermediate. The paper explicitly positions Cosmos-Reason as an *interpretable* backbone: the generated reasoning traces are human-readable and can be inspected, audited, or even used for runtime safety checks and cross-validation. This is particularly important for safety-critical long-tail scenarios, where post-hoc explanations are often insufficient.

> One problem with generative models (like LLMs or text-to-image generators) is that they train with an imitative scheme, such as next-token prediction. Although this exploits large-scale unlabeled data, it remains a proxy: **trying to model the expert's internal reward function that underlying exposed actions**. AR1 mitigates this misalignment with post-training techniques using RL.

Although Cosmos-Reason can generate the discrete trajectory tokens τ in an autoregressive manner and frame the AD problem as text generation, *it is actually generated in training phase*. This strategy faces serious constraints in the physical world, including:

- sequential decoding scheme prevents real-time inference

 - each trajectory has 64 waypoints with 2 quantized values per waypoint (*acceleration* and *curvature*), totaling **128** discrete tokens per trajectory, following the default equation in AR1
- susceptibility to sensor noise

Therefore, instead of directly generating 𝜏 autoregressively, a flow-matching expert module samples the final trajectory (default 5 steps).

!image-20260127-044436.png

From an architectural standpoint, Cosmos-Reason is described as:

- A **pretrained reasoning-centric transformer backbone**, trained on large-scale data (*24.7K curated driving-scense video VQA samples + post-trained on 3.7M VQA samples, please visit the paper for details*),
- Further refined with **verifiable reward signals** to improve causal and counterfactual reasoning quality,
- **Backbone-agnostic by design**: the AR1 framework allows replacing Cosmos-Reason with other off-the-shelf VLMs, as long as they can ingest visual tokens and support autoregressive generation.

This flexibility is explicitly called out in the paper and is important for deployment considerations. While Cosmos-Reason is the canonical backbone used in Alpamayo-R1, the surrounding AR1 system (vision encoder + trajectory decoder) does not hard-code assumptions about a specific VLM family, which opens the door to alternative backbones if needed.

In the released model AR1 10B, they use Qwen3-VL as VLM backend:

```
 "vlm_name_or_path": "Qwen/Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct",
```

From an inference perspective, Cosmos-Reason introduces two practical implications that are worth keeping in mind for hardware deployment:

1. **Autoregressive reasoning decode is unavoidable** in the default AR1 formulation. Even if trajectory decoding is efficient, generating reasoning tokens incurs sequential latency.
2. **The number of reasoning tokens is a critical knob**. The paper demonstrates examples with dozens of reasoning tokens per inference cycle; this directly impacts end-to-end latency and dominates runtime on bandwidth-limited accelerators.

In summary, Cosmos-Reason is not merely a perception backbone, but the **decision-making core** of Alpamayo-R1. It reframes autonomous driving as a structured language reasoning problem, enabling stronger generalization in long-tail scenarios—at the cost of increased inference complexity that must be carefully managed during deployment.

The vision encoder converts multi-camera, multi-timestep images into compact visual tokens for the Cosmos-Reason backbone. Its primary design goal is **aggressive token reduction without losing planning-critical semantics**, as vision tokens directly dominate inference latency in VLA settings.

The architecture is **vision-backbone agnostic**: different encoders can be selected based on sensor layout, history length, and hardware constraints. In practice, AR1 defaults to single-image tokenization, while triplane or video-based encoders are preferred for dense multi-camera or long-horizon inputs to maintain real-time performance.

By default, AR1 adopts **single-image patch tokenization** from the underlying VLM. In the released model, they utilize Qwen3 Vision path for encoding multi-view images:

```
 (visual): Qwen3VLVisionModel()
```

Each input image is first encoded into fixed-size patch embeddings, then spatially downsampled to reduce token count before entering the language backbone. This explicitly trades spatial granularity for lower transformer compute and memory cost.

```
I
 => ViT(I) => bilinear_downsample(2×) => flatten_spatial()
[W, H] [W/14, H/14, D] [W/28, H/28, D] [N_tokens, D]
```

For example, `W = 448, H = 280 => N_tokens = 448/28 x 280/28 = 160`. Although this approach is simple and robust, but **token count scales linearly with camera count and resolution**, quickly becoming prohibitive for 360° perception and video frames.

Beyond the strategies above, token counts can be further reduced with post‑training pruning. Methods like SparseVILA dynamically drop redundant tokens during inference without retraining, cutting compute for existing models. This makes it easier to scale AR1 to larger backbones while preserving real-time performance.

| **Category** | **Single-image patch tokenization** | **Multi-camera tokenization** | **Multi-timestep (video) tokenization** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Input scope** | One image per camera, per timestep | Multiple cameras, single timestep | Multiple cameras, multiple timesteps |
| **Core idea** | ViT-style spatial patching per image | Fuse cameras into shared latent (e.g., triplane) before patching | Joint spatiotemporal compression via attention bottleneck |
| **Token scaling** | Linear in cameras × timesteps × resolution | Independent of camera count and resolution (fixed latent) | Independent of cameras and timesteps (fixed query set) |
| **Typical token count** | ~160 tokens / image @ 448×280 | ~288 tokens / timestep total (**~3–4× reduction**) | As low as 8–32 tokens / timestep (up to **20× reduction**) |
| **Temporal redundancy** | Not exploited | Not exploited | Explicitly exploited |
| **Prefill & KV cost** | High; dominates memory bandwidth | Moderate; bounded per timestep | Low; bounded across time |
| **Impact on latency** | Poor scaling for surround + history | Good scaling for multi-camera | Best scaling for long horizons |

| **Impact on latency** | Poor scaling for surround + history | Good scaling for multi-camera | Best scaling for long horizons |
| **Driving quality impact** | Baseline | Near-parity with baseline | Near-parity or slight improvement |
| **Hardware suitability** | Simple, but bandwidth-limited | Better fit for multi-camera AV stacks | Best for bandwidth-limited accelerators |
| **When to use** | Few cameras, short history, simplicity | Many cameras, short history | Many cameras, long history, tight latency budget |

Previously, we formulated AD problem to predict a trajectory of the raw future position (i.e.,

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

) waypoint. However, this representation is vulnerable to sensor noise, and low-level vehicle controllers typically smooth the input trajectory outputs to ensure consistent execution with their kinematic design. Therefore, instead of directly learning

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

in the raw position waypoint space, we apply Euler discretization to utilize unicycle dynamics with control input

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

.

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

During inference, at each timestep ΔT = 0.1s, *acceleration*

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

and *curvature*

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

During inference, at each timestep ΔT = 0.1s, *acceleration*

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

and *curvature*

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

are integrated to recover position, heading, and velocity, ensuring that predicted trajectories are dynamically feasible. This formulation provides a reversible mapping between state-space trajectories and control sequences while reducing high-frequency noise through least-squares fitting with regularization during training.!image-20260128-072037.png

Building on the control-based representation, the trajectory decoder converts high-level reasoning outputs into continuous ego trajectories without autoregressive decoding at inference. Although AR1 is trained with discrete trajectory tokens (64 timesteps × *acceleration*/*curvature*), sequential generation is avoided due to prohibitive latency and poor real-time scaling. Instead, a **dedicated action-expert decoder based on conditional flow matching samples the full control sequence (a,κ) in a small, fixed number of steps (default: 5)**, conditioned on reasoning and meta-actions from the Cosmos-Reason backbone.

!image-20260128-080614.png

```
 (expert): Qwen3VLTextModel(
 (layers): ModuleList(
 (0-35): 36 x Qwen3VLTextDecoderLayer(
 (self_attn): Qwen3VLTextAttention(
 (q_proj): Linear(in_features=2048, out_features=2048, bias=False)
 (k_proj): Linear(in_features=2048, out_features=1024, bias=False)
 (v_proj): Linear(in_features=2048, out_features=1024, bias=False)
 (o_proj): Linear(in_features=2048, out_features=2048, bias=False)
 (q_norm): Qwen3VLTextRMSNorm((128,), eps=1e-06)
 (k_norm): Qwen3VLTextRMSNorm((128,), eps=1e-06)
 )
 (mlp): Qwen3VLTextMLP(
 (gate_proj): Linear(in_features=2048, out_features=8256, bias=False)
 (up_proj): Linear(in_features=2048, out_features=8256, bias=False)
 (down_proj): Linear(in_features=8256, out_features=2048, bias=False)
 (act_fn): SiLUActivation()
 )
 (input_layernorm): Qwen3VLTextRMSNorm((2048,), eps=1e-06)
 (post_attention_layernorm): Qwen3VLTextRMSNorm((2048,), eps=1e-06)
 )
 )
 (norm): Qwen3VLTextRMSNorm((2048,), eps=1e-06)
 (rotary_emb): Qwen3VLTextRotaryEmbedding()
 )
```
This section aims to give a rough estimation of the AR1's performance on A0, positing that A0 has enough capacity to load the model via quantize, pruning, etc..

**Paper:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.06762

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
Deep neural networks (DNNs) consist of many tensor operators (e.g., `conv2d`, `matmul`). To run efficiently on different hardware (CPU, GPU, accelerators), these operators must be implemented as **high-performance tensor programs**.

Traditionally this is done using:

1. **Vendor libraries**

 - Example: cuDNN, MKL-DNN
 - Require heavy manual engineering for each operator and hardware.
2. **Template-based auto-tuning**

 - Example: TVM
 - Uses manually designed optimization templates.

These approaches suffer from:

- Limited search space
- Heavy engineering effort
- Poor support for new operators or hardware

Ansor proposes a **fully automatic system to generate optimized tensor programs** without manual templates.

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
The most important insight of the paper:

> Instead of searching a small parameterized template space, generate a **very large program space automatically** and **efficiently explore** it using **learning** and **evolutionary search**.

Ansor automatically searches for optimized implementations of tensor operators by exploring a **large optimization space**. It improves previous systems by:

1. Automatically constructing a **large hierarchical** **search space**
2. Sampling **complete programs** instead of incomplete ones
3. Using **evolutionary search + learned cost model**
4. Prioritizing important **subgraphs** in a DNN

The system architecture has **three major components**:

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
```
Deep Learning Model
 ↓
Graph Partitioning
 ↓
+----------------------+
| 1. Task Scheduler |
| 2. Program Sampler |
| 3. Performance Tuner |
+----------------------+
 ↓
Optimized Tensor Programs
```

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
**Key contributions:**

1. Hierarchical search space: sketches + annotations
2. Automatic tensor program generation
3. Evolutionary search with learned cost model
4. Task scheduler for multi-operator tuning
5. Hardware-agnostic optimization framework

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
Ansor introduces a **hierarchical search space**.

Two levels:

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
Defines program structure such as:

- loop tiling
- fusion
- caching
- reduction factorization

Example transformation:

```
Original loop:
for i
 for j
 for k

Tiled loop:
for i0
 for j0
 for i1
 for j1
 for k0
 ...
```

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
These structures are generated using **derivation rules** applied to the computation DAG.

**Example rules:**

| **Rule** | **Description** |
| --- | --- |
| Inline | inline simple ops (ReLU, add) |
| Multi-level tiling | create tile hierarchy |
| Fusion | fuse consumer ops |
| Cache stage | introduce cache buffers |
| Reduction factorization | increase parallelism |

Typical subgraph produces **<10 sketches**.

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
After selecting a sketch, Ansor randomly fills in:

- tile sizes
- vectorization
- loop unrolling
- parallelization
- computation placement

This produces **complete programs**.

**Key idea:**

Instead of pruning partial programs early, Ansor samples **complete implementations**, avoiding prediction errors.

![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Complete and Incomplete programs

Suppose we implement **matrix multiplication**:

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

**Naive version**

```
for i in range(512):
 for j in range(512):
 for k in range(512):
 C[i, j] += A[i, k] * B[k, j]
```

**Incomplete programs (Sketch)**

Now the compiler starts applying optimizations like **tiling**, but **tile sizes are not decided yet**.

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
The paper shows that **cost models predict poorly on incomplete programs**.

Reasons:

- performance depends on **all scheduling decisions**
- partial programs hide important memory behavior

Therefore Ansor:

1. generates **complete programs**
2. predicts their cost
3. measures the best ones

!image-20260308-113440.png

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
Randomly generated programs may not be optimal. There for this step will iteratively fine-tune the graph to retrieve the better optimized versions. High-level flow:

1. Use **evolutionary search** to find a small batch of **promissing programs** according to **cost model**.
2. Measure these programs on hardware to get the **actual execution cost**.
3. The actual execution cost is then used to **re-train the cost model** to make it more accurate.

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
Inspired by genetic algorithms. Basic idea is:

> By **iteratively** mutating **high quality** programs, we can generate new programs with potentially **higher quality**.

1. Start from the initial generated programs, **sample some programs** from **current generation** according to **certain probabilities**. The probability to sample a program is proportional to a fitness predicted by a **learned cost model**.
2. For the selected programs, we randomly apply one of the **evolution operations** to generate a new program.
3. This repeated for **several rounds** and outputs a small **set of programs** with **highest scores**.
4. These programs is used to measure the **real cost** on the hardware.
5. The collected measurement data is used to **update the cost model**.

![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Evolution operations used in the paper

Evolution operations include:

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Evolution operations used in the paper

Evolution operations include:

1. **Tile mutation** Change tile sizes while keeping loop length valid.
2. **Parallel mutation** Adjust loop parallelization.
3. **Pragma mutation** Modify compiler pragmas like unroll factors.
4. **Computation location mutation** Move where an operation executes.
5. **Crossover** Combine transformations from two programs.

These operations iteratively improve candidate programs.

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
To avoid compiling thousands of programs, Ansor predicts program performance using a machine learning **cost model**.

> For a full program, Ansor makes predictions for **each innermost non-loop statement** and **add the predictions up as the score.**

**Features** for cost model can base on:

- arithmetic operations
- memory access patterns
- loop structure

Specifically, the loss function of the model `f`on a program `P`with throughput `y`is

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

Where `S(P)` is the set of **innermost non-loop statements** in `P`. We directly use the throughput `y`as weight. We **gradient boosting decision tree** as the underlying model `f`.

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
A full DNN contains many **operator subgraphs**.

- Not all subgraphs impact performance equally.
- Not all subgraphs are major bottlenecks.

Ansor introduces a **task scheduler** that decides **"Which subgraph should be tuned next?"**

The scheduler:

- allocates tuning time dynamically
- prioritizes performance bottlenecks
- uses a **gradient-descent based scheduling algorithm**

> For **larger models** leads to **more subgraphs a**s well as more opportunities to reduce the total tuning time, because we can s**hare and reuse knowledge between subgraphs**. A subgraph can also **appear multiple times** in a DNN or across different DNNs.

To minimize the end-to-end latency of a single DNN, we can define:

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

where:

- `g{i}` = latency of subgraph `i`
- `w{i}` = frequency in the network

This focuses tuning on important operators.

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
Compared to previous approaches:

| **Feature** | **TVM Templates** | **Halide Autoscheduler** | **Ansor** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Manual templates** | Required | No | No |
| **Search space** | Limited | Moderate | Large |
| **Works with new ops** | Difficult | Moderate | Easy |
| **Program evaluation** | Partial programs | Partial programs | Complete programs |
| **Exploration strategy** | Parameter tuning | Sequential | Evolutionary |

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
Ansor improves performance compared to state-of-the-art systems:

| Hardware | Speedup |
| --- | --- |
| Intel CPU | up to **3.8×** |
| ARM CPU | up to **2.6×** |
| NVIDIA GPU | up to **1.7×** |

It can also find optimized programs **outside the search space of previous systems**.

---

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
- Ansor cannot optimize graphs with **dynamic shapes**.
- Ansor only supports **dense operators**. To support **sparse operators** (*e.g.*, SpMM) that are commonly used in sparse neural networks and graph neural networks, we need to **redesign the search space**.
- Ansor only performs program optimizations at a **high level** but relies on other **code generators** (*e.g.*, LLVM and NVCC) to do **platform-dependent optimizations** (*e.g.*, instruction selection).

metadata.title: Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Ansor_ High-Performance Tensor Programs for DNNs_336364539.md

content:
- [Model Development Structure](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-ModelDevelopmentStructure)
- [Development Checklists](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-DevelopmentChecklists)
 - [1. Model Analysis](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-1.ModelAnalysis)
 - [2. Reference - reference/](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-2.Reference-reference/)
 - [3. TTNN - tt/](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-3.TTNN-tt/)
 - [Parameter Preprocessing](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-ParameterPreprocessing)
 - [TTNN Implementation](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-TTNNImplementation)
 - [4. Unit Tests - tests/](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-4.UnitTests-tests/)
 - [5. Model Demo Checklist](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-5.ModelDemoChecklist)
 - [6. README Checklist](#Appx.A:ModelDevelopmentChecklist-6.READMEChecklist)

**Model Development**

The structure of a model can be devided into:

- `README.md` every model must have this file to illustrate:

 - Environment setup,
 - Dataset preparation,
 - Guide of model demo, functional/performance tests,
 - Guide of model performance reports (Tracy Profile Report, L1 Visualization Report, etc.).
- `reference` directory contains the source code of model using referenced library, in most case it is `pytorch` implementation.
- `tt` directory has similar modular structure of `reference` directory, contain `ttnn` implementation of the model.
- `tests` folder contains unit tests of model's components or end-to-end performance/validation.
- `requirements.txt` (if required)
- `<model_name>_runner.py`: model runner
- `run.py`: script for model demo
- `<optional folders>` other folders if neccessary.

See https://github.com/bos-semi/tt-metal/tree/develop/models/bos_model for more information

```
tt-metal/models/bos_model/<model_name>
 |- README.md
 |- reference
 |- tt
 |- tests
 |- <model_name>_runner.py
 |- run.py
 |- requirements.txt
 `- <optional_folders>
```

---

**I. Model Analysis**

- Model overview document, includes (*recommended*)

- Background
- Model Overall Architecture
- Module block description
- Model size
- Computational cost (FLOPs)
- Performance benchmark data: FPS/TTFT/TPS/etc.

- Dataset description document
- Dataset prepration (should load to NAS server for sharing among teams)
- Development plan (development timeline, constraints estimation, etc.)

**II. Pytorch Implementation**

- Pytorch implementation under `models/bos_model/<model>/reference`
- Unit test for Pytorch implementation under `models/bos_model/<model>/tests`
- Additional dependencies list in `models/bos_model/<model>/requirements.txt`, *if any*
- List of unsupported device operations (add to development plan), *if any*

**III. Torch to TTNN Conversion**

- **Weight conversion impl.** (script/function) under `models/bos_model/<model_name>/tt/`. It should:

- Preprocess pytorch weights (fuse weights/bias, tensor transform, capture shapes, etc.)
- Converting `torch` tensor into `ttnn` tensor format.

- **Standalone preprocessed model weight file** (pytorch)`.pt` contains

- Fused parameters (e.g., convolution weights with batch norm fused in)
- Input/output shapes operation-wises (e.g., required for `ttnn.conv2d`, `ttnn.max_pool2d`, etc.)
- Other necessary (meta) data (*optional, model variant*), *if needed*
- Share preprocessed **model weight** to common workspace (recommend load to NAS server to share accross team)

**IV. TTNN Implementation**

- TTNN implementation under `models/bos_model/<model_name>/tt`, should to contains:

- Separated model configuration file.
- Separated module implementation.

**V. Functional Validation**

- Unit tests under `models/bos_model/<model_name>/tests`, should contains:

- Separated unit test for each module (random generated inputs)
- End-to-end unit test for the model (random generated inputs)
- Test script that exports Tracy Profile data.
- Test script that exports L1 visualizer data.

- Model Runner `models/bos_model/<model_name>/<model_name>_runner.py` (for demo and unified CI/CD tests). Read: Model Runner Requirements

- Run on real dataset.
- With trace enabled.
- With double command queue enable.

- A demo script under `models/bos_model/<model_name>/` incorrporate the model runner above.

`README.md` of each model should have those sections:

- Environment setup (if any)
- Dataset and model parameters prepration guide
- Model demo guide.
- Model unit tests guide.
- Performance report guide (to get Tracy Profile/ L1 Visualizer data).

Add **Appendix** sections if needed.

content: Usually each engineer is working in the docker environment, thus the docker container command should mount the `/data` folder to `/data` directory in the docker environment. Example script to create docker environment on Wormhole server is illustrated as below

content:

DOCKER_NAME="docker_name"
PORT_NUMBER=<PORT-NUMBER>

HOME_FULL_PATH="full/path/to/working/directory"

*If bos host container is already setup and available (with* `/tt-metal` *at root), step 0 can be skipped.*

0. Build tt-metal from source

```
# !!! IMPORTANT: Due to bos_demo's requirement, we need to work at /
cd /
git clone git@github.com:bos-semi/tt-metal.git --recurse-modules
cd tt-metal/

# Build tt-metal
sudo ./install_dependencies.sh
source create_venv.sh
source env_set.sh
./build_metal.sh -b Release

# Hugepage setting for the first set up
cd tt_metal/third_party/umd
sudo -E env PATH=$PATH python3 ./scripts/setup_hugepages.py enable

# !!! restart computer !!!
cd tt_metal/third_party/umd
sudo -E env PATH=$PATH python3 ./scripts/setup_hugepages.py enable
```

1. Clone bos\_demo inside the tt-metal

```
 git clone git@github.com:bos-semi/bos_demo.git
 ```

2. Install necessary packages. For convenience, all setup is done with:

```
 cd bos_demo/
 ./install_demo.sh
 ```

3. Download the following models from huggingface

 1. Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct (Public model)
 2. Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct (Gated model)

```
cd $TT_METAL_HOME/
./run_demo_server.sh
```

Wait until all warm-up is done. It might take some time (around 1~2 minutes). Wait until you can see "Additional warmup completed successfully" message as below:

```
...

WARNING: This is a development server. Do not use it in a production deployment. Use a production WSGI server instead.
 - Running on all addresses (0.0.0.0)

 - Running on http://172.17.0.2:8080
Press CTRL+C to quit

[YOLO] Service worker ready for commands
[YOLO] Received command: warmup

 Warmup iteration 1/5...

 Warmup iteration 3/5...
 Warmup iteration 4/5...
 Warmup iteration 5/5...
🔥 Additional warmup completed successfully
```

*Audio models (Whisper, Vosk, and Glow-TTS) are automatically downloaded at the first time the demo is run*.

The homepage is a **multimodal interaction workspace** for Qwen 2.5 VL.

!image-20251217-055947.png

| | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **UI Section** | **Component** | **Description** |
| **Left Panel – Image Input** | Image display | Shows the currently selected image used as visual context for the model. |
| Sample images | Users can select from preset example images. |
| Upload image | Allows users to upload their own image for analysis. |
| **Top Bar – Model & Performance** | Model selector | Dropdown to choose the active model, currently support 2 variants of Qwen VL only: - Qwen**2.5**-VL-7B-Instruct - Qwen**3**-VL-8B-Instruct |
| TPS | Displays tokens per second during inference. |
| TTFT | Displays time to first token, indicating initial response latency. |
| TIT | Displays token inference time, reflecting per-token generation speed. |
| **Right Panel – Working Space** | Response area | Main workspace where Qwen's responses are streamed and displayed. |
| Streaming output | Text appears progressively as the model generates tokens. |
| **Bottom Input Area – User Interaction** | Prompt box | Text input field for asking questions about the image. |
| Microphone button | Enables speech input (STT) for voice-based interaction. |

| **Bottom Input Area – User Interaction** | Prompt box | Text input field for asking questions about the image. |
| Microphone button | Enables speech input (STT) for voice-based interaction. |
| Ask button | Submits the prompt to the model for processing. |

!demo-20251217-054611.png

This section describes the end-to-end **voice input and voice output** interaction used in the Qwen 2.5 VL demo.

!image-20251217-061141.png

| 1. **Voice Input via Microphone (Speech-to-Text)** | **2. Qwen 2.5 VL Response Generation** | **3. Text-to-Speech (TTS) Playback per Assistant Bubble** |
| --- | --- | --- |

| 1. **User clicks the microphone button** - Located next to the prompt input box at the bottom of the UI. - This initiates audio capture from the user's microphone. 2. **Audio capture and STT processing** - The browser records the user's speech. - Audio is sent to a **Speech-to-Text (STT) engine** running on the PC: - Supported engines: **Whisper** or **Vosk**. - The STT engine converts spoken audio into English text. - Whisper typically finalizes transcription after speech ends. - Vosk may provide low-latency partial results internally. 3. **Transcript becomes the prompt** - Once STT finalizes, the transcribed text is placed into the prompt. - The interaction now proceeds exactly like a typed query. 4. **Prompt submission** - The user clicks **Ask** (or auto-submit is triggered). - The text prompt, along with the selected image context, is sent to Qwen. | 1. **Model inference** - The backend runs **Qwen 2.5 VL on TT-Metal**. - Tokens are generated progressively and streamed to the UI. 2. **Streaming output** - The assistant response appears incrementally in the chat bubble. - Performance metrics (TPS, TTFT, TIT) update in real time. 3. **Completion** - When token generation

UI. 2. **Streaming output** - The assistant response appears incrementally in the chat bubble. - Performance metrics (TPS, TTFT, TIT) update in real time. 3. **Completion** - When token generation finishes, the assistant bubble is marked as **complete**. - Only after this point is TTS playback enabled. | 1. **Speaker button availability** - Each assistant response bubble contains a **speaker button** at its lower-right corner. - The button is enabled **only after the full response has been generated**. - Clicking during streaming has no effect. 2. **User clicks the speaker button** - The UI reads the bubble's internal `message-id`. - This ID is mapped to the corresponding synthesized audio file (e.g., `qwen-msg-<id>.wav`). 3. **Audio playback behavior** - Audio is played through a shared playback controller: - Any currently playing audio is stopped first. - The selected response audio is then played. - The speaker icon visually indicates the **active / playing state**. 4. **Replay** - Clicking the same speaker button again replays the same audio. - Clicking a different bubble switches playback to that response. |

| -> | -> | -> |

Following those steps to set up Docker Container:

0. Load docker image from the file

```
docker load -i bos_demo_0.1.tar
```

1. Check if image is loaded successfully.

```
$ docker image ls
REPOSITORY TAG IMAGE ID CREATED SIZE
bos_demo 0.1 a9ff6a637438 56 minutes ago 62.6GB
```
2. Start docker container Forward 8080 port to access web UI from external. (Assuming that all other configurations such as Hugepage setting and KMD installation are already done.)

```
docker run -it --device=/dev/bos/0:/dev/bos/0 --cap-add=ALL \
 --ipc=host --security-opt seccomp=unconfined \
 --security-opt apparmor=unconfined \
 -v /dev/mem:/dev/mem \
 -v /sys/bus/pci/devices/:/sys/bus/pci/devices/ \
 -v /dev/hugepages-1G:/dev/hugepages-1G \
 -p 8080:8080 \
 --name bos_demo bos_demo:0.1
```

3. Execute Docker Container

```
docker exec -it bos_demo bash
```

Docker Container already has `tt-metal` installed in `/tt-metal`

content:
**Jira:**

AISW-532 - Getting issue details... STATUS

The objective of this test is to validate that **automatic microphone endpointing** (silence-based stop detection) is:

- **Robust in noisy booth environments** similar to CES
- **Reliable and low-friction** for users
- Able to **minimize manual intervention** while preserving a manual mic stop as fallback

This validation supports enabling auto-endpointing as the default behavior for the CES demo.

**STT engine accuracy itself is not the primary test objective.**

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
| **Dimension** | **Values** |
| --- | --- |
| Noise condition (SNR) | +15 dB (quiet), +5 dB (busy expo), 0 dB (loud booth) |
| Endpointing mode | Off / 300 ms / 500 ms / 700 ms |
| Prompts | 8 |
| Trials per condition | 1 |

> **Note:** Noise is injected by *offline mixing* of clean prompt recordings with a fixed booth-noise audio file at controlled SNR levels. Absolute dBA is not used for offline testing.

**Total records:**

```
# participants * #prompts * #noise_levels = 288 records
```

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
---

**Hypotheses:**

- **500–700 ms** thresholds provide better robustness at **low SNR (0–5 dB)**
- **300 ms** may feel faster but risks premature cutoff
- With tuned VAD, **≥90% auto-stop success at +5 dB SNR** is achievable

---

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
| **Prompt ID** | **Description** | **Spoken Prompt** | **Key Phrases (for accuracy check)** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| P1 | Clean command | Describe the exterior of the car | `describe`, `exterior`, `car` |
| P2 | Overlapping / hesitation | Explain the – uh – the thing in this image | `explain`, `thing`, `image` |
| P3 | Numbers | Using 123 words, describe the image | `123 words`, `describe`, `image` |
| P4 | Proper nouns | Introduce a white BMW i8 | `introduce`, `white`, `BMW i8` |
| P5 | Mixed + pauses | Should I eat… ice creams in the winter? | `should I eat`, `ice creams`, `winter` |
| P6 | Trailing filler | What's the battery range on this vehicle, uh, yeah. | `battery range`, `vehicle` |
| P7 | Short utterance | Good morning. | `good morning` |
| P8 | Rapid speech | Good morning, here's [your name], who are you? | `good morning`, `[your name]`, `who are you` |

./dataset/

| | |-- P1.wav
| | |-- P2.wav
| | |-- ...wav
| | `-- P8.wav
| |-- snr_p05 # most aggressive environment
| | |-- P1.wav
| | |-- P2.wav
| | |-- ...wav
| | `-- P8.wav
| |-- snr_p15 # quiet environment
| | |-- P1.wav
| | |-- P2.wav

`-- noise.mp3 # talking background noise

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
- **Clean prompt recordings:**

 - Recorded once per participant in a quiet environment
 - Participant stops recording immediately after finishing the prompt
- **Noise source:**

 - Single booth-noise audio file (`noise.mp3`) containing crowd chatter and ambient expo noise

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
- All audio is:

 - Mono
 - 16 kHz sample rate
- Each prompt recording is padded with:

 - **Pre-silence:** 300 ms
 - **Post-silence:** 1200 ms
(to stress VAD start/end detection)

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
- Clean prompts are mixed with the noise file at fixed **SNR levels**:

 - **+15 dB SNR** → quiet / mild background
 - **+5 dB SNR** → typical expo floor
 - **0 dB SNR** → loud booth / worst case
- Noise segment offset is randomized per prompt to simulate different booth moments.

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
1. Gather participants' clean prompt recordings:

 - Speak the prompt naturally.
 - Stop immediately after finishing the prompt.
 - Label audio as `P<id>.wav`.
2. **Preprocess data** (noise, remove pre-speech silent, post-speech silent, etc.)
3. **Synthesize noisy test audio via SNR-based mixing:**

 - Mix each clean prompt with booth noise at:

 - +15 dB / +5 dB / 0 dB SNR
 - Add silence padding before and after the prompt.
4. Run all prompts under each:

 - **SNR level**
 - **Endpointing threshold:** 300 / 500 / 700 ms (and Off)
5. Trial order randomized to reduce learning bias.
6. After each condition, log trial results (see Section 6).

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
**Meta-data:**

- `snr_db` (15 / 5 / 0)
- `endpointing_slience_ms` (300 / 500 / 700 ms)
- `prompt_id` (P1 → P8)
- `transcript_text`
- `audio_duration_ms` (length of clean prompt)

**Output log:**

- `auto_stop_triggered` (true/false)
- `stop_latency_ms`

content:

| --- | --- |
| Auto-stop success @ +5 dB SNR | ≥ 90% |
| Average stop latency | ≤ 700 ms |
| Manual stop usage | ≤ 10% |
| Trust rating | ≥ 4 / 5 |

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
- [vad\_results.jsonl](/wiki/spaces/bossemi/pages/256639602/Auto-Endpointing+Validation+for+Speech+Input+CES+Demo?preview=%2F256639602%2F257032902%2Fvad_results.jsonl) [vad\_summary.json](/wiki/spaces/bossemi/pages/256639602/Auto-Endpointing+Validation+for+Speech+Input+CES+Demo?preview=%2F256639602%2F257000272%2Fvad_summary.json) [vad\_results.csv](/wiki/spaces/bossemi/pages/256639602/Auto-Endpointing+Validation+for+Speech+Input+CES+Demo?preview=%2F256639602%2F256934542%2Fvad_results.csv)

> **Note:** `auto_stop_success_rate` alone is misleading because it includes premature stops.
> The true success rate is computed as:
> **Effective Success Rate = auto\_stop\_success\_rate – premature\_stop\_rate**

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

section_title: **Effective Auto-Stop Success Rate by Noise Level**

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)_256639602.md

content:
| **Noise Level** | **300 ms** | **500 ms** | **700 ms** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Avg** | 320ms | 512ms | 704ms |
| **P95** | 320ms | 512ms | 704ms |

Mean and P95 stop latency closely match configured thresholds. It's worth noting that 320 / 512 / 704 ms are expected, since we sliced the audio into 32ms frames for *seamless* experience, hence numbers 300 / 500 / 700 ms are padded (by 32) to the respective numbers.

This indicates **stable endpointing timing once silence is correctly detected**.

metadata.title: Auto-Endpointing Validation for Speech Input (CES Demo)

content:
**Use 700 ms as the default endpointing threshold for CES demos.**

- Minimizes premature stops across all tested noise conditions
- Provides predictable latency
- Significantly improves user trust
- Manual mic stop remains essential as fallback

Do **not** ship 300 ms as a selectable option.

Keep 500 ms as experimental / non-default if needed.
