content: This document describe detail about the ttnn.flip operation implementation that mimic the torch.flip operation.

Reference: torch.flip — PyTorch 2.10 documentation https://www.codegenes.net/blog/pytorch-flip/

Reverse the order of an n-D tensor along given axis in dims.

> torch.flip makes a copy of `input`'s data. This is different from NumPy's np.flip, which returns a view in constant time. Since copying a tensor's data is more work than viewing that data, torch.flip is expected to be slower than np.flip.

> Parameters:
> **input** (*Tensor* – the input tensor.
> **dims** (*a list* *or* *tuple* – axis to flip on

!image-20260310-024219.png

Note that this operation **return a copy**, so there must be new memory allocate, and the value of this memory space is just a way of arrange the data of the original tensor

Comment:

Note that this operation **return a copy**, so there must be new memory allocate, and the value of this memory space is just a way of arrange the data of the original tensor

Comment:

- If flip at all dim, it return to reverse the flatten data
- It is useful in image processing when inverting up/down/left and right or reverse the chronological order of sequence data.

In the latest implementation, I implement the method to perform **flip on any dimensions** that support up to **5D tensor** in **Row Major** and **Interleaved** layout. The implementation is expected to replaced the walk around method from mentor Quy leveraging the bos\_getitem operation implemented before. To sum up, the target for ttnn.flip is to not only work correctly by torch.allclose() verification with the torch version but also achieve a faster performance compared to the walk-around method and add-in an inplace data movement option, allow for more flexible work in L1 space.

Walk-around method:

!image-20260310-024120.png
> In the later part of this document, the experiment results is display that my method **time performance** is **better in every test cases** and **able** to perform flip with **large size tensor** while the walk-around method reach its **L1 memory limitation**.

Row major layout is **friendly** with the flip operation:

Observation:

- After flip, a tensor size is preserve. Moreover, a (1d array) page data is still independent
- **If and only if** we flip the last channel the value inside a page is modified (reverse)
- The page index before and after flip is computable (flip corresponding dimension index) --> We know where a page should be place after the flip operation

 - Example with tensor shape (2,3,4,5) --> Page index from (0,0,0) to (2,3,4)
If we flip at dim 0 and 2 , page index (1,2,1) would be (2 - 1, 2 , 4-1) = (1,1,3)

Due to these observe, for the row major case, I propose the strategy of:

- Each core will handle a part of the tensor input (a few consecutive page?)
- Read kernel reverse data inside a page if need (flip on last/ second last channel)
- Read kernel send page to SRC\_CB
- Write kernel read a page from DST\_CB
- Write kernel compute the destination of the page after flip and write the page data to the corresponding page ID

!image-20260310-024353.png
> The key implementation here is the function to get the page ID after flip, given the tensor shape and flip dimension.

To reduce the computation in the read/write kernel, I precompute some value size, stride,.. from the tensor shape and flip dimensions then pass in the kernel as compile time arguments. The kernel compute the output page id base on these parameters.

Note that for the case that we flip the last dimension, data inside a page is reverse We should not implement a for loop to reverse the data internal a page, this would cause a bunch of time in the kernel to due with (even with parallel between multiple core). In this case, follow by multiple other data movement operations, **I perform an transpose (permute) with another dim so the last dim is never flip.**

[#Todo](#) What if all dim is flip...?

Example of flip operation on a tensor with shape (2,3,4) and dim = [1]. Then we can logically view the tensor as 6 pages, each has 4 elements.

!image-20260310-024446.png

We can see the flip function mapping graph is decompose to multiple 2-cycle and ring. This is due to the characteristic of `flip(flip(x)) = x` for every page id. This is important for later implementation in the flip inplace method.

Although the original torch.flip operation emphasize the function to create a new tensor and not shared the memory with the input tensor. In TTNN, we reasonably want to support inplace for operation due to the limitation of L1 memory. Also, the flip operation is feasible to inplace modification naturally and in the last dimension flip, inplace help on not to expand the memory where we need to do walk around by a pair of traspose (permute) which have allocate new tensor memory buffer.

The most challenge in Inplace version from previous version is that when we write a page to another page\_id, we must move the original output\_id page to somewhere else to avoid losing data. Fortunately, as we discuss in previous part, the flip mapping graph can be decompose to only rings and 2-cycle, so it support on **swap** operation!

Follow this motivation, in the inplace version, **I design the read and write kernel to perform swap page instead of load/write naively.**

> These experiments are performed in the Wormhole Device A5.
> The device time is record by tracy.

!image-20260310-024548.png

My implementation has pass over the torch.allclose verification with the golden function torch.flip().

For the experiment, I compare with the walk around method to express the efficiency of my implementation. The time record focus on the device time (I do not measure the time of processing on the host). The walkaround method have a tensor init (**idx\_flip tensor**) that does not record in the device time, I **do not include this time** in the comparisons below.

I design the test case tensor shape as:

```
test_shapes = [
 (16, 128, 256),
 (16, 256, 256),
 (16, 256, 512),
 (32, 256, 512),
 (1, 1024, 4100),
 (1, 4100, 1024),
 (32, 32, 32),
 (64, 64, 64),
 (128, 128, 128),
 ]
```

These test case support for size increasing and some dim-balance to have a fair test among the different dim flip.

!image-20260310-024646.png

For the last dimension case, time efficient is accelerate from **2x to about 1.4x**.

> For the specific case (1,1024,4100) that mentor Quy want to apply the new operation, it is faster than **1.75x**

Note that in this case, both walk around method and my implementation need a pair of transpose operation, ignore of this same work among 2 methods, the device time of FlipOperation is about **a half** of the BosGetItemOperation.

!image-20260310-024706.png

For the second last dimension case, it is notice that this is the optimal case of the walk around method where it no need to perform the pre and post transpose operations. However, the device time in my method is still better where the **worst case** is **just the same** with the walk around.

> For the specific case (1,4100,1024) that mentor Quy want to apply the new operation, it is faster than **1.18x**

!image-20260310-024727.png
> For this case, my method show an outperform result compare to walk around method with **from** **3x to 4.2x**

This support the idea of generality of ttnn.flip implementation which can support multiple dimensions flip at same time complexity (No flip on last dimension)

This is the describe of the overall efficiency

!image-20260310-024807.png

When tensor is on L1, we see a similar pattern of the efficiency graph:

!image-20260310-024816.png

Also, in this case, I highlight the **inplace advantage** when processing on large tensor. The walkaround method is unable to run with the tensor size (128, 256, 256) (dtype is int32) while my method is sustainable!

```
Method 1 FAILED: TT_FATAL @ /workspace/tt-metal/tt_metal/impl/allocator/bank_manager.cpp:431: address.has_value()

Out of Memory: Not enough space to allocate 33554432 B L1 buffer across 64 banks, where each bank needs to store 524288 B, but bank size is only 1396928 B

Method 2 (ttnn.flip) total: 1.049650 s | per-iter: 1049649.67 us
 Speedup (M1/M2): N/A
```

Multi-Head Attention is an important part of all Transformer-based models. In current implementation, after attention heads are calculated. We need to gather information from many different attention. Attention output is produced in **[B, H, S, D]** shape, heads are concatenated to **[B, S, H \* D]** and then perform a matmul with weight in **[H \* D, O]** shape.

!image-20260310-022501.png

We used to use separate ops for head-concatenation step and FFN (output projection / feed-forward input) (see Multi-Head Attention — TT-NN documentation. By calling many operations, we are adding data movement overhead with 3 operations are called by default:

- permute.
- reshape.
- matmul.

Additionally, permute and reshape are data movement operation and can become a significant performance bottleneck on large sequence lengths and multi-head configurations.

- **input\_tensor\_a:** TTNN input tensor containing the activations produced. Typically has shape `[batch, num_heads, seq_len, head_dim]`. This tensor serves as the **A operand** in the linear (matrix multiplication) operation.

 - Constrants:

- seq\_len, head\_dim: is `divisible` by 32
- **input\_tensor\_w:** TTNN weight tensor of the **linear projection**. Contains the projection weights applied to the input activations. Common shapes include `[d_model, d_model]` for attention output projection or `[d_model, d_ff]` for the first FFN layer.
- **bias:** (Optional) TTNN tensor containing the bias added after the matrix multiplication. When provided, the operation computes:`output = input_tensor_a @ input_tensor_w + bias`. Typically has shape `[d_out]`. – currently not supported.
- **optional\_output\_tensor:** (Optional) Pre-allocated tensor to store the output. If not provided, a new output tensor will be allocated.
- **memory\_config:** (Optional) Configuration specifying how the output tensor is allocated and stored in memory (e.g., DRAM, sharded layout) for performance optimization.
- **mode:** Flag indicating the execution mode of the operation, default using mcast data reuse.
- **mcast\_in\_a:** Indicates whether `input_tensor_a` should be multicast across multiple compute cores during execution to improve performance. If is false

- **mcast\_in\_a:** Indicates whether `input_tensor_a` should be multicast across multiple compute cores during execution to improve performance. If is false
- **output\_dtype:** (Optional) Data type of the output tensor. If not specified, the output dtype is inferred from the inputs or the kernel configuration.

In order to understand to computation flow of the operation, we will dive deep into factory code as well as kernel codes.

- Factory: Currently, ops only support tile input layout but got 1 for applied mcast.

 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/concat_linear_multicore_program_factory.cpp`
 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/concat_linear_multicore_mcast_program_factory.cpp`
- Reader:

 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/kernels/dataflow/multicore_reader.cpp`
 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/kernels/dataflow/multicore_writer.cpp`
 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/kernels/dataflow/reader_bmm_tile_layout_a_receiver.cpp`
 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/kernels/dataflow/reader_bmm_tile_layout_a_sender_padding.cpp`
 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/kernels/dataflow/reader_bmm_tile_layout_w_sender_writer_padding.cpp`
- Writer:

- `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/kernels/dataflow/multicore_writer.cpp`
 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/kernels/dataflow/reader_bmm_tile_layout_w_sender_writer_padding.cpp`
- Compute:

 - `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/concat_linear/device/kernels/compute/bmm_large_block_zm_fused_bias_activation.cpp`

Original approach (permute → reshape → matmul).

!image-20260312-035742.png

First, we will get the data movement insight in cores by doing a simple fused operation version:

- To compute a single tile, heads row instead of one.

 - We have to adjust how tiles are read in read kernel.
 - An output tile [i, j] requires:

 - weight column j
 - for each heads, we need input row i.

 - after one row is read: input tensor tile id at end of the row.
 - moving to next head row's end: + Mt (seq) \* Dt (head\_dims)
 - moving to the row's start: - Dt + 1.

!image-20260311-091657.png

!image-20260311-091657.png

Furthermore, one input tensor row could be called more than one, we could think about reuse technique here.

One weight column is called seq times, adding reading overhead. We could think about mcast technique here.

These led to Mcast1D reuse implementation:

- In this approach, core grid is flattened into 1D core set.

!image-20260312-022412.png

- Output tensor is divided into blocks with [per\_core\_M, per\_core\_N] shape; each block is handled by one core.

!image-20260312-034432.png

There are 5 kernels, start core will get the sender kernel, take responsible for multicasting input tensor.

There are 5 kernels, start core will get the sender kernel, take responsible for multicasting input tensor.

- input tensor sender (0): Read all input tensor tiles if mcast input tensor
- input tensor receiver (1): Receive needed tensor tiles from sender.
- weight tensor reader (2): Read all weight tensor tiles if mcast weight
- weight tensor writer (3): Write computed to output circular buffer.
- compute kernel (4): Perform matmul compute.

| | **core 0** | **core 1** | **core 2** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **reader kernel** | **0, 2** | **1, 2** | **1, 2** |
| **writer kernel** | **3** | **3** | **3** |
| **compute kernel** | **4** | **4** | **4** |

- Each block is divided into subblocks:

 - Subblock's shape [in0\_block\_h, in0\_block\_w] satified:

 - Subblock is easily divided from the block.

 - per\_core\_M % in0\_block\_h == 0
 - per\_core\_N % in0\_block\_w == 0
 - maximize core's compute ability (can storge maximize 8 output tiles):

 - in0\_block\_w \* in0\_block\_h <= 8
 - In this example, subblock's shape is [2, 1]
- One output subblocks:

 - Require many inner dim blocks from input tensor, each inner dim blocks are used to calculate accumulated results.

- Require many inner dim blocks from input tensor, each inner dim blocks are used to calculate accumulated results.

 - In one inner dim block, tile stride by 1 till reach the end of inner dim block width, move to the next row.
 - Inner dim blocks stride by `in0_block_w` (stride).
 - When inner dim blocks reach the end of a row, move to the next heads, stride by `Dt * Mt + a_block_w - Dt` (big stride) until all head iterated.

 - ```
dt_block_counter++; if (dt_block_counter == in0_dt_num_blocks) { in0_tensor_current_inner_dim_block_start_tile_id += in0_tensor_next_inner_dim_block_big_stride; dt_block_counter = 0; } else { in0_tensor_current_inner_dim_block_start_tile_id += in0_tensor_next_inner_dim_block_stride; }

```

![image-20260312-041051.png](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/341999682/image-20260312-041051.png?api=v2)

# 4. Performance Analysis and Issues.

Experiment on A0

- Correct test: 50 test cases.

!image-20260311-182455.png
> All PCC bigger than 0.9999, all test passed

- Memory footprint on [b, h, s, d, o] = [2,32,128,64,512] (Mt = 4, Nt = 16), DRAM case:

 - Input tensor: 2×32×128×64x2 Bytes = 1 MiB
 - Weight tensor: 2048x512x2 Bytes = 2 MiB
 - Output tensor: 2 \* 128 \* 512 \* 2 = 256 KiB

 - This tensor is auto allocated in DRAM because we are not passing one.
 - Number of cores: 16 = Nt / div\_up(Nt, grid\_core.x \* grid\_core.y)

 - Per\_core\_M: 4 = Mt
 - Per\_core\_N: 1 = div\_up(Nt, grid\_core.x \* grid\_core.y)
 - in0\_block\_w: 2
 - in0\_block\_h: 4
 - in1\_block\_w: 1
 - in1\_block\_h: 2
 - CBs (double buffering):

 - cbA: 32 KiB → in0\_block\_w \* in0\_block\_h = 8 tiles in, 8 tiles out
 - cbW: 16 KiB → in1\_block\_w \* in1\_block\_h = 2 tiles in, 2 tiles out → but i allocated 8 tiles, mistake huhu?
 - cbOut/cbIterm: 16 KiB → per\_core\_M \* per\_core\_N = 4 tiles in, 4 tiles out

!image-20260312-051422.png

- Performance test (End to End) recorded manually:

 - Iteration: 20
 - Warm up: 5

| **Test case** **[B, H, S, D, 0]** | **Memory** | **Data type** | **Fused time** **(µs)** | **Unfused time** **(µs)** | **Speed up ratio** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2,32,128,64,512 | DRAM | fp16 | 4.66 | 18.47 | 3.96x |
| 2,16,256,64,1024 | DRAM | bf16 | 2.70 | 7.08 | 2.62x |
| 2,32,256,32,512 | L1 | fp16 | 4.57 | 14.93 | 3.27x |
| 4,32,384,32,1024 | L1 | bf16 | 4.01 | 10.02 | 2.49x |

> On all test case, fused operation **2.2-4 times** faster the permute → reshape → matmul.

Optimized result coming up from remove unnecessary time to banks, in compute time, mcast and reuse technique is applied to reduce memory read.

Further applied techniques:

- Double buffering.
- HiFi2 for fp16 dtype.

Although considered speed up ratio, there are some big issues that you must use it with cautions:

Further applied techniques:

- Double buffering.
- HiFi2 for fp16 dtype.

Although considered speed up ratio, there are some big issues that you must use it with cautions:

- Narrow in input, output dtypes, layout supports (currently only supports tile layout for both input and output).
- Bias is required to be 1D tensor, so bias is currently not supported.
- S got constraint: s % 32 == 0 → in inference phase, when generating text, seq are set 1, make it inefficient in both memory and speed.

# 5. Potential Optimizations and Recommendations.

Applied Mcast 1D is not optimized since all read process from banks executed by only 1 core, I considered this approach is not optimized.

Sharding can be considered but since 2 last dims are read first, it can reduce reading time but can't minimize reading time.

# 6. Conclusion

This ops inherited many techniques from matmul operation, main different:

- Input constraint:

 - Matmul got more flexible input shape.
 - Concat\_linear strictly have [B, H, S, D] shape.
- Read order:

 - Matmul read value dim by dim from the last.
 - Concat\_linear need to read from D to H to S to B.

There are no much different in write and compute kernel. If i have to start again, i will clone the matmul operation first, and then just change the read kernel logic.

Overall performance show that this operations work well on specified scenario: FFN with multi-head in multihead-attention transformer like MiniCPM.

section_title: GPU vs NPU – Pros and Cons Analysis
content:
| Aspect | GPU | NPU |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Main purpose** | General-purpose parallel computing | AI / neural network acceleration |
| **Flexibility** | Very high | Limited, task-specific |
| **AI performance** | High, but not specialized | Highly optimized for AI workloads |
| **Power efficiency** | Lower (high power consumption) | Higher (better performance-per-watt) |
| **Training** | Very suitable | Usually not designed for training |
| **Inference** | Works well but inefficient at scale | Optimized for fast, low-latency inference |
| **Software ecosystem** | Mature (CUDA, libraries, tools) | Smaller, domain-specific (TTNN, TT-Metal) |
| **Model deployment** | Easy but costly | Efficient and scalable |
| **Debugging** | Easier, better tooling | Harder, limited tooling |
| **Cost efficiency** | High cost in production | Better cost efficiency in deployment |

High-level intermediate representations (IRs) play a crucial role in machine learning (ML) compilers, enabling optimization and code generation for various hardware platforms. These IRs provide an abstract representation of the machine learning models that can be transformed and optimized before being compiled into executable code.

!image-20250826-040518.png

DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) is a graph structure where nodes represent operations or computations, and edges indicate data dependencies between these operations. A DAG-based IR represents a program's computation flow using this graph structure and it can be either **static**, established at compile-time and unchanging during execution, or **dynamic**, evolving during runtime based on program behavior. Dynamic DAG-based representations are commonly used in just-in-time (JIT) compilation systems and some dynamic programming languages. They can enable more adaptive and context-sensitive optimizations, as the IR can be tailored to the specific data and execution paths encountered during runtime.

Additionally, DAG-based IRs can be both **symbolic** or **imperative**: A symbolic representation focuses on capturing the relationships and semantics of computations using symbolic expressions. On the other hand, an imperative DAG representation focuses on capturing the actual sequence of operations and their effects on data. It represents computations as a sequence of instructions that are executed step-by-step. MXNet provides both Symbolic and Imperative DAG based IRs.

Here's a really good article on the tradeoffs between symbolic and imperative IR: https://mxnet.apache.org/versions/1.9.1/api/architecture/program_model

The simplicity of DAG-based IRs makes it easier to implement automatic differentiation and compile for heterogeneous execution environments (e.g., executing parts of the graph on specialized hardware).

**Examples of DAG-based IR**: Torch.Fx, XLA HLO, ONNX Computation Graph, Glow High-level IR, DLVM.
The goal of constant folding is to **evaluate expressions involving constants at compile time** and replace them with their computed values, reducing the need for runtime computations.

Operator strength reduction aims to **replace computationally expensive operations** with equivalent, less expensive operations.

**Example**: Original operation: `y = x / 2` Strength-reduced operation: `y = x * 0.5`

This optimization aims to **identify and eliminate "No-op" tensor operators**. No-op tensor operators refer to ML operators that do not do any meaningful computation, i.e., are identity functions. For example, a scalar multiply operation with the scalar being 1 is an identity function, and this optimization removes these No-op nodes from the computation graph.

Zero-dimensional tensor elimination is a graph optimization technique aimed at r**emoving tensors with zero dimensions** from the computation graph. Zero-dimensional tensors represent scalars, and eliminating them simplifies the graph and reduces unnecessary overhead associated with tensor operations.

Example: Original computation graph:

`Input: x (shape: [])` `Operation: y = x * 2`

After zero-dimensional tensor elimination:

`Input: x (scalar)` `Operation: y = x * 2`

Block-level algebraic simplification involves applying various mathematical identities, properties, and transformations to simplify operations. The goal is to replace complex expressions with simpler forms that are equivalent in terms of their mathematical results. These optimizations leverage the commutativity, associativity, and distributivity of different tensor operators to generate efficient, semantically equivalent code.

In Operator fusion, multiple consecutive tensor operations or nodes in a computation graph are combined into a single fused operation. This optimization aims to reduce memory transfers, improve cache utilization, and minimize overhead by executing multiple operations together as a single unit.

Here's an example:

Original computation graph: `Operation 1: y = x * 2` `Operation 2: z = y + 3` `Operation 3: w = z / 4`

After operator fusion:

`Fused Operation: w = (x * 2 + 3) / 4`

In this example, the consecutive operations `x * 2`, `y + 3`, and `z / 4` are fused into a single operation `(x * 2 + 3) / 4`. This **reduces the need for intermediate memory storage, minimizes memory transfers, and improves cache locality** by executing these operations together.

Operator fusion can be particularly effective on hardware with limited memory bandwidth, like GPUs, as it reduces the amount of data movement between memory and computation units. It also reduces the overhead of launching multiple kernels for individual operations.

Dead code elimination (DCE) **identifies and removes code segments whose outcomes or effects are unused**. DCE aims to improve efficiency by eliminating computations that don't impact the final result. Dead code is often generated by earlier optimizations and is not typically the programmer's doing. **DCE, like common sub-expression elimination (CSE), is performed after other graph optimizations.** Furthermore, DCE encompasses optimizations like dead store elimination, which discards unnecessary tensor stores that won't be utilized. These optimizations collectively enhance the effectiveness and streamline the computation graph by targeting redundant or unused operations.

Layout transformation optimizes data layouts of tensors in the computation graph to enhance memory access and cache utilization. It introduces layout transformation nodes in the graph, but the actual transformation occurs during computation. Different hardware benefits from specific layouts, e.g., NCHW on GPUs, [1, 1, H \* W, C] on NPUs. Compilers consider hardware-specific libraries, complex layouts, and heterogenous units. However, layout transformations impact performance and resource usage, demanding careful consideration

```
import timeit
conv_layer = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(100, 3)

@tf.function
def conv_fn(image):
 return conv_layer(image)

image = tf.zeros([1, 200, 200, 100])
# Warm up
conv_layer(image); conv_fn(image)
print("Eager conv:", timeit.timeit(lambda: conv_layer(image), number=10))
print("Function conv:", timeit.timeit(lambda: conv_fn(image), number=10))
print("Note how there's not much difference in performance for convolutions")
```

```
Eager conv: 0.006058974999177735
Function conv: 0.005791576000774512
Note how there's not much difference in performance for convolutions
```

When TensorFlow (especially TF1.x or TF2 with `tf.function`) builds a graph, it automatically applies a number of graph-level optimization passes. These passes are contained in Grappler – TensorFlow's optimization framework.

PyTorch has dynamic graph compilation mechanisms for optimization:

- `TorchScrip`: trace or script code into static graph → then apply optimization.
- FX (Functional Transformations): Python-level graph extraction tool for analysis and optimization.
- `TorchDynamo` (**PyTorch 2.x**): capture Python bytecode and compile to graph → then use backend compiler like `TorchInductor` for optimization.

```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class MyModel(nn.Module):
 def __init__(self):
 super().__init__()
 self.conv = nn.Conv2d(3, 16, 3)
 self.bn = nn.BatchNorm2d(16)
 self.relu = nn.ReLU()

 def forward(self, x):
 return self.relu(self.bn(self.conv(x)))

model = MyModel()
scripted = torch.jit.script(model)
print(scripted.graph)
```

Detailed pipeline of `torch.compile:`

- `TorchDynamo` captures Python execution into FX graphs.
- `AOTAutograd` generates functional forward/backward graphs.
- **Decomposition** lowers ops into core `ATen` primitives.
- `TorchInductor` compiles graphs into optimized fused kernels (CPU C++ / GPU Triton).
- Runtime executes cached kernels with guard checks.

!image-20250827-023318.png

```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(
 nn.Conv2d(3, 16, 3),
 nn.ReLU()
)

compiled_model = torch.compile(model) # TorchDynamo + Inductor
```

!image-20250827-024922.png

See more: Interactive Tenstorrent Sofware Architecture Diagram

TT‑Forge is Tenstorrent's MLIR-based compiler, which connects high-level ML frameworks (such as **PyTorch**, **JAX**, **TensorFlow**, **ONNX**, etc.) to Tenstorrent hardware (such as **Wormhole**, **Blackhole**) through multiple IR layers and complex optimizations.

It consists of three main components:

- Frontends: The task of importing models from the input framework.
- Backend/Middle compiler (TT‑MLIR): Performs a series of optimizations, passes down to the internal IR, and generates code.
- TT‑Blacksmith: A tool that automates loading, running, checking results, and benchmarking.

**TT-Forge-FE** is a graph compiler frontend that optimizes and transforms deep learning computational graphs to improve performance and efficiency. It is part of the broader **TT-Forge** ecosystem, which compiles and executes machine learning models on Tenstorrent hardware platforms such as **Wormhole** and **Blackhole**. TT-Forge-FE ingests models from frameworks like **PyTorch**, **ONNX**, and **TensorFlow** using the **TVM Intermediate Representation (IR)**.

TT-MLIR is a compiler that is interfacing with `tt-metalium` our opens source low level AI Hardware SDK. TT-MLIR provides a solution for optimizing machine learning and other compute workloads for all **tenstorrent** hardware. TT-MLIR bridges the gap between all different ML Frameworks and **Tenstorrent** Hardware. TT-MLIR is broken into different dialects:

- **TTIR Dialect**: Our common IR that can then be lowered into multiple different backends
- **TTNN Dialect**: Our entry point into the TTNN Library of Ops
- **TTMetalium** Dialect: Our entry point into directly accessing `tt-metalium` kernels.

**TT-NN** provides a mechanism for tracing operations and memory activities in a neural network's execution.

Using this trace, it is possible to analyze the operation even without executing it on the accelerator. The output trace can then be processed to get a single number like Peak Memory Load or to print tabular data or visualize a call graph.

Important note: **TT-NN** graph trace is independent from the **TT-Forge** compiler graph. It is primarily used for visualization and analysis of model execution, not for compilation or optimization.
Renders the sequence and connectivity of **traced PyTorch and TTNN operations** as they move through your model, helping you understand end‑to‑end dataflow and op boundaries.

- You need a **model‑level view** of operator ordering and dependencies.
- You're validating that TTNN ops are being invoked as expected from a PyTorch graph.

```
from ttnn.tracer import trace, visualize
with trace():
 ...
 output = ttnn.from_device(output)
visualize(output, file_name='path/to/file_name')
```
Visualizes **captured runtime graphs** that track **memory allocations, buffer operations, and device‑level activities**. Useful for performance analysis and correctness at the runtime layer.

- You're investigating **memory behavior** (allocations/frees, buffer lifetimes).
- You need a **device‑centric** view of execution and resource usage.

When run in `NO_DISPATCH` run mode, real allocations do not happen, so trace collection does not have side effects on the allocator state. You can pass unrealistically big tensors in this mode and unless an operation does upper limit validation, you still can collect the trace. In this mode trace collection is faster because ops are dispatched to the device.

When run in the `NORMAL` mode, memory can be fragmented, which can lead to a different trace and you see real addresses where everything is allocated.

Another capability of the Graph, is to collect the whole set of arguments provided for each operation executed in a ttnn call. This can be done in an individual operation, or a model as a whole, providing additional information about the internals of the operations

```
ttnn.graph.begin_graph_capture(ttnn.graph.RunMode.NORMAL)
.... # Your code here
ttnn.graph.begin_graph_capture(ttnn.graph.RunMode.NORMAL)
captured_graph = ttnn.graph.end_graph_capture()
ttnn.graph.visualize(captured_graph, file_name="my_trace.svg")
# or you can also export trace data as JSON for programmatic analysis
from ttnn.graph_tracer_utils import GraphTracerUtils
json_data = GraphTracerUtils.convert_to_json(captured_graph)
# data = GraphTracerUtils.serialize_graph(captured_graph)
```
For a more detailed trace format of the low-level graph capture, please refer to the following documentation. Trace Format.

- Start with `ttnn.torch_tracer.visualize` to confirm that your **model's op flow** (PyTorch → TTNN) matches expectations.
- Drop down to `ttnn.graph.visualize` when you need **runtime details**: memory footprints, buffer transitions, and device‑level sequencing.

- To use graph tracing in the SSR model, adjust torch\_tracer.py, tracer.py. These handle graph tracing for models defined by bos-metal (wrapper nn.Module).

- TT-Metal's tracing system is incompatible with fast runtime mode. When you try to enable tracing while `ttnn.CONFIG.enable_fast_runtime_mode` is `True`, the system raises a `ValueError` to prevent this unsupported configuration.

```
# Export enviroment variable
export TTNN_CONFIG_OVERRIDES='{"enable_fast_runtime_mode": false}'
# or programmatically
import ttnn
ttnn.CONFIG.enable_fast_runtime_mode = False
```

- Forwarding through a module wrapped by `nn.Module` is now unsupported forhttps://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bossemi/pages/edit-v2/142542732#ttnn.torch_tracer.visualize-%E2%80%94-Operation-Flow-Tracing.

It will raise an error,

```
E TypeError: object of type 'TracedTTNNTensor' has no len()
```

- This error occurs because the **visualization utilities in TT-Metal rely on Graphviz**. If Graphviz is not installed, or if its executables (e.g., `dot`) are not available on your system `PATH`, visualization will fail.

Example error message:

```
graphviz.backend.execute.ExecutableNotFound: failed to execute PosixPath('dot'), make sure the Graphviz executables are on your systems' PATH
```

You need to install Graphviz and ensure its executables are accessible from the command line.

```
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install graphviz
```

After installation, confirm that `dot` is on your `PATH`:

```
which dot
```

If it prints a valid path (e.g., `/usr/bin/dot`), Graphviz is correctly installed.

- https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/b30b189303c349f3dfb870d4c2dfb73ac7b77f67/tech_reports/ttnn/graph-tracing.md
- https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-forge-fe
- https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-mlir
- https://www.computer.org/csdl/proceedings-article/iist/2024/744200a332/21B9OQW0QcE
- …
[[AISW-249] [GridSample] Benchmarking TT GridSample and BOS GridSample - Jira](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/browse/AISW-249)

content:
==**Not relate to quantization**==

This spreadsheet is a **technical evaluation/planning document** that compares **5 AI/ML models** for computer vision tasks.

It assesses each model's:

- estimated development time,
- technical requirements,
- framework dependencies,
- and use cases.

==**Not relate to quantization**==

Provides an overview and comparison of several state‑of‑the‑art deep learning models used in **2D/3D object detection**, **BEV perception**, and **autonomous driving**

---

==**Not relate to quantization**==

llama3.2 - 1B, llama3.2 - 3B, llama3.1 - 8B input/output shape, num params, flops for each layer

---

The presentation provides an overview of **quantization techniques** used in deep learning inference, how they're applied in hardware, and their implementation status across different frameworks and platforms.

---

- Quantization helps accelerate **inference** by performing operations on **integer tensors** instead of floating‑point.
- This reduces:

 - **Computation cost**
 - **Energy consumption**
 - **Data transfer overhead**, especially for MAC operations
- INT8 quantization typically maintains model accuracy while improving performance.

---

- Many models include nonlinear activations (e.g., **ReLU**).
- A trivial dequantize–compute–quantize workflow slows inference.
- Faster solutions:

 - **Layer fusion** such as Conv2D + ReLU
 - **Custom quantized kernels**, e.g., Quantized ReLU .

---

- ONNX simulates quantization using **Quantize-Dequantize (QDQ)** nodes inserted between operators.
- Operators like Conv still run in **FP32**, but each tensor receives:

 - A **scale factor**
 - A **zero‑point**

---

- A quantization model zoo with **15 models** and **6 quantization techniques**.
- End‑to‑end pipeline from quantization to evaluation is available.
- Output models follow the **ONNX QDQ** format.
- Available since **M1.2 release**.

---

- Tenstorrent replaces QDQ nodes with **Requantize** nodes.
- The Buda backend provides:

 - **Quantize → Requantize → Requantize API**
 - Low‑level **SFPU** kernel support for quantization.
- Metal backend does not provide equivalent functionality yet.

---

- The quantization computation pipeline is described as:
**L1 → unpacker → DEST → SFPU → DEST → packer → L1**
- The slides contrast:

 - **Default (FP16) flow**
 - **Quantized (INT8/INT32) flow**, with quantizer/dequantizer components.

---

The presentation shows how quantization reduces inference cost, how it is represented in ONNX, how BOS implements quantization in its workflow, and how Tenstorrent hardware provides additional specialized support. It also outlines the detailed data flow for implementing quantized operations in hardware.

---

Quantization experiments on MobileNet family models using different AIMET configurations.

List some quantization papers:

- Adaround
- CLE&BC
- AdaQuant
- Qualcomm-AutoQuant
- PD-Quant

---

==**Not relate to quantization**==

Presentation on Llama model techniques.

---
Presentation on Bos Quantization GUI Concepts​

1. User select model(to quantize) and dataset(for calibration)​
2. FE copy(or move) those files to designated mounted volume(which BE docker directly read)​
3. BE directly read model and datasets, do quantization and save outputs(quantized model and etc.) into mounted volume.​
4. FE move(or copy) outputs to designated Path which user can access. And shows result on interface.​

- Compared and analyzed five popular Python-based web frameworks.​

---

==**Not relate to quantization**==

Merge request flow

---

The presentation introduces **AdaQuant**, an **adaptive quantization technique** designed to improve the accuracy of quantized neural networks by *jointly optimizing* the quantization parameters and weights at a per‑layer level.

- Traditional quantization minimizes MSE between full‑precision tensors and their quantized versions.
- While simple and widely used, this approach:

 - **Cannot differentiate** which weights/activations are more sensitive.
 - **Hurts accuracy**, especially for lightweight models.

AdaQuant reduces quantization error more effectively by:

- Performing **per-layer joint optimization** of:

 - Weights
 - Weight quantization parameters
 - Activation quantization parameters
- Minimizing MSE between:

 - **Original layer output**
 - **Quantized layer output**
- Essentially works like **per-layer QAT (Quantization-Aware Training)** but more lightweight.

1. Pre-quantize to obtain initial Q‑params:
`Act_range, Act_zeropoint, W_range, W_zeropoint`
2. Cache inputs and outputs of target layers.
3. For each layer:

 - Quantize forward pass.
 - Compute MSE loss.
 - Backpropagate to update:

 - Weight
 - Weight Q‑params
 - Activation Q‑params

This turns Q‑params into **trainable parameters**, unlike typical static quantization.

- **AdaQuant Q‑wrapper**

 - Holds *Range* and *Zero‑point* as **trainable** parameters.
- **AIMET Q‑wrapper**

 - Holds *Max*, *Min*, *Scale*, *Offset* as **static** parameters.

To integrate AdaQuant into AIMET:

- Convert AIMET parameters → AdaQuant format.
- Optimize using AdaQuant.
- Convert back.

- AdaQuant improves INT8 accuracy for both MobileNetV2 and ResNet50.
- **MobileNetV2:**

 - FP32: 71.64%
 - INT8: 70.25%
 - AdaQuant: 70.94% (≈ +0.7% improvement)
- **AIMET implementation** improves but less than original AdaQuant.
- Works best for **asymmetric quantization**.

- Integrate AdaQuant fully into BOS quantization code.
- Compare and analyze AIMET vs AdaQuant quantization mechanisms.
**AdaQuant** is an optimization-based quantization technique that:

section_title: **12.2.1. Turns quantization parameters (scale/zeropoint/range) into trainable variables.**
content: Instead of fixing quantization parameters, AdaQuant **learns** them through gradient-based optimization.

For each layer:

- Cache full-precision input and output.
- Quantize forward pass.
- Compute MSE loss:
 `loss = || quantized_output – original_output ||²`
- Backpropagate gradients through:

 - Weights
 - Activation Q‑params
 - Weight Q‑params

- More accurate than simple post-training quantization.
- Lighter and layer-local compared to full QAT.
- Works particularly well for depthwise-heavy models (e.g., MobileNetV2).

- Post-training accuracy recovery.
- Asymmetric quantization workflows.
- Deployment on edge/low‑power hardware.

---

Vision dataset survey.

==**Not relate to quantization**==

This presentation provides a **deep technical walkthrough** of how to analyze, modify, and extend the AIMET (AI Model Efficiency Toolkit) framework, focusing on **quantization**, **custom C++ integration**, **pybind11 bindings**, **encoding analyzers**, and **adding new data types** across Python, C++, and CUDA components.

The deck guides developers through:

- Understanding AIMET's structure
- Adding new quantizers and analyzers
- Extending encoding logic
- Integrating custom data types (e.g., BF16)
- Modifying CMake, CUDA, and pybind11 bindings
- Testing customized AIMET components

It explains how quantization works across:

- **Python layer** (quantsim, qc\_quantize\_op.py)
- **C++ layer** (TensorQuantizer, EncodingAnalyzers)

The slides show directory structures, critical files, and function call graphs.

Walkthrough includes:

- Adding new `.cpp`/`.h` files to `DlQuantization`
- Implementing custom logic inside `TensorQuantizer`
- Updating:

 - CMakeLists

The presentation traces the function flow:

section_title: **15.6. Adding a New Data Type (e.g., BF16)**

`BFp16Quantization.cpp/hpp`

The deck shows how to rebuild AIMET:

```
source $BUILDDIR/aimet/packaging/envsetup.sh

mkdir -p $BUILDDIR/aimet/build && cd $BUILDDIR/aimet/build

cmake $BUILDDIR/aimet -DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON \

 -DENABLE_CUDA=ON -DENABLE_ONNX=ON -DENABLE_TEST=OFF

make -j8

make install
```

N1 A0 use same tensix core architecture as blackhole's tensix core architecture.

N1 B0 will use next version of tensix core architecture and it's not same as N1 A0 tensix core.

---

**We** ***can*** **change the tt-metal software code — it is fully open-source**, and Tenstorrent explicitly provides it on GitHub.

---

Tenstorrent publishes the entire tt-metal stack on GitHub:

- Runtime
- HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)
- Kernel infrastructure
- Device handling
- Build system
- Runtime tests

You are free to:

✔ Modify the source code ✔ Add new features ✔ Fix bugs ✔ Build custom runtimes ✔ Create custom kernels ✔ Fork and extend the repository

There are no license restrictions that prevent modification for research, development, or product use (standard open-source license applies).

---

section_title: 2. **You must still conform to hardware constraints**
content:
Although you *can* change the software, the hardware (Tensix ISA, tile memory, mesh network) imposes real constraints.

You can modify:

- runtime behavior
- scheduling logic
- how kernels are launched
- DMA strategies
- memory layouts
- communication patterns

But you **cannot** change:

- hardware mesh topology
- Tensix instruction set
- physical tile memory size
- hardware DMA engine behavior

Those are fixed by silicon.

---

section_title: 3. **Changing tt-metal is common in advanced development**
content:
Tenstorrent engineering teams (and some customers) modify tt-metal when:

- adding new kernel types
- optimizing hardware paths
- improving DMA overlap
- enhancing multi-chip routing
- debugging new chip features
- building custom MLIR → tt-metal pipelines
- experimenting with new tiling strategies

For deep compiler/hardware research, modifying tt-metal is often necessary.

---

Even if you modify tt-metal, the compiler expects certain behaviors:

- DRAM allocation
- tile-memory mapping
- kernel API contracts
- DMA correctness
- synchronization semantics

You must ensure these remain consistent, or you may break model execution.

---
**Yes, tt-metal is fully open-source and modifiable, but changes must respect the hardware interface and runtime/compiler contracts.**

section_title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
content:
You *can* modify/improve an AI compiler—but you have to pick **which layer** you're touching and **why**. I'll frame this in a Tenstorrent/tt-metal context, but the ideas apply to most AI compilers (XLA, TVM, Glow, etc.).

I'll break it into:

1. Where an AI compiler can be improved (layers)
2. Concrete examples of modifications
3. How this ties into tt-metal / Tenstorrent
4. A practical "how to get started" path

---

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

section_title: 1️⃣ Layers where you can improve an AI compiler
content:
Think of the compiler stack like this:

```
[ Frontend: PyTorch / ONNX / XLA / HF ]
 ↓
[ High-level IR: graph, ops, fusion ]
 ↓
[ Mid-level IR: tiling, layout, quantization ]
 ↓
[ Low-level IR: kernels, scheduling, memory ]
 ↓
[ Runtime/HAL: tt-metal, CUDA, ROCm, etc. ]
 ↓
[ Hardware: NPU/GPU/ASIC ]
```

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

section_title: 1️⃣ Layers where you can improve an AI compiler
content:
You can improve the compiler at several levels:

1. **Frontend / Graph level**

 - Better support for new ops (e.g., RoPE, FlashAttention, MoE)
 - Smarter graph fusion (e.g., `MatMul + Bias + ReLU` → fused op)
 - Constant folding, dead-code elimination, shape inference
2. **Tiling & layout level**

 - Choose better tile sizes for your hardware
 - Tune layouts for cache/tile memory (e.g., row-major vs block-major)
 - Special tiling strategies for attention, convolutions, etc.
3. **Kernel selection & specialization**

 - Pick the best kernel implementation per shape/dtype
 - Add new specialized kernels for common patterns
 - Auto-tune kernel variants
4. **Scheduling & parallelization**

 - Order ops to maximize overlap of compute + communication
 - Better pipeline parallelism and model parallelism
 - Smarter multi-core / multi-chip mapping
5. **Memory planning**

 - Reuse buffers to reduce peak memory
 - Optimize DRAM ↔ tile memory traffic
 - Use recomputation instead of storing huge intermediates
6. **Quantization & numerical optimization**

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

section_title: 1️⃣ Layers where you can improve an AI compiler
content:
- Reuse buffers to reduce peak memory
 - Optimize DRAM ↔ tile memory traffic
 - Use recomputation instead of storing huge intermediates
6. **Quantization & numerical optimization**

 - Add new quantization schemes (e.g., per-channel, FP8, int4)
 - Mixed precision policies
 - Error-compensation paths for sensitive layers
7. **Cost models & auto-tuning**

 - Build an analytical or learned cost model for performance
 - Auto-search tile sizes, layout, kernel variants

---

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

content: Here are some specific things you *could actually implement*.

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

section_title: Example A: Better fusion for Transformer blocks
content:
**Goal:** Reduce memory traffic and kernel launches in attention/MLP.

**What to modify:**

- High-level IR passes:

 - Pattern-match sequences like:

 - `MatMul → Add → LayerNorm → Activation`
 - `Q/K/V projections → ScaledDotProductAttention`
 - Replace them with a **single fused op** in IR.
- Then implement a custom kernel (or kernel sequence) for that fused op.

**Effect:**

- Fewer DRAM reads/writes
- Better cache/tile reuse
- Lower kernel launch overhead
- Big latency and throughput gains especially on small batch/sequence

---

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

section_title: Example B: Smarter tiling for Tenstorrent mesh
content:
**Goal:** Match tile layout to **mesh topology** and **tile memory size**.

**What to modify:**

- Mid-level IR tiling pass:

 - Instead of fixed tile size, choose tile shapes based on:

 - Tensix tile memory size
 - Mesh neighbor bandwidth
 - Shape of the tensor (e.g., favor tiling along sequence dimension)
- Add a cost model:

 - Estimate cost = compute\_time + communication\_time + DMA\_time
 - Search over candidate tile sizes, choose the best.

**Effect:**

- Higher utilization of Tensix cores
- Less communication bottleneck across the mesh
- Better scaling when going from 1 chip → multi-chip

---

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

content:
**Goal:** Always pick the "right" kernel for the job.

**What to modify:**

- Kernel selection logic:

 - For `MatMul`:

 - Small M/K/N → use small-tile kernel
 - Big M × N with specific ratios → choose better-optimized kernel
 - For `Conv`:

 - Depthwise vs regular
 - Small filter vs large filter
- Add specialized kernels:

 - e.g., "matmul with fused bias+gelu"
 - "conv2d + batchnorm + relu"

**Effect:**

- 2–5× improvement for common shapes
- Fewer intermediate writes
- Better mapping to hardware pipelines

---

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

content:
**Goal:** Overlap compute and communication to hide latency.

**What to modify:**

- Low-level scheduling pass:

 - Insert asynchronous DMA ops earlier
 - Interleave compute ops with DMA ops
 - Use double-buffering in tile memory
- Define a schedule representation like:

 - "preload next tile while current tile is computing"
 - "start sending outputs to next core before finishing entire batch"

**Effect:**

- Higher throughput, especially multi-chip
- Lower bubbles in the pipeline
- More deterministic performance

---

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

content:
tt-metal is the **runtime/HAL**. Improving the compiler typically means:

- You **don't** change tt-metal for most algorithmic improvements.
- You **do** change:

 - how the compiler emits **tt-metal calls**
 - which **kernels** it asks tt-metal to run
 - how it arranges tile DMAs and kernel sequences

But sometimes you *also* improve tt-metal when:

- Adding new Tensix kernels
- Exposing new runtime APIs (better DMA primitives, new sync modes)
- Improving internal scheduling primitives used by the compiler backend

So the picture looks like:

```
[ Compiler ]
 - IR passes
 - Tiling
 - Fusion
 - Scheduling
 ↓ emits
[ tt-metal calls + kernel configs ]
 ↓ executes
[ Tensix cores / mesh / DRAM ]
```

You mostly improve the **top part**, but occasionally extend the bottom.

---

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

content:
If your team actually wants to contribute or modify an AI compiler (especially for Tenstorrent-like hardware), a practical sequence is:

1. **Pick 1–2 target models**

 - e.g., LLaMA-2 / GPT-style LLM, or a ResNet / ViT
 - Profile them to see where time is spent.
2. **Locate the hot spots in the compiler**

 - Are you losing time on:

 - Many tiny kernels?
 - Poor tiling?
 - Bad memory layout?
 - Communication overhead?
3. **Choose one improvement axis**

 - Example:

 - "Improve attention block mapping"
 - "Better tiling for matmul"
 - "Fuse LN + MLP"
4. **Add or modify passes**

 - New fusion pass for the chosen pattern
 - New tiling heuristic
 - New kernel selection rule
 - Extra IR annotation for scheduling
5. **Tie into tt-metal**

 - Ensure the compiler emits the right:

 - tensor layouts
 - kernel IDs
 - DMA sequences
 - barrier/sync patterns
6. **Benchmark & iterate**

 - Compare before/after:

 - latency
 - throughput
 - utilization (cores, bandwidth)
 - Refine cost models and heuristics.

---

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

content:
You improve an AI compiler by:

- **Understanding the hardware** (mesh, tile memory, cores),
- **Changing the compiler passes** that decide:

 - how to break tensors into tiles,
 - how to place and route tiles across cores/chips,
 - which kernels to run,
 - and in what order,
- And making sure those decisions are translated into **efficient tt-metal calls** that the hardware executes well.

If you tell me:

- Which *hardware* you target (specific Tenstorrent chip / or GPU),
- And which *models* you care about (LLM, CNN, etc.),

metadata.title: How can we change_mprove AI Compiler
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/How can we change_mprove AI Compiler_209751681.md

content:
---

This guide explains how to use either Vosk or Whisper for speech recognition in the BOS demo.

- **Pros**: Runs entirely in browser, works offline, no server load, instant startup.
- **Cons**: Lower accuracy, larger model download (~50MB), limited language support.
- **Best for**: Quick demos, offline scenarios, lightweight deployments.

- **Pros**: Superior accuracy, better language support, smaller client footprint.
- **Cons**: Requires server processing, server dependency.
- **Best for**: Production deployments, accuracy-critical applications.

1. **Install Dependencies**

```
 pip install openai-whisper
 pip install soundfile librosa # For audio processing
 pip install onnx onnxruntime
 pip install coqui-tts
 ```

2. **Enable Whisper in HTML**

In `web/index.html`, ensure these lines are active:

```
 <!-- Option 2: Whisper (server-based, better accuracy) -->
 <script defer src="/client-assets/stt_whisper.js"></script>
 ```

And comment out Vosk:

```
 <!-- Option 1: Vosk (browser-based, works offline) -->
 <!-- <script defer src="https://unpkg.com/vosk-browser/dist/vosk.js"></script>
 <script defer src="/client-assets/stt.js"></script> -->
 ```

3. **Start the Server**

```
 sudo ./run_demo_server.sh
 ```

The Whisper model will be lazy-loaded on first use.
4. **Use Speech Input**

 - Click the microphone button (🎤)
 - Speak into your microphone
 - Audio is sent to server every 3 seconds for near-real-time transcription
 - Click stop (■) to finish and process remaining audio

1. **Enable Vosk in HTML**

In `web/index.html`:

```

 ```

Comment out Whisper:

```
 <!-- <script defer src="/client-assets/stt_whisper.js"></script> -->
 ```

2. **No server dependencies needed** - Vosk runs entirely in the browser
3. **First use**: Model downloads automatically (~50MB)

**POST** `/whisper/transcribe`

Request body:

```
{
 "audio": "base64-encoded audio data",
 "sampleRate": 16000,
 "language": "en",
 "model": "base"
}
```

Response:

```
{
 "text": "transcribed text",
 "language": "en",

}
```

| Model | Size | Speed | Accuracy | Use Case |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `tiny` | 39M | Fastest | Lowest | Quick tests |
| `base` | 74M | Fast | Good | Quick tests |
| `small` | 244M | Medium | Better **Recommended default** | |
| `medium` | 769M | Slow | Great | High accuracy needed |
| `large` | 1550M | Slowest | Best | Maximum accuracy |

Change model in `stt_whisper.js`:

```
body: JSON.stringify({
 // ... other params
 model: "small", // Change here
}),
```

In `stt_whisper.js`:

```

const TARGET_SAMPLE_RATE = 16000; // Audio sample rate
const WHISPER_ENDPOINT = "/whisper/transcribe"; // Server endpoint
```

**Problem**: `Whisper not installed` error

```
# Solution
pip install openai-whisper
```

**Problem**: Slow transcription

```
# Solutions:
# 1. Use smaller model (tiny/base)
# 2. Increase CHUNK_DURATION_MS for less frequent updates
```

**Problem**: Model not loading

- Check browser console for CORS errors
- Ensure model URL is accessible
- Clear browser cache

**Problem**: Poor accuracy

- Vosk has inherently lower accuracy than Whisper
- Consider switching to Whisper for better results

**Problem**: No microphone access

- Use HTTPS or localhost (required for microphone API)
- Check browser permissions
- Ensure microphone is connected

**Problem**: No audio detected

- Check microphone levels in system settings
- Try different browser (Chrome/Edge recommended)
- Check browser console for errors

Current DFSharding policy is optimized for **linear L1 chains** and makes **local layout decisions**.

Limitations observed:

- Operand-0–centric layout decisions
- No true global L1 peak modeling
- Weak fork-join handling
- Greedy search leads to suboptimal graph-level configurations
- Hard to scale across diverse model families

Target models contain:

- Residual fork-join (ResNet, ViT, LLaMA, Qwen)
- Concat joins & multi-scale graphs (YOLO, PDL, OFT)
- Repeated blocks (Transformer layers, bottlenecks)
- Layout churn boundaries (tokenization, upsample paths)
- Long-range tensor lifetimes (encoder-decoder skips)

---

- Support arbitrary DAG (fork / join / concat)
- Global L1 peak enforcement
- Deterministic layout selection
- Bounded compile time

- Deterministic output
- Debuggable and easy to trace
- Least runtime modification (unless strictly required)

---

```
TTIR
 → DAGGraphModel
 → CandidateGenerator
 → BeamSelectionEngine (Join-Aware)
 → LayoutFinalization
```

---
- TTIR graph (MLIR operations and SSA values)

- Extract a deterministic **DAG representation** of the computation graph
- Normalize structural patterns (fork / join / concat)
- Attach tensor metadata required for optimization

- Deterministic **DAGGraphModel**
- Stable **topological order**
- Graph structure used by optimization policy

- `DAGGraphModel`
- `LegalTensorLayoutAnalysis`
- `OpModel` constraints

- Generate **legal layout / config candidates** for each node
- Prune illegal combinations early

For each DAG node, bounded candidate search space:

> Node → {CandidateLayout1, CandidateLayout2, …}

- `DAGGraphModel`
- Candidate layout decisions (partial or final)

- Track **tensor lifetime across the DAG**
- Compute **global L1 peak memory usage**
- Enforce L1 budget constraints

Track:

> tensorL1PeakSize
> cbL1PeakSize
> peakL1MemorySize
> fragmentation\_reserve

Hard constraint:

```
peakL1MemorySize ≤ L1_budget * usageCap
```

For each candidate state:

> peak\_L1\_usage
> CB\_usage
> liveness\_state
> feasible / infeasible

We don't have a way to export this inline extension.

Where:

| **Term** | **Meaning** | **Source** | **Suggested W** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| We don't have a way to export this inline extension. | Normalized runtime estimate | `getOpRuntime()` | 0.35 |
| We don't have a way to export this inline extension. | Core grid under-utilization penalty | Grid size | 0.25 |
| We don't have a way to export this inline extension. | L1 pressure headroom penalty | `peakL1MemorySize / L1 budget` | 0.15 |
| We don't have a way to export this inline extension. | Layout conversion cost | Layout mismatch | 0.2 |
| We don't have a way to export this inline extension. | Circular buffer peak ratio | `cbL1PeakSize` | 0.05 |
**Algorithm:** Deterministic Beam Search over Topological Order with Join-Aware Constraints

At each node:

- Expand candidate layouts
- Enforce legality + L1 cap
- Update global state
- Keep top-K partial solutions (beam width)

```
State:
 chosen_layouts
 accumulated_score
 current_peak_L1
 live_tensor_summary
```

- Layout compatibility required
- Simultaneous liveness of both operands
- Peak = main + skip + add\_temp

- Extra staging buffer
- Higher CB pressure
- Additional memory penalty

---

| Pattern | Handled By |
| --- | --- |
| Residual Add | Join-aware memory tracking |
| Concat multi-scale | Concat-specific peak modeling |
| Transformer layers | Bounded beam avoids explosion |
| Long skips | Global liveness tracking |
| Layout churn | Conversion penalty term |
| Grid scaling | Core utilization term |

---

| DFSharding | DAG Policy |
| --- | --- |
| Linear chain | Graph-based |
| Greedy local | Bounded global search |
| Operand-0 layout focus | Full operand compatibility |
| Limited memory reasoning | Global peak enforcement |
| Hard to scale across models | Unified across CNN + Transformer |

---

Epic 1 — DAG Graph Model

Epic 2 — Candidate Generation

Epic 3 — Global Memory Tracking

Epic 4 — Beam Selection Engine

Epic 5 — Cost Model

Epic 6 — Validation

---

This DAG-based policy:

- Replaces local greedy chain optimization
- Enforces global L1 correctness
- Uses deterministic bounded beam search
- Integrates real OpModel runtime + memory signals
- Scales across CNN, Transformer, and multi-scale architectures

It provides a safe migration path from DFSharding while unlocking true graph-level optimization.

- 1 [Summary](#I.ModelAnalysis-Summary)
- 2 [Outputs](#I.ModelAnalysis-Outputs)
 - 2.1 [Output Checklist](#I.ModelAnalysis-OutputChecklist)
 - 2.2 [Output Description](#I.ModelAnalysis-OutputDescription)

Model analysis represents a critical early stage in the model development lifecycle. It lays the groundwork for all subsequent phases by providing a clear understanding of the model's architecture, complexity, and performance benchmarks.

---

Overall, after this step, the assigned person needs to **in-depth understand the model's architecture**, and open a **seminar to disseminate insights and gather collaborative feedback**.

- Model overview document, includes (*recommended*)

- Background
- Model Overall Architecture
- Module block description
- Model size
- Computational cost (FLOPs)
- Performance benchmark data: FPS/TTFT/TPS/etc.

- Dataset description document
- Dataset prepration (should load to NAS server for sharing among teams)
- Development plan (development timeline, constraints estimation, etc.)

> **Note that:** *Target of model analysis step relies heavily on the target of model implementation*. **Current target** of model implementation within AISW Part focuses on ***inference (deployment) phase*** of the model, ***not training phase**.* Therefore, these strategies can be changed accordance to the purpose of the project in the future.

1. **Model information: (i) model architecture,** a detailed visualization of the model structure, highlighting the interconnections between layers and modules. From model graph, we can obtain information related to inference time as inputs and outputs, block-wise tensors size, model size (roughly estimated through model graph), etc. **(ii) model size and FLOPs,** quantitative assessments that reveal the model's computational complexity and efficiency.

!image-20250224-082529.png

2. **Performance Benchmark:** Metrics such as **FPS** and **hardware utilization** that indicate how the model performs under different operational scenarios.
3. **Dataset description:** Description of datasets that used for the model evaluation or demo is neccessary outcome of this step. Additionally, definition of inputs and outputs type should be comprehensively documented.
4. **Constraints Estimation and Development Plan:** As mentioned in the preceding section, the TTNN package currently lacks several deep learning features. This section estimates the impact of these limitations and outlines a phased development plan to address the identified gaps and enable broader model support.

- 1 [Summary](#II.PytorchImplementation-Summary)
- 2 [Outputs](#II.PytorchImplementation-Outputs)
- 3 [Implementation Practices](#II.PytorchImplementation-ImplementationPractices)

In most cases, this step serves as an opportunity to gain a thorough understanding of the model's implementation before transitioning to the TTNN implementation. Additionally, it allows us to **identify and define implementation constraints** that may not have been apparent during the initial model analysis.

---

See a model folder structure in Model Development. Output checklist of this section includes (*recommended*)

- Pytorch implementation under `models/bos_model/<model>/reference`
- Unit test for Pytorch implementation under `models/bos_model/<model>/tests`
- Additional dependencies list in `models/bos_model/<model>/requirements.txt`, *if any*
- List of unsupported device operations (add to development plan), *if any*

There are some best practices for this step:

1. **Using native pytorch for your implementation as much as possible. If not, define the dependencies list well!**
 - Mostly, the model that you're working on already has pytorch implementation elsewhere on public repository. It's worth noting that some models use torch-inherited frameworks such as MMCV which has complicated dependicies with pytorch and built of libary.
 - If you can, I recommend you to *implement the model in plain pytorch only* without effort to introduce any new dependencies to the environment.
2. **Choose only 1 model architecture for your implementation!**
 - After analysis phase, you must know well about your model architecture! Some models has complicated architecture configuration (for example, some model can has different Backbone architecture options).
 - I recommend you to *choose* *only 1 model architecture and stick to it*. This has your development simplified so much in the following stages.
