This document describes different techniques for optimizing model performance, specifically how to reduce the overhead between individual operations and between model forward pass invocations. Specifically, **Metal Trace** can be used to remove the host overhead of executing ops, reducing the gap between op invocations, whereas **Multiple Command Queues** can be used to reduce the overhead between model forward passes by overlapping the cost of IO transfers with operation execution, as illustrated in the following figure.

!image-20251118-035831.png

Metal Trace is a performance optimization feature that aims to remove the host overhead of constructing and dispatching operations for models and will show benefits when the host time to execute/dispatch a model exceeds the device time needed to run the operations.

This feature works by **recording** the commands for dispatching operations into a DRAM buffer and replaying these commands when executing a trace. This means that all of the operation's parameters are statically saved, the input/output tensor shapes, addresses, etc. do not change.

The following figure shows the runtime of a model execution that is host-bound. We see that the host is not able to run and dispatch commands to the device ahead of time, and the device is stuck stalling waiting for the host in order to receive commands to run operations, which is why there are large gaps between operations on the device.

!image-20250709-073056.png

!image-20250709-073056.png

With trace, we can eliminate a large portion of these gaps. In the figure below we now execute the model using trace. We see that the host finishes dispatching the model almost immediately and is just waiting for the device to finish for most of the time. On the device, we see that the gaps between ops is much smaller

!image-20250709-073123.png

Trace only supports capturing and executing operations, and no other commands such as reading/writing tensors. This also means that to capture the trace of a sequence of operations, we must run with program cache and have already compiled the target operations before we capture them.

In order to use trace, we need to use the following trace APIs:

- `trace_region_size`

This is a parameter to the device creation API (`ttnn.open_device()`), and this determines the size of the memory region we remove from the regular DRAM buffer space and where we can allocate our trace buffers on device.

```
 ttnn.open_device(device_id=0, trace_region_size=2048)
 ```

Since this is pre-allocating the region, there is currently no automated infrastructure in order to determine the size needed. To determine what size should be used, we can try capturing the operations we want to trace, and when this fails this will report the required size to use.
 `Always | FATAL | Creating trace buffers of size 751616B on device 0, but only 2048B is allocated for trace region.`
- `tid = ttnn.begin_trace_capture(device, cq_id=0)`

This marks the beginning of a trace.
- `ttnn.end_trace_capture(device, tid, cq_id=0)`

This marks the end of the trace. All operations that were dispatched between beginning and end will be recorded and associated with the trace id.
- `ttnn.execute_trace(device, tid, cq_id=0, blocking=False)`

This will execute the captured trace with the specified trace id, and is equivalent to running all the operations that were captured between begin and end

Since trace requires the tensor addresses to remain the same across replay (whether in DRAM or L1), we can either reuse the tensor or recreate it with the same specifications and buffer address each iteration using these APIs:

- To reuse the input tensor, use this API to copy new data to the pre-allocated tensor:
`ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, device_tensor, cq_id=0)`

This copies data from a host tensor to the allocated device tensor.
- Alternatively, recreate a new tensor with the same specifications and buffer address using this API:
`device_tensor = ttnn.allocate_tensor_on_device(tensor_spec, device)`

This allocates a tensor with the specified parameters on the device. The tensor data is uninitialized.

TT‑Metal's trace mechanism (sometimes called *Metal Trace*), it does require **persistent tensor addresses** (i.e., the input, output, and all intermediate tensor buffer addresses must remain the same across capture and replay). Whether the persistence must be in DRAM or L1 depends on memory strategy - but there are clearly defined patterns.

Normally for performance we try to allocate tensors in L1, but many models are not able to fit in L1 if we keep the input tensor in L1 memory. The easiest way to work around this is that we can allocate our input in DRAM so that we can keep the tensor persistent in memory.

```
# Allocate our persistent input tensor
input_dram_tensor = ttnn.allocate_tensor_on_device(shape, dtype, layout, device, sharded_dram_mem_config)

# First run to compile the model
ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_dram_tensor, cq_id=0)
output_tensor = run_model(input_dram_tensor)

# Capture the trace of the model
ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_dram_tensor, cq_id=0)
tid = ttnn.begin_trace_capture(device, cq_id=0)
# It is important that we keep the output tensor on device returned here, so that we have the output tensor and associated address to read from after executing trace
output_tensor = run_model(input_dram_tensor)
ttnn.end_trace_capture(device, tid, cq_id=0)

# Execute the trace
ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_dram_tensor, cq_id=0)
ttnn.execute_trace(device, tid, cq_id=0, blocking=False)
host_output_tensor = output_tensor.cpu(blocking=False)

# Final synchronize to wait for all outputs to be read to host since we used non-blocking reads
ttnn.synchronize_device(device)
```

Below is another strategy where our input tensor will be in L1 and we allow it to be deallocated during model inference. We will reallocate it at the same address and with the same specification (sharding configuration, layout, …) at the end of the forward pass.

```
# First run to compile the model
input_l1_tensor = host_tensor.to(device, sharded_l1_mem_config)
output_tensor = run_model(input_l1_tensor)

# Capture the trace of the model
# Record the address of the input tensor to trace so that we can validate we allocated our input tensor at the right address
input_l1_tensor = host_tensor.to(device, sharded_l1_mem_config)
input_trace_addr = input_l1_tensor.buffer_address()
spec = input_l1_tensor.spec
# Deallocate the previous output tensor here so that we will allocate our input tensor at the right address afterwards
output_tensor.deallocate(force=True)
tid = ttnn.begin_trace_capture(device, cq_id=0)
# It is important that we keep the output tensor on device returned here, so that we have the output tensor and associated address to read from after executing trace
output_tensor = run_model(input_l1_tensor)

# Try allocating our persistent input tensor here and verifying it matches the address that trace captured
input_l1_tensor = ttnn.allocate_tensor_on_device(spec, device)
assert input_trace_addr == input_l1_tensor.buffer_address()

ttnn.end_trace_capture(device, tid, cq_id=0)

# Execute the trace

ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_l1_tensor, cq_id=0)
ttnn.execute_trace(device, tid, cq_id=0, blocking=False)
host_output_tensor = output_tensor.cpu(blocking=False)

# Final synchronize to wait for all outputs to be read to host since we used non-blocking reads

ttnn.synchronize_device(device)

```

Metal supports up to two independent command queues for fast dispatch, allowing parallel command execution on a device. Both queues handle any dispatch command, so either can perform I/O data transfers or launch programs. Since the queues operate independently, we use events to synchronize them, ensure command order as well as synchronize host and device.

In order to use multiple command queues, we need to be familiar with the following APIs:

- `num_command_queues`

This is a parameter to the device creation API and sets how many command queues.

```
 ttnn.open_device(device_id=0, num_command_queues=2)
 ```

The default is one, and the max is two.
- `event = ttnn.record_event(device, cq_id=0)`

This records the event after all commands dispatched before it on the specified command queue finish. The event is visible to all command queues.
- `ttnn.wait_for_event(cq_id=0, event=event)`

This enqueues a command to the specified command queue to stall until the specified event occurs on the device. Commands sent to the queue after this waits command will not execute until the event occurs.
- `ttnn.event_synchronize(event)` blocks the host until the device has executed all operations prior to that event on that event's command queue.

The illustration of APIs:

!image-20251118-035553.png

Most of current implementation of model runners use **Compute and Output Readback on CQ 0**, **Input Writes CQ 1**.But the optimal strategy is using **Ops on CQ 0**, **Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1**.

section_title: 3.3.1. Compute and Output Readback on CQ 0, Input Writes on CQ 1
content:
A common setup assigns one queue to write inputs and another to dispatch programs and read outputs. This benefits device-bound scenarios where input tensor writing is slow, allowing the next input dispatch to overlap with the current model's execution.

This configuration suits models with **large input/output tensors and significant data transfer overhead**.

!image-20251118-033451.png

Key characteristics:

- CQ 1 handles input writes only.
- CQ 0 handles operations and output readback.
- Simpler event synchronization (2 events).
- Better for models where input transfer time limits performance.

Using two command queues—one for writes, one for running programs and reading—requires two events:

section_title: 3.3.1. Compute and Output Readback on CQ 0, Input Writes on CQ 1
content:
Using two command queues—one for writes, one for running programs and reading—requires two events:

- The first event (`write_event`) signals that the write on command queue 1 has completed. Command queue 0 waits on this event to execute its operations only after the write finishes.
- The second event (`op_event`) signals that command queue 0 has consumed the input tensor, allowing command queue 1 to overwrite it with new data. Command queue 1 waits on this event before writing the next input.

!image-20251118-035519.png

section_title: 3.3.1. Compute and Output Readback on CQ 0, Input Writes on CQ 1
content:
```
# This example uses 1 CQ for only writing inputs (CQ 1), and one CQ for executing programs/reading back the output (CQ 0)

# `op_event` signals when the first operation is completed. This is the consumer of the input tensor so once this is completed, we can issue the next write

# `write_event` signals when input write is completed. This is used to signal that the input tensor can be read/consumed

# Allocate our persistent input tensor
input_dram_tensor = ttnn.allocate_tensor_on_device(shape, dtype, layout, device, sharded_dram_mem_config)

section_title: 3.3.1. Compute and Output Readback on CQ 0, Input Writes on CQ 1
content:
# Dummy record an op event on CQ 0 since we wait on this first in the loop

op_event =ttnn.record_event(device, 0)

outputs = []

for iter in range(0, 2):

# Stall CQ 1 for the input tensor consumer (CQ 0) to signal it has finished so we can start overwriting the previous input tensor with the new one

ttnn.wait_for_event(1, op_event)

# Write the next input tensor on CQ 1

ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_dram_tensor, cq_id=1)

# Signal that the write has finished on CQ 1

write_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 1)

# Make CQ 0 stall until CQ 1 has signalled that the write has finished

ttnn.wait_for_event(0, write_event)

# Run the first operation of the model on CQ 0

input_l1_tensor = ttnn.to_memory_config(input_dram_tensor, sharded_l1_mem_config)

# Signal to the producer (CQ 1) that CQ 0 is finished with the input and it can be overwritten

op_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 0)

# Run the rest of the model and issue output readback on the default CQ (0)

output_tensor = run_model(input_l1_tensor)
 outputs.append(output_tensor.cpu(blocking=False))

# Final synchronize to wait for all outputs to be read to host since we used non-blocking reads

ttnn.synchronize_device(device)

```

section_title: 3.3.2. Compute on CQ 0, Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1
content:
Other configurations include one queue handling both writes and reads while the other dispatches programs, or both queues running different programs concurrently.

For using 2 command queues where **one is for reads and writes**, and **one for running programs**, we will need 2 persistent tensors, one for the input, and one for the output.

This configuration is optimal when **operation execution time dominates over read/write time**, allowing better overlap of I/O with computation.

!image-20251118-033614.png

Key characteristics:

- CQ 0 handles only operations
- CQ 1 handles both input writes and output readback
- More complex event synchronization (4 events needed)
- Better overlap when operation time >> I/O time

To efficiently overlap reads, writes, and operation execution between two command queues, **four synchronization events** are used.

section_title: 3.3.2. Compute on CQ 0, Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1
content:
To efficiently overlap reads, writes, and operation execution between two command queues, **four synchronization events** are used.

- The first event (`write_event`) signals when a write on command queue 1 is complete, allowing command queue 0 to proceed only after this write finishes.
- The second event (`first_op_event`) indicates when command queue 0 has consumed the input tensor, permitting command queue 1 to safely overwrite the input with new data.
- The third event (`last_op_event`) notifies when command queue 0 has completed its operations, so command queue 1 can read the resulting data.
- The fourth event (`read_event`) signals that command queue 1 has finished reading the output, enabling command queue 0 to overwrite it with new results.

This event-based coordination ensures correct sequencing and prevents data hazards during concurrent execution.

section_title: 3.3.2. Compute on CQ 0, Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1
content:
```
# This example uses 1 CQ for writing inputs and reading outputs (CQ 1), and one CQ for executing programs (CQ 0)

# `first_op_event` signals when the first operation is completed. This is the consumer of the input tensor so once this is completed, we can issue the next write

# `write_event` signals when input write is completed. This is used to signal that the input tensor can be read/consumed

# `last_op_event` signals when the last operation is completed. This is the producer of the otuput tensor so once this is completed, we can issue the next read

# `read_event` signals when output read is completed. This is used to signal that the output tensor can be overwritten

# Allocate our persistent input tensor
input_dram_tensor = ttnn.allocate_tensor_on_device(input_shape, input_dtype, input_layout, device, input_sharded_dram_mem_config)
# Allocate our persistent output tensor
output_dram_tensor = ttnn.allocate_tensor_on_device(output_shape, output_dtype, output_layout, device, output_sharded_dram_mem_config)

# Dummy record an op event on CQ 0 since we wait on this first in the loop
first_op_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 0)

section_title: 3.3.2. Compute on CQ 0, Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1
content:
# Dummy record an op event on CQ 0 since we wait on this first in the loop

first_op_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 0)

# Dummy record a read event on CQ 1 since we wait on this first in the loop

read_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 1)

outputs = []

# Stall CQ 1 for the input tensor consumer (CQ 0) to signal it has finished so we can start overwriting the previous input tensor with the new one

ttnn.wait_for_event(1, first_op_event)

# Write the next input tensor on CQ 1

ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_dram_tensor, cq_id=1)

section_title: 3.3.2. Compute on CQ 0, Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1
content:
# Signal that the write has finished on CQ 1

write_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 1)

for iter in range(0, 2):

# Make CQ 0 stall until CQ 1 has signalled that the write has finished

ttnn.wait_for_event(0, write_event)

# Run the first operation of the model on CQ 0

input_l1_tensor = ttnn.to_memory_config(input_dram_tensor, sharded_l1_mem_config)

# Signal to the producer (CQ 1) that CQ 0 is finished with the input and it can be overwritten

first_op_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 0)

# Run the rest of the model and issue output readback on the default CQ (0)

output_tensor = run_model(input_l1_tensor)

# Make CQ 0 stall until CQ 1 has signalled that the read has finished

ttnn.wait_for_event(0, read_event)

# Run the last operation of the model on CQ 0

output_dram_tensor = ttnn.reshard(output_tensor, output_sharded_dram_mem_config, output_dram_tensor)

# Signal that the model has finished on CQ 0

last_op_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 0)

section_title: 3.3.2. Compute on CQ 0, Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1
content:
# Stall CQ 1 for the input tensor consumer (CQ 0) to signal it has finished so we can start overwriting the previous input tensor with the new one

ttnn.wait_for_event(1, first_op_event)

# Write the next input tensor on CQ 1

ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_dram_tensor, cq_id=1)

# Signal that the write has finished on CQ 1

write_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 1)

# Make CQ 1 stall until CQ 0 has signalled that the model has finished

ttnn.wait_for_event(1, last_op_event)
 outputs.append(output_dram_tensor.cpu(blocking=False, cq_id=1))

# Signal that the read has finished on CQ 1

read_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 1)

section_title: 3.3.2. Compute on CQ 0, Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1
content:
# Make CQ 0 stall until CQ 1 has signalled that the write has finished

ttnn.wait_for_event(0, write_event)

# Run the first operation of the model on CQ 0

input_l1_tensor = ttnn.to_memory_config(input_dram_tensor, sharded_l1_mem_config)

# Signal to the producer (CQ 1) that CQ 0 is finished with the input and it can be overwritten

first_op_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 0)

# Run the rest of the model and issue output readback on the default CQ (0)

output_tensor = run_model(input_l1_tensor)

# Make CQ 0 stall until CQ 1 has signalled that the read has finished

ttnn.wait_for_event(0, read_event)

# Run the last operation of the model on CQ 0

output_dram_tensor = ttnn.reshard(output_tensor, output_sharded_dram_mem_config, output_dram_tensor)

# Signal that the model has finished on CQ 0

last_op_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 0)

# Make CQ 1 stall until CQ 0 has signalled that the model has finished

ttnn.wait_for_event(1, last_op_event)
outputs.append(output_dram_tensor.cpu(blocking=False, cq_id=1))

# Signal that the read has finished on CQ 1

read_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 1)

section_title: 3.3.2. Compute on CQ 0, Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1
content:
# Signal that the read has finished on CQ 1

read_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 1)

# Final synchronize to wait for all outputs to be read to host since we used non-blocking reads

ttnn.synchronize_device(device)

```
**NOTE**: Many current implementations use **Compute on CQ 0**, **Input Writes and Output Readback on CQ 1**.

In both strategies described in Section 3, an event (either `op_event` or `first_op_event`) is signaled when the input is no longer needed, allowing the input writer to overwrite it. However, if multiple input tensors exist - such as in models like SSR that require different input tensors at various stages—we must track these tensors and record the *allow-write* event immediately after the last input usage.

In this scenario, the model would need to capture and execute different parts of the trace separately. This approach, however, significantly increases the implementation complexity.

**Double Storage** acts as a **dual input buffer**, enabling concurrent data preparation and model computation.

Instead of waiting for the current inference to complete before loading the next input, Double Storage alternates between two pre-allocated buffers, allowing the next input to be written immediately after inference starts.

- One buffer holds data currently being processed by the model.
- The other buffer is available for the next input write.
- Once processing completes, the buffers swap roles.

This mechanism is conceptually similar to **double buffering** used in graphics pipelines — while one buffer is "in use," the other is "in preparation."

In this mechanism, two dedicated input buffers are alternately used — one for the **current model execution** and the other for **preloading the next input**. When the inference process starts on one buffer, the system simultaneously prepares data in the other. Once inference completes, the buffers swap roles.

```
# Input of n_th iter only be written after starting forward of n-1_th iter
ttnn.wait_for_event(1, start_compute_event)
ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_dram_tensor, cq_id=1)
input_storage.set(input_dram_tensor)
finish_write_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 1)

# Op CQ need to wait for write CQ to finish
ttnn.wait_for_event(0, finish_write_event)
start_compute_event = ttnn.record_event(device, 0)
output_tensor = run_model(input_storage.get())
```

The double input buffer requires Double Trace to capture 2 model run corresponding to 2 different input buffer addresses.

```
for _ in range(2):
 ttnn.synchronize_device(device)
 trace_idx = input_storage.write_idx
 ttnn.copy_host_to_device_tensor(host_tensor, input_dram_tensor, cq_id=1)
 input_storage.set(input_dram_tensor)
 ttnn.synchronize_device(device)
 trace_dict[f"trace_{trace_idx}"] = ttnn.begin_trace_capture(
 device, cq_id=0
 )
 output_tensor = run_model(input_storage.get())
 ttnn.end_trace_capture(
 device, trace_dict[f"trace_{trace_idx}"], cq_id=0
 )
 ttnn.synchronize_device(device)
```

The **Invocation Buffer** provides a lightweight **deferred execution and scheduling mechanism** that groups related operations (I/O, synchronization, event recording) into "blocks" and executes them in a controlled order.

It ensures proper synchronization between command queues — for example, guaranteeing that input writes finish before a compute queue starts processing.

```
with self.sched.add_block(priority=0) as defer:
 defer(lambda: ttnn.wait_for_event(self.io_cq_id, self.start_compute_event.pop(0)))
 defer(lambda: self.input_storage.set(input_tensor))
 defer(lambda: self._record_event("finish_write_event", self.io_cq_id))
```

!image-20251118-030829.png

After defining the sequence, calling `self.sched.flush()` executes the deferred functions in order of priority.

!image-20251118-030931.png

In the `SSRPerformanceRunner`, the `InvocationBuffer` ensures:

- Proper **ordering** between I/O operations and computation.
- Asynchronous **scheduling** of post-processing (e.g., copying outputs, logging, visualization).
- Fine-grained control over pipeline timing without blocking the main thread.

- **Flexible scheduling:** Developers can group related operations with priorities.
- **Improved readability:** Avoids deeply nested synchronization logic.
- **Non-blocking execution:** Operations are enqueued and flushed asynchronously.

---

When used together:

- **Double Storage** manages alternating input/output buffers.
- **Invocation Buffer** coordinates when each buffer's operations (write, read, compute) should occur.

1. **CQ1** writes new input into the *next* Double Storage buffer.
2. **CQ0** runs inference on the *current* buffer.
3. Once compute finishes, **CQ1** reads back the output while **CQ0** begins the next inference.

This tightly overlapped execution maximizes throughput and ensures continuous utilization of compute and I/O resources.

Metal Trace is used to reduce the operation-to-operation (op-to-op) gap during model execution on the TT device.

In a normal execution flow, each operation is launched sequentially from the host (Python), which introduces overhead between operations. Metal Trace solves this problem by capturing the sequence of device operations once and replaying them directly on the device, eliminating most host scheduling overhead.

The workflow consists of three main stages:

1. Compile and preprocess weights
2. Capture trace
3. Execute trace

---

Before capturing the trace, the model must be executed once. This initial execution serves two purposes:

- Trigger kernel compilation
- Preprocess Conv2D weights and bias for device execution

During this stage, Conv2D weights are converted into adevice-optimized format.

Example:

```
out , (H_out, W_out), (self.stem_weight, self.stem_bias) = ttnn.conv2d(
 input_tensor = x,
 weight_tensor = self.stem_weight,
 bias_tensor = self.stem_bias,
 device = self.device,
 in_channels = 3,
 out_channels = 64,
 batch_size = N,
 input_height = H,
 input_width = W,
 kernel_size = [7,7],
 stride = [2,2],
 padding = [3,3],
 dtype = ttnn.bfloat16,
 conv_config = self.conv2d_cfg["stem.conv"],
 return_output_dim = True,
 slice_config = ttnn.Conv2dL1FullSliceConfig,
 compute_config = self.compute_config,
 return_weights_and_bias = True,
)
```

The flag:

```
return_weights_and_bias = True
```

returns the processed Conv2D weights and bias tensors.

These tensors are already transformed into a format suitable for device execution (e.g., tilized and layout-optimized).

The returned tensors are stored back into the model:

```
self.stem_weight
self.stem_bias
```

This allows the model to reuse the processed weights in subsequent executions without repeating the preprocessing step.

---

section_title: 3. Why Preprocessed Weights Must Be Stored
content:
When capturing a Metal Trace, **host-device read/write operations are not allowed**.

During trace capture:

- The device execution graph is recorded.
- No additional memory transfers or host preprocessing can occur.

Therefore, all required tensors (including Conv2D weights) must already exist in the correct device format before the trace capture begins.

The workflow becomes:

```
Original Torch Weights
 ↓
Conv2D Preprocess (first run)
 ↓
Processed Device Weights
 ↓
Stored inside model
 ↓
Used during Trace Capture
```

Without storing these processed weights, the system would need to reprocess them during trace capture, which is not permitted.

---

After the compilation run, the execution graph of the model is captured.

Trace capture records all device operations executed during the model forward pass.

Example:

```
tid = ttnn.begin_trace_capture(device, cq_id=0)

output = model(capture_trace_input)

ttnn.end_trace_capture(device, tid, cq_id=0)
```

During this stage:

- All device operations are recorded
- The host no longer schedules individual operations
- The trace graph is stored in device memory

---

Once the trace is captured, inference can be executed by replaying the recorded operations.

Example:

```
ttnn.execute_trace(device, tid, cq_id=0)
```

This eliminates most of theop-to-op gap.

---

Using Metal Trace significantly improves inference throughput.

```
EXECUTE TRACE TIME: 0.0237 s
FPS: 337
```

This improvement is achieved by removing host scheduling overhead.

---

The overall workflow is:

```
Load Torch Model
 ↓
Prepare Input
 ↓
First Model Run (Compile + Weight Preprocess)
 ↓
Store Processed Conv Weights
 ↓
Begin Trace Capture
 ↓
Run Model (Record Ops)
 ↓
End Trace Capture
 ↓
Execute Trace for Inference
```

---

---
Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) training and inference efficiency have emerged as a core bottleneck in making MLLMs more accessible and scalable.

The paper presents MiniCPM-V 4.5, an 8B-parameter model optimized for efficiency and performance with three core improvements:

- **Model architecture:** a unified 3D-Resampler model architecture for highly compact encoding over images and videos.
- **Data strategy:** a unified learning paradigm for document knowledge and text recognition that avoid heavy data engineering.
- **Training method:** a hybrid reinforcement learning strategy for proficiency in both short and long reasoning modes.

In OpenCompass evaluation show that MiniCPM-V 4.5 surpasses:

- Widely used proprietary models such as GPT-4o-latest.
- Significantly larger open-source models such as Qwen2.5-VL 72B.

On the widely adopted VideoMME benchmark, MiniCPM-V 4.5:

- Achieves state-of-the-art performance among models under 30B size.
- 46.7% GPU memory cost.
- 8.7% inference time of Qwen2.5-VL 7B

---
The efficiency problem is decomposed into three core aspects.

A primary efficiency bottleneck in MLLMs comes from the large number of visual tokens for high-resolution image encoding, which brings heavy computation overhead for visual encoders and LLMs. The problem is even exacerbated in video understanding.

For example, processing a video:

- Duration: 6-second
- Resolution: 448x448
- FPS: 2-FPS

requires:

- 1536 visual tokens for Qwen2.5-VL.
- 3072 visual tokens for InternVL3.

The paper introduces Unified 3D-Resampler for Compact Image and Video Encoding:

- Exhibit 2D-Resamplers with high compression rates for high-resolution images.
- This version extends the 2D-Resampler to a 3D-Resampler that jointly compresses spatial-temporal information for videos.
Encode a video with the above spec into only 128 visual tokens.

Modern MLLMs rely on high-quality multimodal knowledge from documents like scientific papers and textbooks stored as PDFs.

Existing methods need external parsing tools to convert documents into interleaved image-text sequences for training. These tools often fail on complex layouts, causing:

- Errors in knowledge learning.
- Extensive data engineering to fix failures.

The paper introduces Unified Learning Paradigm for Document Knowledge and OCR:

- Enable the model to acquire knowledge directly from documents, eliminating the need for external parser.
- Corrupting text regions in documents with varying noise levels and asking the model to reconstruct the text.
The model learns to adaptively and properly switch between accurate text recognition (when text is roughly visible) and multimodal context-based knowledge reasoning (when text is heavily corrupted).

Reinforcement Learning has shown promise in improving complex reasoning capabilities by enabling a step-by-step explicit thinking process before providing the final answer.

However, most existing thinking models produce excessively long outputs, inducing poor efficiency in both training and inference.

The paper introduces Hybrid Strategy for Post-Training:

- Unlike prior models that optimize for a single long reasoning mode, the paper develop a hybrid RL post-training strategy to support both short reasoning mode for efficient usage and long reasoning mode for complex tasks.
- Randomly alternate between the two modes during the rollout process for joint optimization.

- Enables flexible control over the short and long reasoning modes.
- Allows for mutual performance enhancement.
- Can achieve better reasoning performance with fewer training samples for both modes.

---

The Processor in MiniCPM-V is responsible for converting raw multimodal inputs (images or videos) into a structured, model-ready representation that bridges vision data and the language model. It performs input normalization, aspect ratio-aware resizing, optional high-resolution image slicing, and temporal grouping for video frames, while ensuring all inputs are aligned to the vision encoder's patch size.

**Image Processing**

To handle high-resolution images in any aspect ratio, they adopt the LLaVA UHD image partitioning strategy.

!image-20260113-030103.png

For each image, they estimate the ideal number of slices from the input resolution and select a partitioning strategy so each slice's resolution closely matches the visual encoder's original pre-training resolution.

**Example 1 - Image slicing:**

**Given**

Original:

```
W = 1234
H = 567
```

**Step 0: Slicing grid**

Step 0.0: Decide whether slicing is needed

Compute area ratio

```
original area = 1234 x 567 = 699,678
reference area = 448 x 448 = 200,704
ratio = 699,678 / 200,704 ~ 3.49
```

Estimated number of slices:

```
multiple = ceil(3.49) = 4
```

Since:

```
multiple > 1
```

=> **Image will be sliced**

Step 0.1: Choose slicing grid (`get_sliced_grid` result)

Aspect ratio:

```
r = 1234 / 567 ~ 2.176
log(r) ~ 0.777
```

Candidate slice counts:

```
[3, 4, 5]
```

Factor grids:

- 3 → (1×3), (3×1)
- 4 → (1×4), (2×2), (4×1)
- 5 → (1×5), (5×1)

Best grid matching aspect ratio ~ 2.176 is:

```
grid = (4, 1)
```

So the image is sliced into:

```
4 columns x 1 row = 4 slices
```

**Step 1 — Compute refined full-image size (**`get_refine_size`**)**

Step 1.1 — Make original size divisible by grid

```
refine_width = ensure_divide(1234, 4) = 1232
refine_height = ensure_divide(567, 1) = 567
```

Step 1.2 — Compute per-slice size before scaling

```
slice_width = 1232 / 4 = 308
slice_height = 567 / 1 = 567
```

So each slice initially is:

```
308 x 567
```

**Step 2: Resize each slice using** `find_best_resize`

Step 2.1: Resize the slice to make its area close to the reference area (448x448).

This technique is illustrated in example 2.

After resize:

```
(330, 608)
```

Step 2.2 — Patch alignment

Width:

```
330 / 14 = 23.57 → 23 × 14 = 322
```

Height:

```
608 / 14 = 43.43 → 43 × 14 = 602
```

So final per-slice size:

```
322 x 602
```

**Step 3: Compute refined full-image size**

```
refine_width = 322 × 4 = 1288
refine_height = 602 × 1 = 602
```

Final refined image size:

```
1288 x 602
```

**Step 4: Patch grid & patch count (per slice)**

Patch grid

```
Width patches: 322 / 14 = 23
Height patches: 602 / 14 = 43
```

Total patches per slice

```
22 × 43 = 989 patches
```

**Step 5: Total visual units and patches**

Number of slices:

```
4
```

Total patches across all slices:

```
4 x 989 = 3956 patches
```

These will later be compressed by the Resampler as:

```
4 slices -> 4 x 64 = 256 visual tokens
```

**Final summary (same style as your example)**

```
1234 x 567
-> sliced into 4 x 1 grid
-> refined to 1288 x 602
-> each slice: 322 x 602
-> patch grid per slice: 23 x 43
-> 989 patches per slice
-> 4 slices -> 64 tokens = 256 visual tokens
```

**Example 2 - Image resizing:**

**Given**

- Original: W=345, H=234
- `scale_resolution = 448`
- `patch_size = 14`

**Step 1: Resize target**

So we compute a new size with:

- same aspect ratio
- area ~ 448²

**Step 2: Aspect ratio**

```
r = W / H
 = 345 / 234
 = 1.474358974
```

**Step 3: Compute new height and width (area-normalized)**

```
height = int(448 / sqrt(r)) = 369
width = int(height * r) = 544
```

So before patch-alignment:

```
(544, 369)
```

**Step 4: Patch alignment to multiples of 14**

```
width = max(round(544 / 14) * 14, 14) = 532
height = max(round(369 / 14) * 14, 14) = 364
```

Final result:

```
best_resize = (532, 364)
```

**Step 5 — Patch grid & patch count**

Patch grid

- Width patches: `532 / 14 = 38`
- Height patches: `364 / 14 = 26`

Total patches: `38 * 26 = 988`

So:

```
345×234 -> 532×364 -> 988 patches
```

**Video Processing**

Videos consist of frames, which are processed like images.

To handle the significant redundancy in video data, they employ a joint spatial temporal compression strategy for higher compression rates.

For each video, they first split it into packages along the temporal dimension, where each package contains adjacent frames. Intuitively, the video frames within the same package typically share highly redundant visual information, which can be identified and compressed when jointly modeled.

The number of temporal packages (clips) is input-dependent. It depends on:

- number of frames
- sampling FPS
- chosen frames-per-clip (valid range: 1–6)
- how `temporal_ids` is constructed upstream

The vision encoder is responsible for transforming processed image or video inputs into high-level visual representations that can be efficiently consumed by the language model. MiniCPM-V adopts a **Vision Transformer (ViT)-based encoder**, instantiated as a **NaViT-style SigLIP encoder**, which supports variable-resolution and arbitrary aspect-ratio inputs.

**Patch Embedding**

After preprocessing and optional slicing, each image slice (or video frame slice) is converted into non-overlapping patches using a fixed patch size (14x14). Each patch is linearly projected into a visual embedding space, forming a sequence of patch tokens. This patch-based representation enables flexible handling of different image sizes while preserving spatial locality.

**Positional Encoding**

To encode spatial structure, the encoder applies 2D positional embeddings corresponding to the patch grid coordinates of pretrained model.

Example:

Split image into patches

!image-20260113-174057.png

Positions

Example:

Split image into patches

!image-20260113-174057.png

Positions

!image-20260113-174021.png

Positions: 0 → 1 → 2 → … → 5 → 12 → 13 → … → 22 → 23 → 30 → 31 → … → 34 → 35

Then apply a learnable embedding layer.

**Transformer Encoding**

The patch embeddings are processed by a stack of Transformer layers, each consisting of multi-head self-attention and feed-forward (MLP) blocks. The encoder uses smooth nonlinear activations (e.g., GELU-Tanh) and Layer Normalization to ensure stable training and effective feature extraction across varying resolutions and modalities.

**Output Representations**

**Output Representations**

The encoder outputs a sequence of high-dimensional patch features for each image slice or video clip. These features retain rich spatial (and temporal, for video) information but are still too long to be directly consumed by the language model. Therefore, the encoder outputs are subsequently passed to a **Resampler module**, which compresses the variable-length patch sequences into a fixed number of visual tokens.

!image-20260114-032743.png

**2D-Resample**

The image tokens are then augmented with 2D spatial positional embeddings to produce a fixed-length sequence for each slice through cross-attention using learnable queries. Most existing MLLMs adopt MLP and pixel un-shuffle operation for visual compression, and typically require visual 256 tokens for encoding a 448×448 image.

Leveraging the flexibility of re-sampler architecture, by choosing a small number of query tokens, MiniCPM-V can achieve a significantly higher compression rate for visual tokens (e.g., 64 tokens for a 448×448 image) while maintaining good performance.

**3D-Resample**

To this end, they resample the frame features from the visual encoder in each package into a fixed-length feature sequence through cross attention. They augment the learnable queries with both 2D spatial positional embedding, as used in image encoding, and temporal positional embedding. The final video representation is obtained by concatenating the token sequences from all packages.

They sample at most 1080 frames per video at a maximum frame rate of 10 FPS. During training, the package size and frame rate are randomly augmented to improve robustness. This design also provides flexibility at inference time, allowing these hyperparameters to be adjusted to meet the demands of diverse scenarios and devices.

To summarize this section:

- The **Processor** normalizes and structures raw images and videos, handling aspect ratios, resolutions, slicing and temporal grouping.
- The **Vision Encoder** (NaViT-style SigLIP) converts these inputs into rich visual representations, using patch embeddings, positional encodings, and Transformer layers.
- The **Unified 3D-Resampler** then compresses these long sequences into a **small, fixed number of visual tokens** for both images and videos.

Together, these components enable MiniCPM‑V to:

- Efficiently process **high-resolution, arbitrary-aspect-ratio images**,
- Compress **redundant video frames** in space and time, and
- Provide the language model with compact, informative visual tokens.

---

This section empirically evaluate the performance of MiniCPM-V 4.5, and the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

- **STEM** includes mathematics and science-oriented benchmarks such as MMMU, Math Vista, AI2D, MathVerse, LogicVista, and EMMA, designed to evaluate logical reasoning, mathematical problem-solving, and scientific understanding capabilities.
- **Document, OCR & Chart** covers OCR-related tasks through OCRBench, ChartQA, TextVQA, DocVQA, and OmniDocBench, testing ability to extract, interpret, and reason about textual information in various visual contexts, including documents and charts.
- **Hallucination** evaluates model reliability through HallusionBench, ObjHalBench, and MMHal-Bench, measuring the tendency to generate false or inconsistent information.
- **Multi-Image & Real-World & Instruction Following** includes Mantis, MMT-Bench, RealWorldQA, and MM-IFEval, assessing performance on complex scenarios involving multiple images, real-world understanding, and instruction following.
- **Video Understanding** encompasses Video-MME, LVBench, MLVU, LongVideoBench, MotionBench, and FavorBench, evaluating temporal reason ing and dynamic visual comprehension across various video tasks.

- **Comprehensive Multimodal Understanding** includes benchmarks such as OpenCompass, MMVet, MMStar, MME, and MMBench V1.1, which assess general vision-language comprehension across diverse task types. Within the OpenCompass average, they use the long reasoning mode for 5 benchmarks, including MMStar, MMVet, HallusionBench, MathVista, and MMMU.

!image-20251229-031303.png

For more detail information of MiniCPM-V, refer to this page: https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/x/sIdzDw

This report covers the core architecture and high-level concepts of MiniCPM-o 2.6, focusing on aspects relevant to optimizing TTNN and TT-METAL. It addresses model structure and computational characteristics but excludes the training procedure. For details on the (post-)training process, see the Appendix below.
MiniCPM-o 2.6 accepts image, video, text, and audio inputs and delivers high-quality text and speech outputs in a fully end-to-end manner.

- Despite its 8B parameter scale, the model reaches or exceeds the performance of several larger proprietary systems on standard visual benchmarks, includes single-image perception tasks as well as multi-image and short video understanding, suggesting that the model's visual tokenization and fusion mechanisms are effective at compressing high-resolution visual information without losing spatial- and/or temporal- context.
- The model also displays non-trivial in-context learning behavior in visual tasks, indicating that its cross-modal representations retain sufficient flexibility for implicit adaptation.

- MiniCPM-o 2.6 enables real-time bilingual speech interaction in **English** and **Chinese** with configurable voices. Empirically, it outperforms GPT-4o-realtime on established audio understanding metrics, including ASR and AST.
- Evaluations in both semantic and acoustic dimensions within open-source settings indicate that the model maintains stable alignment between linguistic content and acoustic attributes while supporting controllable parameters such as speaking rate, style, and emotion.
- It also supports end-to-end voice cloning and role-conditioned conversation, allowing systematic probing of its speech synthesis generalization capabilities.

- The model operates on continuous audio and video streams without requiring query boundaries, which provides a controlled setting for measuring streaming consistency, latency, and long-context stability.
- Exceeds the performance of GPT-4o-202408 and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, suggesting that its streaming pathway maintains temporal coherence and avoids the drift often observed in models not explicitly optimized for persistent multimodal input.

- The model handles images with arbitrary aspect ratios up to 1.8 million pixels, showing reliable and safe visual behavior
- Its support for more than 30 languages enables cross-lingual VQA and OCR studies without task-specific tuning.

Processing a 1.8-megapixel image requires only 640 tokens, delivering a significant efficiency advantage in both throughput and latency.

!image-20251118-035417.png

!image-20251118-040039.png

**Overview**: The input audio is encoded as chunks, each with its own speech encoding. The Omni-Modality Streaming backbone processes audio, visual, and text as text tokens. If the user requires audio output, these text tokens combine with latent variables to decode the output audio.

Most traditional voice conversation models use an ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition) - LLM - TTS (Text-to-Speech) pipeline, causing: (1) compounding errors and (2) loss of fine-grained details like users' complex emotions and environmental sounds.

- The input audio is fed into a Whisper encoder and then compress the token representations for efficient understanding. By default, Whisper encoder produces 50 tokens for a 1s audio.
- For better token density and computation efficiency, audio tokens are further compressed before feeding into the LLM backbone. Experimental results show that the information loss can be omitted when compressing the encoding token rate from 50 token/s to 25 token/s.

- MiniCPM-o 2.6 uses a lightweight auto-regressive speech decoder initialized from ChatTTS, which jointly models speech embedding, text tokens and audio tokens. They serve as conditions to generate the final mel spectrogram.
- The audio encoder and LLM backbone is bridged via encoded audio representations (i.e., no ASR is performed). The LLM backbone and speech decoder are bridged in a hybrid fashion: (1) The dense hidden representations (speech embedding) `H` is a latent feature, which can control the voice, emotion, accent, and other fine-grained speech-centric features. `H` ensure that speech supervision can be back-propagated to full model parameters and (2) The text connection offers strong semantic control and reduces training data requirements.

!image-20251118-091551.png

- First, divide images into slices, where each slice better **matches ViT's pre-training setting** in terms of resolution and aspect ratio. For computation efficiency, support at most 9 slices for each image.
- Second, feed each slice and the original image into **Shared Vision Transformer** to get the visual feature.
- Finally, apply a perceiver resampler layer MiniCPM-V to compress each variable-sized image slice into 64 visual tokens.

**Overview:** We need only partial text to decode each segment into an audio signal that outputs speech. The attention mechanism must focus on `H` to control voice settings (tone, accent, speed), previous audio tokens to maintain consistency between speech chunks and determine the next output, and the presented text for TTS conversion.

To enable online streaming, split the input audio into chunks of fixed audio tokens representing one second. During audio encoding, encode each chunk using causal attention.

!image-20251118-092300.png

- A **streaming** text-to-speech model should start generating and playing audio as soon as partial text is available. We **prefill a fixed number of text tokens per time** (a chunk size of `n`) and **the decoder immediately decodes a fixed number of audio tokens** (a chunk size of `m`). Then, repeat for the next text and audio chunks. By controlling the chunk-based attention mask between text and audio tokens, we enable partial audio output to be generated (and played) in a live streaming fashion.
- Note that the alignment between text chunks and their corresponding audio chunks is not exact, so a larger budget for text token chunk size is preserved in practice. `N`, `n`, and `m` are hyperparameters. In practice, we use N=300, n=10, m=50 to achieve a balance between performance and inference speed.
- What if at N=300, it do not generate <EOS>? It will apply sliding window technique. In other word, some of previous chunks of text and TTS result will be reused for next iteration until it generates <EOS>.

- In each second, sample one frame from the input visual stream (1FPS to avoid redundancy information), and encode it with adaptive high-resolution encoding strategy. Finally, the encoded features are fed into the LLM backbone.
- To strike a balance between efficiency and performance, we devise a simple mixed-resolution encoding strategy. Specifically, only encode the last frame by the end of the user query with high resolution (e.g., 1344 x 896), while keeping other frames in moderate resolution (e.g., 448 x 448).

The purpose of OTDM is to achieve a temporal-aligned streaming understanding of different modalities:

- First, we synchronize the information flows from each modality along a shared timeline.
- Then, divide the stream of each modality into small chunks (1s for a chunk), and group all modality information within the same-second chunk into a compact sequence unit.
- Finally, this multimodal unit is then sequentially processed by the LLM backbone over time.

Note that MiniCPM-o 2.6 continuously processes multimodal input streams **independent of user queries.** In other words**,** it processes input in real time, even without active user questions. The user prompt only triggers the response. This enables the model to handle tasks requiring temporal memory and reasoning (e.g., "The ball is inside which cup", "Which words did I just erase?") and supports multi-turn live streaming conversation.

An **Audio System Prompt (ASP)** allows users to control the model's vocal attributes in an acoustic way, ensuring the output voice aligns appropriately with user intention. We can also perform description-based voice creation, by leaving the ASP empty, and instruct the model to create a new voice (e.g., "Speak like a male charming superstar, radiating confidence and style in every word").

!image-20251119-040842.png

1. **Potentially Unstable Speech Output.** The multimodal system prompt allows for more flexibility over voice control, enabling many fun features. However, it also presents more challenges to the stability of speech output. Compared with traditional speech-to-speech models that memorize a single voice in full parameters, MiniCPM-o 2.6 needs to distill and clone the voice from a multimodal system prompt. As a result, the speech generation can sometimes be flawed with noisy backgrounds and meaningless sounds.
2. **Long Speech Generation.** Currently, the model do not natively support generating speech longer than 45 seconds. We use a sliding-window method to generate longer speech. However, the gap between training and sliding window inference can lead to failure cases during long speech generation.
3. **Limited Multimodal Live Streaming Capability.** As an experimental feature, multimodal live streaming is still limited in perception, understanding and reasoning capabilities.

MobileNetv1 is a first class of MobileNet model. This is an image classification model build for mobile and embedded vision applications.

MobileNets are based on a streamlined architecture that uses **depthwise separable convolutions** to build light weight deep neural networks.

The MobileNet model is based on **depthwise separable convolutions** which is a form of factorized convolutions which factorize a standard convolution into a **depthwise convolution** and a 1×1 convolution called a **pointwise convolution**.

!image-20260116-023728.png

- The depthwise convolution applies a single filter to each input channel. The pointwise convolution then applies a 1×1 convolution to combine the outputs the depthwise convolution.
The depthwise separable convolution splits this into two layers, a separate layer for filtering and a separate layer for combining.
- A standard convolution applies both filters and combines inputs into a new set of outputs in one step.

- **Valid but suboptimal config:**

 - Shard shape: `(96, 128)`
 - Number of cores: `33`
 - Padding: last shard contains zero tiles

 - 3.4.2 [Example patterns](#ModelCommonBottleneckPatterns-Examplepatterns.1)
 - 3.4.3 [Solutions](#ModelCommonBottleneckPatterns-Solutions.2)
 - 3.5 [Op Fusing](#ModelCommonBottleneckPatterns-OpFusing)
 - 3.6 [Defragmentation](#ModelCommonBottleneckPatterns-Defragmentation)
 - 3.7 [Workarounds](#ModelCommonBottleneckPatterns-Workarounds)
 - 3.8 [Others (Low Priority)](#ModelCommonBottleneckPatterns-Others(LowPriority))
- 4 [Appendix](#ModelCommonBottleneckPatterns-Appendix)
 - 4.1 [Low-priority Unsupported Ops](#ModelCommonBottleneckPatterns-Low-priorityUnsupportedOps)

Summarize and categorize common bottleneck patterns for 8 released models including:

- Resnet50: ResNet50 Model Decomposition
- YOLOv8: YOLOv8 Model Decomposition
- ViT: ViT Model Decomposition
- SSR-net: SSR-net Model Decomposition
- Llama: Llama 3.x
- Qwen: Qwen2.5 VL
- OFT: OFT Model Decomposition
- PDL: PDL Model Decomposition

---

> These ops are prerequisite to support in `TT-MLIR`, otherwise compiler **cannot** map them to lower IR like TTIR/TTNN → **Compiler time error**.

| **No.** | **Ops** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | `ttnn.bos_setitem` (Continuous scatter) | SSR-net, custom op |
| 2 | `ttnn.bos_setitem`/ `ttnn.scatter` (Indexing scatter) | SSR-net |

---
Classified by TT-MLIR compiler categories.
Let **alone** the operation at runtime, the input/output tensor is **too large** that it cannot fit on L1 increasing **DRAM pressure**.

- **Resnet50** (`224x224xbf16`): `Conv1 → Relu → Maxpool2d` (ResNet50 Pattern 1
- **SSR** (`6x640x384xbf16`): in Resnet50, most of layers in this module cannot fit inputs on L1.
- **OFT-net** (`7x160x160x4x2xbf16`): `grid_sample` operation at OFT module requires processing 16 slices of input locations (OFT Module Patterns.
- **ViT**: Attention Score Materialization (Vit Pattern 1
- **Qwen2.5 VL:**

 - Dynamic Large Input (Qwen2.5 VL Pattern 1
 - RoPE with dynamic shape allocation, tensor can be too large that require DRAM spill (Qwen2.5 VL Pattern 4

- Apply the **batch tilizing** or batch splitting, see SSR Resnet50 Bottleneck Patterns
- **Datatype conversion** or **quantization**: convert to `bfloat8_b` or lower precision.
- Using in-placed op if possible (see Others - Low Priority
- **Op config-based optimization:**

 - For `ttnn.conv2d` with `stride > 1`, `kernel_size > 1`: applying "weight folding" technique. (See ResNet50 Model Decomposition
 - For `ttnn.conv2d` or `ttnn.maxpool2d`: use slice config for better execution performance.

---

Join Ops introduce many intermediate tensors that are stalling and might occupy L1 memory usage for down streaming ops. This can lead to:

- **Long-lived tensors**
- **L1** **Fragmentation** risk and static circular buffer clash might occur.

We don't have a way to export this macro.

- **Resnet50:** Residual connection introduces `add` op as a joint point (Resnet50 Residual
- **YOLOv8:** Split-concat pattern (YOLOv8 pattern 1, 2
- **Llama 3.x:** fork-join patterns create repeated L1 tensor peak usage (Llama Transformer Blocks Residuals
- **Qwen2.5 VL:** fork-join matmul (Qwen2.5 VL pattern 6
- **OFT-net:** OFT module with long-lived tensors at grid sample op (OFT Module Patterns

- Require a good **life-time aware memory planning** (which to spill back DRAM, which to keep on L1) toward the global optimal performance.

---

Layout transformation between 2 consecutive ops sometimes causing large memory movement and breaking L1 residency, leading to significant performance loss.

- Re-sharding between L1 sharding layouts
- L1 Sharding to L1 Interleaved
- L1 Interleaved to L1 Sharding

**Reasons:** different input layouts required for different operations

- **Resnet50:**

- Layout Conversion from NCHW → NHWC for first conv7x7 (Resnet50 layout conversion because `ttnn.conv2d` requires NHWC format while input is NCHW.
 - Reshape triggers layout changes from `block_sharded, L1, 4x4` to `width_sharded, L1, 1x16` (Resnet50 Reshape before FC.
- **YOLOv8:** Split-concat pattern and heavy reshape transformation (YOLOv8 pattern 2, 3
- **ViT:** Sharding / Memory-Config Churn Inside Each Encoder Block (ViT Pattern 2
- **Llama 3.x:** model must constantly change its layout to suit next op (Llama Layout Conversions

- If layout conversion is **between ops**: develop a **global-aware resharding optimization** mechanism based on some cost function or heuristics.
- If layout conversion is **first op in the model** (e.g. Resnet50)

 - Modify compiler to support flexible input layout. See Resnet50 Support Flexible Input Layout.
 - Introduce fused op that absorbs the layout conversion internally (worst case since requires too many efforts to develop). See Resnet50 Dedicated Fused Op.

---

> **Note:** Only listed out patterns that **not supported yet** in TT-MLIR.

| **No.** | **Patterns** | **Op** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | `x * ttnn.sigmoid(x)` | `ttnn.silu(x)` | **YOLOv8** |
| 2 | Complex pattern | `ttnn.grid_sample`/ `ttnn.bos_grid_sample` | **SSR-net****,** **OFT** |
| 3 | Complex pattern | `ttnn.bos_deformable_attention` / `ttnn.bos_ssr_deformable_attention` | **SSR-net** |
| 4 | Complex pattern | `ttnn.transformer.chunked_scaled_dot_product_attention` | **Qwen2.5 VL** |
| 5 | `(x * rsqrt(mean(x^2) + epsilon)) * gamma` | `RMSNormOp` | **Qwen2.5 VL** |

| 5 | `(x * rsqrt(mean(x^2) + epsilon)) * gamma` | `RMSNormOp` | **Qwen2.5 VL** |
| 6 | `Linear -> Silu -> Linear -> Multiply -> Linear` | `SwiGLU` | **Llama 3.x****,** **Qwen2.5 VL** |
| 7 | `(q * cos) + (rotate_half(q) * sin)` | `ttnn.experimental.rotary_embedding_llama` | **Llama 3.x**, support for `rotary_embedding` but not for `rotary_embedding_llama` |
| 8 | `xq, xk, xv = self.wq(x), self.wk(x), self.wv(x)` | `xqkv_fused = ttnn.linear(x, wqkv, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16)` | **Llama 3.x****,** combine the QKV projection weights into a single tensor and perform standard `ttnn.linear` |

| 9 | `xq = xq.view(bsz, seqlen, self.n_local_heads, self.head_dim)` | `Q, K, V = ttnn.experimental.nlp_create_qkv_heads(xqkv_fused, num_heads=n_q_heads, num_kv_heads=n_kv_heads, transpose_k_heads=False)` | **Llama 3.x**, reshape Q, K, V to match the expected input shape for scaled dot product attention. Split the fused QKV tensor into individual Q, K, V tensors using a custom optimized TM OP, `ttnn.experimental.nlp_create_qkv_heads` |
| | | | |

---

Open topic to discuss, current available solution is `ReallocationAnalysis`: https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/x/4IB0EQ

!image-20260226-061332.png

---

| **No.** | **Patterns** | **Workaround** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | - Device: BOS A0/B0 ``` ttnn.to_layout(input=DRAM_SHARDED, output=L1_SHARDED) # or ttnn.to_layout(input=L1_SHARDED, output=DRAM_SHARDED) ``` | `ttnn.bos_reshard` | DRAM and L1 alignment differences on A0/B0, `ttnn.reshard` does not handle that leads to functionally failed. |
| 2 | `ttnn.slice_static`/ `ttnn.Tensor.getitem` | `ttnn.bos_getitem` | SSR-net, OFT. Custom op that optimized than `ttnn.slice_static` in runtime |
| 3 | `ttnn.nonzero` with known output size | `ttnn.bos_nonzero` | Custom op applied for SSR-net, with the novel optimization, see in SSR-net Model Decomposition |
| 4 | `ttnn.repeat_interleave(repeats_factor=1)` | Should be removed | Llama 3.x, `ttnn.repeat_interleave` uses `repeats_factor` = 1 → redundant |
| | | | |

---

- **In-placed Ops:** Below are **nice-to-have**, they help to reduce memory usage, avoid fragmentation.

| **No.** | **Unsupported Ops** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | `ttnn.softmax_in_place` | ViT |
| 2 | `ttnn.mul_` | ViT, OFT |
| 3 | `ttnn.add_` | SSR-net, OFT |
| 4 | `ttnn.logical_and_` | SSR-net |
| 5 | `ttnn.multiply_` | SSR-net, OFT |
| 6 | `ttnn.subtract_` | SSR-net, OFT |

- **Dynamic allocation:** Some ops have dynamic allocation, that is output shape only known at runtime:

- **SSR BEV-mask:** a novel optimization method already proposed (see SSR-net Model Decomposition.
 - **Qwen2.5 VL:** this model handles variable sequence lengths at runtime through RoPE's dynamic slicing mechanism (see Qwen2.5 VL pattern 4 - RoPE.
- **KV cache:** Compiler does not use KV Cache for Llama3.x (Llama3.x Compiler
- **TTNN unsupported ops:** Conv3d for Qwen2.5 VL (Ref
- **Control Flow:** SSR implementation contains dynamic control flow (see: SSR-net Control Flow; **must do more research** (MLIR Language Reference to identify:

- Which part in the compiler handle such kind of control flow?
 - How to handle if compiler has not supported?

---

**For multi-device:**

| | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **No.** | **TTNN Op** | **Notes** |
| 1 | `ttnn.experimental.all_gather_matmul` | Llama 3. |
| 2 | `ttnn.get_device_tensors` | Llama 3. |
| 3 | `ttnn.rms_norm_pre_all_gather` | Llama 3. |
| 4 | `ttnn.rms_norm_post_all_gather` | Llama 3. |

**(Maybe) not need to support**:

| **No.** | **TTNN Op** | **Replaced by (already supported in TT-MLIR)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | `ttnn.experimental.view` | `ttnn.reshape` |
| 2 | `ttnn.stack` | `ttnn.unsqueeze` and `ttnn.concat` |
| 3 | `ttnn.unsqueeze`/ `ttnn.squeeze` | `ttnn.reshape` |
| 4 | `ttnn.create_sharded_memory_config` | Utility op |
| 5 | `ttnn.Tensor.getitem` | `ttnn.slice` (?) |
| 6 | `ttnn.experimental.fast_reduce_nc` | `ttnn.reduce` |
| 7 | `ttnn.outer` | `ttnn.reshape` and `ttnn.matmul` |
| 8 | `ttnn.split` | `ttnn.Tensor.getitem`/ `ttnn.slice` |
| 9 | `ttnn.full_like` | `ttnn.full` |
| 10 | `ttnn.clone` | - |

This page analyzes the ViT model from a runtime perspective.

It covers:

- **Full operation list** – ops used, call counts, and their locations in the model.
- **Runtime behavior per op** – execution details at the TTNN / TT-Metal level (including tensor layout, memory configurations and memory usage.

- 1 [Introduction](#ModelDevelopment-Introduction)
- 2 [Model development workflow](#ModelDevelopment-Modeldevelopmentworkflow)
- 3 [Model Development Structure](#ModelDevelopment-ModelDevelopmentStructure)

This document describes the process of **implement a deep learning model using TTNN framework (additional implementation practices with BOS-Metal interface)**. The workflow is divided into distinct phases that address everything from initial model analysis to final performance evaluation.

---

**The objective is to implement a deep learning model that efficiently runs on an NPU device with minimal reliance on the CPU,** ensuring both **functional correctness and optimal performance**. For short, we call such function or operation that reliance on CPU process as ***fallback operation (or fallback-op)***.

Below is a *recommended* **7 steps** approach to develop a deep learning model using TTNN framework:

We don't have a way to export this macro.

Below table are short descriptions and highlights for each step. More detailed information can be found in the corresponding pages within this section.

> ![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/plugins/servlet/confluence/placeholder/error?i18nKey=editor.placeholder.error&locale=en_US&version=2 "Cannot invoke \"String.indexOf(int)\" because \"href\" is null") Each document for these steps is structured to cover up to **3 aspects**:
>
> 1. **Summary**
> 2. **Outputs (with checklist).**
> 3. **Implementation Practices:** **TTNN** and **BOS-Metal** implementation examples.

We don't have a way to export this macro.

| **No.** | **Step** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **1** | **Model analysis** | Evaluate the **model architecture, parameters, and baseline metrics** (e.g., FLOPs, dataset characteristics) to establish performance expectations. Besides, deep understand of **model architecture design intuition/techniques** is also important. |
| **2** | **Pytorch implementation** | Develop and validate the original model using PyTorch, ensuring **correct functionality** and establishing **reference outputs and behavior**. |
| **3** | **Torch to TTNN conversion** | Convert the PyTorch model to TTNN by **mapping parameters and fusing operations** (e.g., conv-bn), and more. This step ensures a one-to-one correspondence where applicable. |
| **4** | **TTNN implementation** | Build the **TTNN model** by configuring each layer with device- and shape-specific settings, replicating the original architecture within the TTNN framework. |
| **5** | **Functional Validation** | **Compare outputs between the PyTorch and TTNN models** using shape and value comparisons (comparison metrics as **PCC**), and perform module-wise tests to verify that all functionalities are correct. |

| **6** | **Operation implementation** | **Implement** **TTNN-specific operations** if not supported. |
| **7** | **Performance Optimization** | **Optimize individual modules** using built-in methods offered by TT-Metal or using profiling tools, applying techniques such as operation fusion, pruning, quantization, etc. as needed. |

---

The structure of a model can be devided into:

- `README.md` every model must have this file to illustrate:

 - Environment setup,
 - Dataset preparation,
 - Guide of model demo, functional/performance tests,
 - Guide of model performance reports (Tracy Profile Report, L1 Visualization Report, etc.).
- `reference` directory contains the source code of model using referenced library, in most case it is `pytorch` implementation.
- `tt` directory has similar modular structure of `reference` directory, contain `ttnn` implementation of the model.
- `tests` folder contains unit tests of model's components or end-to-end performance/validation.
- `requirements.txt` (if required)
- `<model_name>_runner.py`: model runner
- `run.py`: script for model demo
- `<optional folders>` other folders if neccessary.

See https://github.com/bos-semi/tt-metal/tree/develop/models/bos_model for more information

```
tt-metal/models/bos_model/<model_name>
 |- README.md
 |- reference
 |- tt
 |- tests
 |- <model_name>_runner.py
 |- run.py
 |- requirements.txt
 `- <optional_folders>
```

| Owner | Model | Sub-Modules | Spec Study | PyTorch Impl | TTNN Impl (4x5) | Model Verification | Perf Optimization |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Quang | OFTNet | - ResNet18 - OFT | 20/06 | 04/07 | 25/07 | 01/08 | 29/08 |
| Thong | DeepLab | - Resnet50 | 13/06 | 27/06 (Whole model) | 11/07 | 18/07 | 29/08 |
| Tram | DeepLab | - PanopticDeepLabSemSegHead - PanopticDeepLabInsEmbedHead | 20/06 | - | 25/07 | 01/08 | 29/08 |
| Nhat | SSR | - TokenLearnerV11 - Latent\_decoder + way decoder - Motion-aware Layer Normalization (MLN) - TokenFuser - Latent\_world\_model | 13/06 | 20/06 | 11/07 | 18/07 | 29/08 |

metadata.title: Moreh Getitem Operation - Description and Performance Issue
