metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
**Hardware**

- A0 Board (needed to confirmed working with SSR-net)
- Firmware: v18.5.0 (TT-Flash)
- Host System OS: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
**Step 1 – Clone TT\_METAL SSR-net**

- Branch: `develop`

```
git clone http://192.128.10.101:8093/tt_metal_demo/tt-metal.git --branch develop --recurse-submodules
```

**Step 2 – Modify environment variables**

Add new environment variables to `~/.bashrc`. Modify `TT_METAL_HOME` pointing to `tt-metal` repository you just clone.

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
```
export TT_METAL_HOME="full/path/to/tt-metal/"
export WORKING_DIR="$TT_METAL_HOME/models/bos_model/ssr"
export BOS_METAL_HOME="$TT_METAL_HOME/tt_metal/third_party/bos-metal"

export ARCH_NAME=blackhole
export TT_METAL_ENV=dev
export VENDOR=BLACKHOLEPLUS_AS_TT
export TT_METAL_DISABLE_L1_DATA_CACHE_RISCVS="BR,NC,TR,ER"

export PYTHONPATH="$TT_METAL_HOME:$BOS_METAL_HOME:$WORKING_DIR:SSR"
export PYTHON_ENV_DIR="$TT_METAL_HOME/python_env"
```

and run the script to save variables:

```
source ~/.bashrc
```

**Step 3 – Build TT\_METAL and Create Python Environment**

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
```
cd $TT_METAL_HOME
# Install required dependencies (sudo might be required)
sudo ./install_dependencies.sh

# Build Virtual Environment
source create_venv.sh
source python_env/bin/activate

# Build TT-Metal (default is Debug mode)
./build_metal.sh -b Release
```

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
**Step 4 – Install Python packages**

> **IMPORTANT:** Since the SSR development requires MMDetection to work with registries, we need to ensure that **mmcv\_** and **mmdet** are correctly installed. Remove any previously installed mmcv package if necessary before install `mmcv-full`

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
```
pip install -r $BOS_METAL_HOME/requirements.txt
pip install -r $WORKING_DIR/requirements.txt
pip install mmcv-full==1.7.2 -f https://download.openmmlab.com/mmcv/dist/cpu/torch2.0/index.html
```

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
All pretrained weights and dataset (`1.0-mini` version) has been prepared and can be downloaded through this Link.

```
cd $WORKING_DIR
# Download 'data.zip'
unzip data.zip
```

Then create symlink `data/` folder in `tt/` and `reference/`

```
ln -s $WORKING_DIR/data/dataset $WORKING_DIR/tt/data
ln -s $WORKING_DIR/data/dataset $WORKING_DIR/reference/data
```

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content: Refer to Suggested Directory Structure or detail dataset preparation in Data Structure

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
We provide a **single CLI** with three modes: **functional**, **performance**, and **cpu** (PyTorch baseline). This allows you to validate SSR-net output, measure performance, or run a PyTorch reference for comparison.

**Script location:** `$WORKING_DIR/scripts/run_ssr_demo.sh`

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

section_title: 4.1. Functional Mode – Validate Output (TTNN vs PyTorch Reference)
content:
Runs SSR-net on sample data using **TTNN** with a given patch and compares against the PyTorch reference model [#TODO: sumodule by submodule].

```
bash $WORKING_DIR/scripts/run_ssr_demo.sh functional
```

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

section_title: 4.2. Performance Mode – Benchmark TTNN on nuScenes Dataset
content:
Measures inference latency and throughput on the **A0 TTNN** version of SSR-net. This will be inferenced on nuScenes dataset (either `full` or `mini` version is accepted)

```
bash $WORKING_DIR/scripts/run_ssr_demo.sh performance
```

Outputs will be saved in pickle format under `$WORKING_DIR/tt/test/` for visualization.

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
Runs the **PyTorch reference model** (CPU or GPU, depending on your PyTorch setup) for parity checks or debugging. Noted that it will save tensors for debugging along the way, you might want to create a folder to store them.

```
bash $WORKING_DIR/scripts/run_ssr_demo.sh cpu
```

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
You can override any of the following arguments for **any mode**:

| Argument | Description | Default in Script |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `--config` | Path to SSR-net config file | `projects/configs/SSR_e2e.py` |
| `--checkpoint` | Path to model checkpoint file | Depends on mode: - functional: `ckpts/ssr_tt.pth` - performance: `ckpts/ssr_tt.pth` - cpu:`ckpts/ssr_pt.pth` |
| `--embeddings` | Path to embeddings file | `embeddings/tensor_dict.pth` |
| `--launcher` | Launch mode for distributed training/inference | `none` |
| `--eval` | Evaluation metric | `bbox` |
| `--patch` | Path to patch file (**functional only**) | `/path/to/patch.pt` |

**Example – functional mode with a custom patch and checkpoint**:

```
bash $WORKING_DIR/scripts/run_ssr_demo.sh functional \
 --config projects/configs/custom_ssr.py \
 --checkpoint ckpts/custom_ssr.pth \
 --patch patches/patch_v2.pt
```

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
The SSR toolkit includes a visualization script located at below paths. These scripts can convert model predictions into a video for easier inspection.

```
$WORKING_DIR/scripts/analysis_tools/visualization.py
$WORKING_DIR/scripts/analysis_tools/realtime_visualization.py
```

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
Before visualizing, you must first run `inference_visualize.py` to generate the output pickle files. Then yse `visualization.py` tool to convert into video. These files store **two separate sets of results**:

- **Torch** model outputs
- **TTNN** model outputs

To run and export visualized video at once, execute with flag `realtime`, `max-batches` illustrates number of batches to be infered. A completed command is shown as below:

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
```
cd $WORKING_DIR
python $WORKING_DIR/scripts/inference_visualize.py \
 --data-root $WORKING_DIR/data/dataset/nuscenes \
 --data-version v1.0-mini \
 --max-batches 10 \
 --realtime
```

This will save result files under `generated` results (i.e. `$WORKING_DIR/generated/`):

```
/path/to/generated/visualization/realtime/
 ├── vis.jpg # last visualization result
 └── vis.mp4 # visualized video
```

metadata.title: SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Demo Guide for A0 Hardware_131793519.md

content:
- **SSR-net Weights:**

 - CPU-aligned PyTorch version: ssr\_pt.pth
 - TTNN version: ssr\_tt.pth
 - Create and move to checkpoints folder:

```
cd $WORKING_DIR && mkdir -p data/ckpts && cd data/ckpts
# Download model weights and move 'ssr_(pt/tt).pth' here
```

content:
Seminar's materials: VNhat\_BHRC\_SW\_NPU\_20250615-SSR.pptx

Underlying architecture: SSR\_underlying\_blocks.xlsx

!image-20250703-062450.png

I've put together a guide on the codebase structure (GitLab: BOS\_MODEL/SSR/) —feel free to check it out here: [SSR Codebase Guide.pdf](/wiki/spaces/bossemi/pages/97977524/SSR+Model+Specification?preview=%2F97977524%2F114589909%2FSSR+Codebase+Guide.pdf)
Search-Space Limitation and Cache-Based Acceleration for Performance Optimization in AI Autonomous Driving Models

The present invention relates to performance optimization techniques for AI-based autonomous driving systems, and more specifically to inference acceleration in end-to-end models utilizing sparse scene representations and multi-camera perception.
The invention is based on SSR (Sparse Scene Representation for End-to-End Autonomous Driving), an AI model designed to perform scene understanding and path planning for autonomous driving.

The SSR model suffers from slow inference speed due to three primary factors:

1. Memory-bound MSDA operations, which repeatedly compute BEV features for each camera, causing excessive memory access and redundant computation.
2. Unrestricted query computation, where the model processes scene queries for BEV regions that are not observable from the corresponding camera viewpoints.
These inefficiencies result in increased latency and reduced real-time performance in autonomous driving applications.

- Cache-Based BEV Feature Reuse: A cache is used to store BEV features produced for each camera within local buffer. BEV features within camera are reusable locally during kernel execution, eliminating redundant dataflow.
- Cache-based reuse of bilinear interpolation coefficients: As a secondary caching mechanism for accelerating MSDA operations, the bilinear interpolation coefficients—which involve floating-point computations—are precomputed, and a lookup table is constructed using these results to eliminate redundant calculations.
- Query Search-Space Limitation: A geometric selection mechanism restricts scene queries to only those BEV regions within the camera-visible area. By reducing the number of queries, unnecessary MSDA and decoder operations are avoided.

The combined approach yields:

- Substantial improvement in inference speed
- Reduced memory bandwidth usage
- Lower computational overhead, especially in MSDA layers
- Improved real-time responsiveness for autonomous driving tasks.

The invention enables efficient deployment of SSR-based models on resource-constrained or latency-critical hardware.

In the SSR model inference process using the NuScenes(https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/x/UwQEC) mini dataset, when processing a camera, the BEV features required by MSDA load into a local buffer (Input Cirr). These BEV feature tiles remain hot and reusable throughout the kernel execution for that camera. During MSDA operations, instead of repeatedly performing intermediate computations in real time, the bilinear interpolation coefficients—normally involving floating-point operations—are precomputed in advance and stored in memory. At runtime, these coefficients are directly loaded and reused, preventing redundant execution of identical calculations. This reduces computational overhead and improves overall inference efficiency. Also, based on camera extrinsic parameters, a visibility mask identifies the BEV regions observed by each camera. Only scene queries whose spatial coordinates fall inside the visible region are retained. A fixed number of "key queries" are selected based on importance scores or geometric filters. Because both BEV computation and query count are reduced, the total execution time is significantly lowered.

A baseline SSR model without the invention behaves as follows:

- No BEV Caching: MSDA repeatedly reads BEV feature tiles from DRAM during deformable attention sampling, while some features are read multiple times resulting in redundant MSDA execution and unnecessary memory bandwidth usage.
- No cache for bilinear interpolation coefficients: Since the interpolation coefficients must be recomputed during every MSDA operation, the computational load increases, resulting in longer overall inference time.
- No Query Restriction: The model computes and evaluates scene queries for all BEV regions, including areas not visible from the camera viewpoints. This increases both MSDA load.

1. A cache module for accelerating MSDA-based BEV feature generation in an SSR autonomous driving model, wherein BEV features required for deformable attention are stored in a local buffer during processing of a camera, enabling repeated reuse of BEV feature tiles without redundant memory access.
2. The SSR model of Claim 1, the model further comprises a cache-based bilinear interpolation coefficient reuse mechanism, wherein, instead of performing intermediate computations repeatedly in real time during MSDA operations, the bilinear interpolation coefficients are precomputed and stored in memory, and the stored coefficients are loaded and reused at runtime, thereby reducing intermediate computational overhead.
3. The SSR model of Claim 1, further comprising a query search-space limitation module that restricts scene queries to BEV regions within the camera-visible area, selects a predetermined subset of key queries, and performs inference using only the selected queries, thereby reducing MSDA dataflow

1. A process that accelerates BEV inference by reusing cached input features during BEV feature computation, thereby eliminating redundant data loading

!image-20251211-055956.png

2. A process that reduces the search space by evaluating multiple scene query candidates generated from the BEV–navigation fused tensor and selecting only the key queries.

!image-20251211-060042.png

section_title: 8. **Comparison Between the Existing Model and the Proposed Method**
content:
The original SSR model processes autonomous driving scenes through the following stages:

1. Multi-Camera Backbone: Six images are encoded into multi-scale feature maps.
2. BEV Encoder: Features are projected into a unified BEV map using temporal self-attention and MSDA-based spatial cross-attention.
3. Query Generation (TokenLearner): The BEV map is compressed into a fixed set(only the essential information for autonomous driving) of scene queries.
4. Planning Decoder: The queries are used to predict the ego-vehicle trajectory.

!image-20250925-042902.png

This design is not hardware-efficient inference especially for model deployment. All MSDA computations are recomputed every frame, and the model processes a fixed number of queries regardless of visibility due to static memory allocation mechanism.

section_title: 8. **Comparison Between the Existing Model and the Proposed Method**
content:
| **Aspect** | **Existing SSR** | **Proposed Method** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Cache-Based BEV Feature Reuse | Typically, the BEV tiles are handled collectively in one operation. However, for hardware with limited computational or memory capacity (e.g., non-GPU accelerators), the system may be unable to process all tiles simultaneously, so BEV tiles are repeatedly loaded from DRAM during MSDA, resulting in redundant memory access. | For hardware with limited computational or memory capacity (e.g., non-GPU accelerators), BEV tiles are cached locally and reused throughout each camera's MSDA execution, eliminating repeated DRAM loads. |
| Query Search-Space Limitation | Due to static memory allocation mechanism for model deployment; naive implementation uses full fixed query size, not well-studied method used to remove non-visible BEV queries. | Query count is reduced using camera-specific visibility masks and dataset-derived thresholds, lowering unnecessary MSDA and decoder workload. |

section_title: 8. **Comparison Between the Existing Model and the Proposed Method**
content: | Cache-based reuse of bilinear interpolation coefficients | Bilinear interpolation coefficients and sampling offsets are recomputed using FP32 operations for every query. | Precomputed values of biliner weight coefficients are stored once and reused, removing most runtime FP32 computation. |

section_title: 8. **Comparison Between the Existing Model and the Proposed Method**
content:
In summary, the proposed method introduces three optimizations absent in the current SSR implementation:

1. Cache-based BEV feature reuse
2. Visibility-based query search-space reduction
3. Cache-based reuse of MSDA bilinear interpolation coefficients

The proposed approach is deployment-oriented, significantly reducing computation and memory bandwidth while accelerating MSDA-heavy stages.

Without our proposal,

1. w/o 'Cache-Based BEV Feature Reuse' and 'Cache-based reuse of bilinear interpolation coefficients':

!image-20251209-223948.png

2. w/o 'Query Search-Space Limitation':

!image-20251211-041301.png

section_title: 9. Diagrams and explanations related to the actual hardware configuration
content:
Currently, the cameras and sensors are not physically attached to a vehicle. Instead, We use the dataset—containing driving videos captured by vehicle-mounted six cameras—which is stored on the host computer.

This data is transferred to the device via PCIe and stored in the device's DRAM. When the deep learning model runs, the required data is fetched from the device DRAM and sent to the compute core at the appropriate time. The compute core processes the data, and the resulting outputs are written back to the device DRAM. Finally, the results are transferred back to the host through PCIe, completing the deep learning model execution pipeline.

Also, the weights of the SSR model are transferred to the device DRAM through the same process. When a specific layer is executed, the corresponding weights are sent from the device DRAM to the compute core and used for computation.

!image-20251210-064558.png

---

Aside from replacing the 'Image Backbone' with a lighter alternative, I believe there is very little room for further optimization in the backbone itself. The next most computationally intensive component is the 'Encoder'.

!image-20251210-012619.png

Input features(multi-scale) are projected into a Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) representation using a BEV encoder. SSR still uses a conventional BEV encoder (based on BEVFormer to convert multi-view images into a dense BEV feature tensor, encoding both temporal (from the previous state) and spatial information. The result is a unified, top-down BEV feature map of the environment.

> Think of it like exploring a town: every step you take offers new street photos from different angles—front, back, and sides. If you tried to stitch these snapshots together directly, you'd quickly get lost. But if you overlay them onto a Google Maps view, everything aligns: buildings, cars, and pedestrians are placed consistently around you. That's what the BEV Encoder does—it transforms fragmented camera views into a coherent map-like representation – it also updates real-time!

!image-20250925-045201.png

Once this BEV map is built, SSR enhances it using two key mechanisms:

Once this BEV map is built, SSR enhances it using two key mechanisms:

- Temporal Self-Attention: integrates information across past frames, so the model understands how the scene is evolving over time.
- Spatial Cross-Attention: fuses details from different camera views, ensuring that the BEV map reflects all perspectives consistently.

Normally, two above goals can be obtained using traditional Transformer ([[1706.03762] Attention Is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762)). However, directly attending to every pixel across every scale is computationally prohibitive. This is where Multi-Scale Deformable Attention comes in:

- Instead of exhaustively scanning all features, it samples only a small set of key points across multiple scales.

 - Think of it like a surveyor using laser pointers to check just a few crucial spots on a blueprint instead of measuring every inch of the map.
- This makes BEV construction both efficient and precise, capturing fine details (like lane markings) and large structures (like intersections) without excessive cost.

By combining temporal alignment, spatial fusion, and deformable multi-scale sampling, the BEV Encoder produces a rich yet efficient scene representation.

!image-20251020-095621.png

In deformable attention using a single scale, k sampling points are used. In contrast, MSDA (Multi-Scale Deformable Attention) samples l × k points, where l is the number of scales used. When multi-scale is added, the only additional consideration compared to deformable attention is the handling of multiple feature-map levels (i.e., different resolutions), rather than a single feature map.

> Deformable Attention aims to reduce the cost of traditional dense attention by attending to a sparse set of key-value pairs based on learned offsets. Instead of attending to all keys, each query attends to a limited number of spatial sampling points across multi-scale feature maps.

!image-20250925-071325.png

This method identifies only the queries that are truly necessary by leveraging the fact that each camera observes a different BEV region size. By examining datasets such as nuScenes, we record—for every scene and for every camera—the number of "meaningful cells" and construct a distribution. We then use the 99~99.9% values of that distribution as a threshold. Using this threshold, we determine how many queries are valid for each camera (e.g., "Camera i has N valid queries").

!image-20251210-061418.png

Optimum Query Search-Space Method and Cache-Based Acceleration for Performance Optimization in AI Autonomous Driving Models

---

!feat-car.png

*#todo: Figure 02 to illustrate the accesses of BEV features in MSDA on NPUs (DRAM, SRAM, CB, RISCV-Cores)*

The present invention relates to performance optimization techniques for AI-based autonomous driving systems, and more specifically to inference acceleration in end-to-end models utilizing sparse scene representations and multi-camera perception.

section_title: todo: more background on autonomous driving models, mention the employment of BEV encoders
content: The invention is based on SSR (Sparse Scene Representation for End-to-End Autonomous Driving), an AI model designed to perform scene understanding and path planning for autonomous driving.

End-to-end autonomous driving systems employing Sparse Scene Representation (SSR) model rely on a BEV (Bird's-Eye-View) Encoder with Multi-Scale Deformable Attention (MSDA) to extract multi-camera image features for downstreaming temporal and spatial representation. When deployed on Neural Processing Units (NPUs) or specialized accelerators, the BEV Encoder must operate within strict hardware and software constraints:

(1) MSDA execution remains **memory-bound** due to **repeated accesses of identical BEV feature locations**, resulting in redundant sampling operations (illustrated in Figure 02).

(1) MSDA execution remains **memory-bound** due to **repeated accesses of identical BEV feature locations**, resulting in redundant sampling operations (illustrated in Figure 02).

- On edge devices, the available on-chip SRAM is typically limited. When BEV feature maps exceed SRAM capacity and must reside in DRAM, MSDA becomes dominated by **memory dataflow** rather than computation, preventing efficient utilization of the hardware's compute resources.
- Although MSDA samples only a small subset of points relative to the total number of BEV keys, the **pigeonhole principle** implies that **multiple sampled points frequently map to the same BEV feature location**. Without a caching mechanism, these locations are repeatedly fetched from DRAM.

**(2)** Typical NPU runtime frameworks require **static tensor allocation** or **fixed output shapes**, meaning all tensor dimensions must be determined prior to execution so that the accelerator can schedule memory and compute resources deterministically.

- This constraint introduces a challenge for the **Spatial Cross Attention** module, which uses deformable attention to extract BEV features from six cameras. The number of valid BEV queries produced for each camera varies significantly due to **camera-specific visibility regions**, and these values are **scene-dependent**.
- A **naive approach** is to **ignore BEV masking** altogether and process the **full pristine BEV grid** (e.g., all 100x100 positions) uniformly for every camera and scene. While this satisfies static-shape requirements by effectively applying a **single global padding size**, it results in substantial redundant computation and unnecessary dataflow, as many of these BEV positions are not visible or meaningful for a given camera (see **Figure 01**). Therefore, a more sophisticated method is needed to reconcile static-shape constraints with the inherently variable, visibility-dependent BEV query counts.

Accordingly, there is a need for:

Accordingly, there is a need for:

- Techniques that **reduce the memory-bound** behavior of Multi-Scale Deformable Attention by minimizing redundant BEV feature accesses and improving data reuse.
- Methods for **determining an optimal static BEV query size** that complies with NPU fixed-shape constraints while accommodating per-camera visibility differences, minimizing redundant computation and retaining model's accuracy.

The present invention addresses these needs.

The invention provides two complementary mechanisms designed specifically for NPUs that require static, preallocated tensor shapes:

1. **A Cache-Based BEV Feature Kernel** that eliminates redundant BEV feature loads during MSDA operations.
2. **An Optimum Query Search-Space Method** that statically restricts per-camera query shapes prior to execution, removing padded queries while preserving fixed tensor dimensions;

These mechanisms operate within the constraints of SIMD-based BEV encoding pipelines and significantly reduce redundant operations originating from global max-query padding.

The combined approach yields:

- Substantial improvement in inference speed
- Reduced memory bandwidth usage
- Lower computational overhead, especially in MSDA layers
- Improved real-time responsiveness for autonomous driving tasks.

The invention enables efficient deployment of SSR-based models on resource-constrained or latency-critical hardware.

In the SSR model inference, when processing a given camera, the BEV features required by MSDA are first **loaded into a local buffer** (Input Circular Buffer). These BEV feature remain **hot and reusable throughout the kernel execution** for that camera BEV feature.

Also, a visibility mask identifies the BEV regions observed by each camera for each scene. Only scene queries whose spatial coordinates fall inside the visible region are retained. A fixed number of "key queries" are selected using a **percentile-based statistical sizing method derived from large-scale dataset sampling**.

Because both BEV computation and query count are reduced, the total execution time is significantly lowered. These two embodiments are described detailed in the following sections.

This part of the invention addresses redundant DRAM reads in MSDA. Due to deformable attention sampling, the BEV Encoder repeatedly accesses the same BEV feature features across multiple queries, points, and attention heads. In naive implementation (see **Figure XX**):

- Each MSDA sampling step triggered DRAM reads,
- Many features were fetched repeatedly,
- The operation became memory-bound.

The invention introduces a **local BEV Feature Cache** with the following properties:

- **Static Preloading of Required BEV Features.** For each camera and its static query size:

 - All BEV features required by MSDA are loaded into a local Input Circular Buffer once prior to computation steps.
 - Features remain "hot" for the entire duration of that camera's MSDA operations.
- **Bilinear Weight Hashing.** In addition to BEV feature caching, the invention introduces a second caching mechanism that accelerates MSDA by avoiding repeated computation of bilinear interpolation weights.

- The model builds a **Precomputed Bilinear Weight Lookup Table** by discretizing the (Δx,Δy) space and computing the corresponding bilinear coefficients in advance.
 - During MSDA execution, a **copy of this table is stored on each NPU core**. The nearest discretized index is selected and the cached weights are retrieved directly, eliminating per-query floating-point calculations on NRISC cores and reducing interpolation overhead.
 - Together with BEV feature caching, this **bilinear weight hashing** method further lowers memory traffic and computational load throughout MSDA.

For each camera, a visibility mask which identifies the BEV grid cells observable by that camera is computed online. Because some cameras cover large frontal regions while others view narrower or partially occluded areas (see **Figure 01**), each camera naturally produces a different number of valid BEV spatial queries across different scenes.

The number of valid queries varies per camera and per scene, but NPUs require fixed tensor shapes at runtime and cannot operate on variable-length query tensors. To address this constraint, the invention:

- Determines a **camera-specific static maximum query size** using the **percentile-based statistical sizing method** (e.g., p99–p99.9 cutoff with headroom and tile alignment). These per-camera static sizes are **derived offline prior to realtime model execution** from large-scale dataset sampling (e.g., nuScenes) to ensure high inclusion confidence.
- Assigns each camera a **fixed query tensor size** that satisfies NPU static-shape requirements without padding everything to a single global maximum, as mentioned in native implementation (Section Problems to Solved.

**Percentile-based Statistical Method**

The invention assigns optimum fixed-size queries per camera and apply attention on each camera features sequentially. This satisfies NPU requirements while removing the vast majority of padded elements. The optimum fixed size of visible BEV queries for each camera is calculated from:

For example, with BEV size of 100x100 (10,000) applied for nuScenes full dataset, we retrieved following data with the mentioned method:

| **Camera** | **Static Max Query Count** |
| --- | --- |
| **CAM 0** | 3,072 |
| **CAM 1** | 1,344 |
| **CAM 2** | 1,376 |
| **CAM 3** | 3,680 |
| **CAM 4** | 928 |
| **CAM 5** | 992 |

This preserves fixed tensor dimensions while aligning computation with actual visibility.

**Sequential Execution on Camera BEV Features.**

This preserves fixed tensor dimensions while aligning computation with actual visibility.

**Sequential Execution on Camera BEV Features.**

Because NPUs require **static tensor shapes** and cannot accommodate variable per-camera BEV query sizes in a single batched operation, the BEV features from all cameras cannot be processed together in one SIMD batch. Instead, the invention executes **each camera sequentially**:

- Each camera uses its own fixed query size derived from the percentile-based sizing method.
- In Spatial Cross Attention, MSDA is invoked **separately for each camera's BEV feature**.

Although the features are processed sequentially, this approach significantly **reduces redundant dataflow and computation by avoiding padded operations on non-visible BEV regions**, while still complying with NPU static-shape constraints.

A baseline SSR model without the invention behaves as follows:

- **No BEV Feature Caching:** MSDA repeatedly reads BEV features from DRAM during deformable attention sampling, while some features are read multiple times resulting in redundant MSDA execution and unnecessary memory bandwidth usage.
- **No Query Restriction:** The model computes and evaluates scene queries for all BEV regions, including areas not visible from the camera viewpoints.

With a combination of 2 embodiments, when inference runs:

1. BEV features are loaded once into the cache and reused for all MSDA sampling steps. Optimized MSDA kernels reduce memory transactions and redundant sampling on masked regions.
2. Each camera uses a **static, visibility-limited query shape**, compatible with NPU constraints. Camera feature are processed sequentially under SIMD execution rules.

The result is a fully static, NPU-compatible pipeline that eliminates the inefficiencies caused by original max-query padding.

1. A cache-based kernel for accelerating MSDA-based BEV feature generation in an SSR autonomous driving model, wherein BEV features required for deformable attention are stored in a local buffer during processing of a camera, enabling repeated reuse of BEV features without redundant memory access.
2. The SSR model of Claim 1, further comprising an optimum query search-space method that restricts scene queries to BEV regions within the camera-visible area, selects a predetermined subset of key queries, and performs inference using only the selected queries, thereby reducing MSDA dataflow.
Search-Space Limitation and Cache-Based Acceleration Techniques for Performance Optimization of AI Models Using BEVFormer

content:
The AI model using BEVFormer suffers from slow inference speed due to two primary factors:

1. Memory-bound MSDA operations, which repeatedly compute BEV features for each camera, causing excessive memory access and redundant computation.
2. Unrestricted query computation, where the model processes scene queries for BEV regions that are not observable from the corresponding camera viewpoints.

These inefficiencies result in increased latency and reduced real-time performance in applications.

content:
- Cache-Based BEV Feature Reuse: A cache is used to store BEV features produced for each camera within local buffer. BEV features within camera are reusable locally during kernel execution, eliminating redundant dataflow.
- Cache-based reuse of bi-linear interpolation coefficients: As a secondary caching mechanism for accelerating MSDA operations, the bi-linear interpolation coefficients—which involve floating-point computations—are precomputed, and a lookup table is constructed using these results to eliminate redundant calculations.
- Query Search-Space Limitation: A geometric selection mechanism restricts scene queries to only those BEV regions within the camera-visible area. By reducing the number of queries, unnecessary MSDA operations are avoided.

content:
Use an SSR model based on BEVFormer.

In that SSR model inference process using the NuScenes(https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/x/UwQEC) mini dataset, when processing a camera, the BEV features required by MSDA load into a local buffer (Input Cirr). These BEV feature tiles remain hot and reusable throughout the kernel execution for that camera. During MSDA operations, instead of repeatedly performing intermediate computations in real time, the bi-linear interpolation coefficients—normally involving floating-point operations—are precomputed in advance and stored in memory. At runtime, these coefficients are directly loaded and reused, preventing redundant execution of identical calculations. This reduces computational overhead and improves overall inference efficiency. Also, based on camera extrinsic parameters, a visibility mask identifies the BEV regions observed by each camera. Only scene queries whose spatial coordinates fall inside the visible region are retained. A fixed number of "key queries" are selected based on importance scores or geometric filters. Because both BEV computation and query count are reduced, the total execution time is significantly lowered.

- No cache for bi-linear interpolation coefficients: Since the interpolation coefficients must be recomputed during every MSDA operation, the computational load increases, resulting in longer overall inference time.
- No Query Restriction: The model computes and evaluates scene queries for all BEV regions, including areas not visible from the camera viewpoints. This increases both MSDA load.

section_title: 8. **Comparison Between the Existing Model and the Proposed Method**
content:
BEVFormer converts multi-view camera images into a dense bird's-eye-view (BEV) feature tensor via transformer-based attention. It aggregates temporal information from the previous BEV state through temporal self-attention and integrates multi-view spatial features using geometry-aware spatial cross-attention, producing a unified top-down BEV representation of the environment.

!image-20251217-042204.png

- Temporal Self-Attention: integrates information across past frames, so the model understands how the scene is evolving over time.
- Spatial Cross-Attention: fuses details from different camera views, ensuring that the BEV map reflects all perspectives consistently.

metadata.title: SSR Patent v2 - Description of Invention
metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Patent v2 - Description of Invention_249954330.md

section_title: 8. **Comparison Between the Existing Model and the Proposed Method**
content:
Normally, two above goals can be obtained using traditional Transformer ([[1706.03762] Attention Is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762)). However, directly attending to every pixel across every scale is computationally prohibitive. This is where Multi-Scale Deformable Attention comes in:

- Instead of exhaustively scanning all features, it samples only a small set of key points across multiple scales.

 - Think of it like a surveyor using laser pointers to check just a few crucial spots on a blueprint instead of measuring every inch of the map.
- This makes BEV construction both efficient and precise, capturing fine details (like lane markings) and large structures (like intersections) without excessive cost.

By combining temporal alignment, spatial fusion, and deformable multi-scale sampling, the BEV Encoder produces a rich yet efficient scene representation.

!image-20251020-095621.png

However, for hardware with limited computational or memory capacity (e.g., non-GPU accelerators), the system may be unable to process all tiles simultaneously, so BEV tiles are repeatedly loaded from DRAM during MSDA, resulting in redundant memory access. | For hardware with limited computational or memory capacity (e.g., non-GPU accelerators), BEV tiles are cached locally and reused throughout each camera's MSDA execution, eliminating repeated DRAM loads. |

metadata.source_file: retrieved_docs/SSR Patent v2 - Description of Invention_249954330.md

content:
The original SSR model processes autonomous driving scenes through the following stages:

1. Multi-Camera Backbone: Six images are encoded into multi-scale feature maps.
2. BEV Encoder: Features are projected into a unified BEV map using temporal self-attention and MSDA-based spatial cross-attention.
3. Query Generation (TokenLearner): The BEV map is compressed into a fixed set(only the essential information for autonomous driving) of scene queries.
4. Planning Decoder: The queries are used to predict the ego-vehicle trajectory.

!image-20250925-042902.png

section_title: 10. Diagrams and explanations related to the actual hardware configuration
content:
Currently, the cameras and sensors are not physically attached to a vehicle. Instead, We use the dataset—containing driving videos captured by vehicle-mounted six cameras—which is stored on the host computer.

This data is transferred to the device via PCIe and stored in the device's DRAM. When the deep learning model runs, the required data is fetched from the device DRAM and sent to the compute core at the appropriate time. The compute core processes the data, and the resulting outputs are written back to the device DRAM. Finally, the results are transferred back to the host through PCIe, completing the deep learning model execution pipeline.

Also, the weights of the SSR model are transferred to the device DRAM through the same process. When a specific layer is executed, the corresponding weights are sent from the device DRAM to the compute core and used for computation.

!image-20251210-064558.png

content:
In deformable attention using a single scale, k sampling points are used. In contrast, MSDA (Multi-Scale Deformable Attention) samples l × k points, where l is the number of scales used. When multi-scale is added, the only additional consideration compared to deformable attention is the handling of multiple feature-map levels (i.e., different resolutions), rather than a single feature map.

> Deformable Attention aims to reduce the cost of traditional dense attention by attending to a sparse set of key-value pairs based on learned offsets. Instead of attending to all keys, each query attends to a limited number of spatial sampling points across multi-scale feature maps.

!image-20250925-071325.png

content:
This method identifies only the queries that are truly necessary by leveraging the fact that each camera observes a different BEV region size. By examining datasets such as nuScenes, we record—for every scene and for every camera—the number of "meaningful cells" and construct a distribution. We then use the 99~99.9% values of that distribution as a threshold. Using this threshold, we determine how many queries are valid for each camera (e.g., "Camera i has N valid queries").

!image-20251210-061418.png

 1. Why do we need modify ttnn.nonzero?

---

The MSDA operator, developed by Mr. Thien, was integrated into the module. At this stage, the operator lacks a dedicated compute kernel implementation. As a result, while the operator itself runs quickly, its presence has side effects that degrade the performance of other module components, resulting in lower overall efficiency. Additionally, the final Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) drops to around 0.6, indicating suboptimal accuracy.

Tabe 1 - before add MSDA op - **Overall duration:** 27.07 seconds

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Temporal Attention** | Temporal before and MSDA | 3.33618617 | 2.82154155 | 2.80874658 | 8.96647430 |
| Temporal after MSDA | 0.00082493 | 0.00082922 | 0.00081062 | 0.00246477 |
| **Spatial Cross Attention** | Get bev\_mask | 0.58899784 | 0.58755708 | 0.59039831 | 1.76695323 |
| Fallback time | 0.46820807 | 0.46525240 | 0.46667647 | 1.40013694 |
| Time MSDA take | 3.87563396 | 3.88442969 | 3.87552094 | 11.63558459 |
| After MSDA | 0.10171676 | 0.10765076 | 0.10261273 | 0.31198025 |

Table 2 - after add MSDA op - **Overall duration:** 30.45 seconds
- **Table 1 (Baseline) is faster** overall (27.07s) compared to Table 2 (30.45s) post-MSDA integration..

- **Get bev\_mask:**

 - Table 1: 1.77s
 - Table 2: 9.91s
 - **Observation:** After adding the MSDA op, this stage is significantly slower, suggesting the op introduces a performance bottleneck or increases workload for this step.
- **Temporal before and MSDA:**

 - Table 1: 8.97s
 - Table 2: 0.0059s
 - **Observation:** This step is drastically faster after integration, indicating that MSDA has positively impacted this attention block, likely due to more efficient temporal attention.
- **Time MSDA take:**

 - Table 1: 11.64s
 - Table 2: 0.0083s
 - **Observation:** Major reduction post-integration; the actual MSDA op runs efficiently.
- **After MSDA:**

 - Table 1: 0.31s
 - Table 2: 15.79s
 - **Observation:** Significant regression. The integration causes this step to consume much more time.
- **Fallback time** and **Temporal after MSDA:**
Both remain nearly the same between logs, so these steps are not bottlenecks.

- **Temporal attention block** shows clear performance improvement after integrating the MSDA op, validating its design for that section.
- **Spatial cross attention block** is **negatively impacted**:

 - Marked increase in time for `get bev_mask` and `after MSDA` indicates that the presence of the MSDA op (even without a compute kernel) introduces overhead or resource contention that slows down spatial inference.
- The **raw MSDA operator** itself is still efficient; the performance hit comes from how it interacts with the module, not the operator core.
- **Temporal attention block** shows clear performance improvement after integrating the MSDA op, validating its design for that section.
- **Spatial cross attention block** is **negatively impacted**:

 - Marked increase in time for `get bev_mask` and `after MSDA` indicates that the presence of the MSDA op (even without a compute kernel) introduces overhead or resource contention that slows down spatial inference.
- The **raw MSDA operator** itself is still efficient; the performance hit comes from how it interacts with the module, not the operator core.

---

Tabe 1 - before add MSDA op - **Overall duration:** 27.07 seconds

| **Name Module** | **Step** | **Iter 1** | **Iter 2** | **Iter 3** | **Total** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Temporal Attention** | Temporal Attention (TTNN) | 3.3924 | 2.8447 | 2.8478 | 9.0848 |
| **Spatial Attention** | Get BEV Mask (TTNN + Torch) | 1.0791 | 1.0645 | 1.0672 | 3.2108 |
| MSDA (TTNN) | 4.0221 | 4.0111 | 4.0155 | 12.0487 |
| After MSDA (TTNN + Torch) | 0.1199 | 0.1109 | 0.1057 | 0.3366 |

Table 2 - after add MSDA op - **Overall duration:** ~12. seconds

| **Name Module** | **Step** | **Iter 1** | **Iter 2** | **Iter 3** | **Total** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Temporal Attention** | Temporal Attention (TTNN) | 0.0153 | 0.0282 | 0.0535 | 0.1047 |
| **Spatial Attention** | Get BEV Mask (TTNN + Torch) | 1.7099 | 1.1901 | 1.1339 | 4.0339 |
| MSDA (TTNN) | 0.0029 | 0.0027 | 0.0027 | 0.0083 |
| After MSDA (TTNN + Torch) | 2.0879 | 2.0568 | 1.8354 | 5.9802 |

After updating the MSDA operation, I observed improved results and faster execution.

After updating the MSDA operation, I observed improved results and faster execution.

However, I noticed that the TTNN operation's performance degrades when both Torch and TTNN are used together within the same execution context. Specifically, when fallback mechanisms involve both frameworks, the overall runtime increases to approximately **11–12 seconds**.

For example, the `After MSDA (TTNN + Torch)` step took approximately 2 seconds when both Torch and TTNN operations were mixed. However, after commenting out the Torch section, the same step only took about 0.0008 seconds. Based on this result, we estimate that if this module were fully implemented in TTNN, the total runtime would be just 0.07 to 0.08 seconds.

**Summary:**

- **MSDA update**: Improved speed and results.
- **TTNN + Torch (fallback enabled)**: Runtime ~11–12s (slower).
- **TTNN only**: Runtime ~0.08s (stable and fast).
- **Performance Note**: Mixing Torch and TTNN in the same workflow causes slowdowns; running them separately maintains optimal performance. After updating the MSDA operation, I observed improved results and faster execution.

---

→ Why it affected to `ttnn.to_torch` (`ttnn.from_device`):

- When using `ttnn.to_torch`, it internally calls the `ttnn.from_device` function with the default `cq_id = 0`, which is also the default setting in `ttnn.deformable_attention`. After I changed the `cq_id` to 1 for `ttnn.from_device`, the performance improved significantly ~6s but not verify pcc yet. Now, it is just a temporary solution, need resolve it in `ttnn.deformable_attention.`

| **Name Module** | Step | **Iter 1** | **Iter 2** | **Iter 3** | **Total** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Temporal Attention** | Temporal Attention (TTNN) | 0.02960 | 0.00337 | 0.00302 | 0.03599 |
| **Spatial Attention** | Get BEV Mask (TTNN + Torch) | 0.51606 | 0.51889 | 0.51481 | 1.5497 |
| MSDA (TTNN) | 0.03457 | 0.03021 | 0.02416 | 0.08894 |
| After MSDA (TTNN + Torch) | 0.05315 | 0.04591 | 0.03483 | 0.13389 |

So, we need the ttnn getter and setter ASAP.

---

I optimized the transformer module by replacing some redundant and persistent tensors, reducing the runtime to around 5 seconds.

I implemented an `.item()` method for `SSRTransformer`, but because it requires transferring tensors from device to host, it causes a performance drop—about 2 seconds for the first call to `.item()`. This has become a bottleneck, so we try to avoid falling back tensors to the host. Additionally, I minimized the use of `.item()` elsewhere, stabilizing the runtime at around 4 seconds.

The root cause of the issue is that `torch.nonzero` returns a tensor listing the indices of all nonzero elements, with a shape that depends on the input tensor's dimensions. For example, consider a tensor `A = [8, 1, 2, 0, 4, 0]`. The expected nonzero indices are `[0, 1, 2, 4]`. However, `nonzero` returns two tensors: one holding the count of nonzero elements (e.g., `[4]`), and another tensor sized like the input tensor (e.g., `[0, 1, 2, 4, 0, 0]`). To retrieve the exact indices, the count is used to slice the second tensor. The challenge arises because to get the count as a plain number, `.item()` is used. This operation transfers the value from the GPU (device) to the CPU (host), which can slow down execution. This host-device synchronization and data transfer is the bottleneck when this operation happens repeatedly.

section_title: Why we need modify ttnn.nonzero function?
content:
The output tensors from `nonzero` are used to perform `getitem` operations on the `query` tensor and then `setitem` into input tensors for MS Deformable Attention. For example:

```
queries_rebatch[j, i, :len(index_query_per_img)] = query[j, index_query_per_img]
reference_points_rebatch[j, i, :len(index_query_per_img)] = reference_points_per_img[j, index_query_per_img]
slots[j, index_query_per_img] += queries[j, i, :len(index_query_per_img)]
```

section_title: Why we need modify ttnn.nonzero function?
content:
Therefore, `index_query_per_img` must be accurate and contain no redundant padding values. If there are extra zeros in the padding, using `index_query_per_img` to access data, such as in:

```
getitem(query[j], [index_query_per_img], [0])
```

section_title: Why we need modify ttnn.nonzero function?
content:
would cause incorrect indexing or unexpected results.

Regarding who has the authority to modify the `nonzero` output, we have identified that **dynamic tensor allocation is currently not supported** due to architectural constraints in `tt-metal`. This limitation results in outputs being preallocated with fixed sizes (static), which explains the presence of zero padding.

We have created a GitHub issue for the `tt` team to address this problem, and we have also set up a [[2025.07.25] TTNN Nonzero Returns Redundant Shape and Separate Count, Hindering Efficient Index Extraction - 보스반도체 - Confluence](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bossemi/pages/120358334/2025.07.25+TTNN+Nonzero+Returns+Redundant+Shape+and+Separate+Count+Hindering+Efficient+Index+Extraction) document for HQ to coordinate with the `tt` team for future improvements to support dynamic allocation.

We have also explored multiple workaround approaches, but the root cause requires slicing off the redundant part of the `nonzero` output. We will continue to look for possible workarounds; however, at this stage, no viable alternative exists.

These subpages provide profiling analysis of SSR-net Submodules on different devices.

**Method**

- First, sort the ops by latency from highest to lowest, then accumulate their percentage contributions starting from the top.
- Then, select the set of ops whose cumulative share reaches roughly 80%, following the Pareto principle.

> **NOTE:** After each MR to `feature/ssr_v62` we will generate a tt-perf report folder and upload to SSR profile Sharepoint: ssr\_encoder\_profiles (format: `YYYYMMDD_ssr_<module-name>_!<MR-id>`). With this we can benchmark if there is any new optimization MR.

- Every Transformer must process `sequence_length` before doing attention. Therefore, it must be padded and unpadded after calculation:

 - Op TilizeWithValPadding: `ttnn.tilize_with_val_padding`
 - Op UntilizeWithUnpadding: `ttnn.untilize_with_unpadding`

`Numbers of ops pair = 3 * num_layers`

(TilizeWithValPadding pads dimensions to the nearest value where`val % 32 == 0`)

These ops pair usually take considerable device time to process, compared to other ops in Transformer.

- These are **element-wise** operations between **two tensors**:

 - `add`, `subtract`, `multiply`, `divide`
 - `power`, `modulo`
 - `logical_and`, `logical_or`, `logical_xor`
 - `greater_than`, `less_than`, `equal`, `not_equal`
 - `min`, `max`

This document aims to provide a comprehensive analysis of SSR E2E performance on BOS-A0. We mainly focus on area:

- Device Execution Time
- Memory Usage
- Benchmarking

The full pipeline runs in **221.3 ms**, with the **BEVEncoder** dominating the computation at **190 ms** (**85.9%** of total latency). The **Backbone** contributes **26 ms** (**11.7%**), while the **Scene Token Learner**, **Planning Decoder**, and **Trajectory module** together add only **5.3 ms** (**2.4%**). (Reference data: 20251107\_ssr\_e2e\_blocks.xlsx

| **Module** | **Device Time (ms)** | **Percentage (%)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Backbone (Resnet50 + FPN) | 26 | 11.7 |
| BEVEncoder | 190 | 85.9 |
| Scene TokenLearner + Planning Decoder + Planning Trajectory | 5.3 | 2.4 |
| **Total** | **221.3** | **100** |

These results indicate that BEV feature transformation is the primary runtime bottleneck, whereas the planning components impose minimal overhead.

Since **Resnet50** and **BEVEncoder** are the 2 most time-dominant modules, I will focus my analysis on these 2 modules.

Since **Resnet50** and **BEVEncoder** are the 2 most time-dominant modules, I will focus my analysis on these 2 modules.

- **Resnet50**: this is the most computational cost module in the whole model since it mainly contains convolutions.
- **BEVEncoder**: this is the most time dominant module in the whole model, mainly due to data movement operations.

In terms of operations, the top 5 dominant operations for SSR E2E are

| **Operation Code** | **Device Time (us)** | **Percentage** | **Number of OPs** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| BosSSRDeformableAttention (in0:l1\_interleaved) | 103,710 | 46.9% | 15 |
| ReshapeDeviceOperation (in0:dram\_interleaved) | 13315 | 6.0% | 14 |
| MoveDeviceOperation (in0:l1\_interleaved) | 10300 | 4.7% | 54 |
| ReshapeDeviceOperation (in0:l1\_interleaved) | 8665 | 3.9% | 42 |
| Conv2d (in0:height\_sharded) | 8553 | 3.9% | 69 |

!image-20251114-013249.png

If you look at these data, `BosSSRDeformableAttention` are the ones that account over 80% of model execution time on device. We will take a closer look on this operation in later section.

Resnet50 is densly-convolutional module, it contains most computations of whole model [#todo: how much FLOPs?], mainly convolutions layers. The optimization strategy was to perform conv on L1 and avoid data movement as much as possible at the same time.

Observations:

- From `conv1` to `layer3.0`, we can perform only 1 batch input at once with L1 Sharded
- From `layer3.1`to the end, we can perform 2 batches at once with L1 Sharded

We don't have a way to export this macro.

[#todo: explain why msda takes so much time, memory-bound?]
Memory profiling data here: ssr\_feb25\_0941

!l1_memory_occupation.png

This graph reflects **L1 occupancy** state at execution time, *not shows per-op allocation size*.

**Backbone (op IDs ~1350–1800):**

- Earlier stage shows lower and oscillating usage (~0.1–0.6 MiB), indicating lighter compute stages.

**BEV Encoder (op IDs ~2000–2600):**

- **Peak L1 occupation** reaches ~**1.31 MiB/~1.40 MiB**. Top contributors include `ttnn.concat`, `ttnn.Tensor.__getitem__`, and `ttnn.reshape`.
- A **high-memory plateau** (~0.7–0.95 MiB) is sustained between ~2000–2550, indicating long-lived tensors or heavy intermediate activations.
- Multiple **sharp spikes** suggest temporary buffer expansion, likely caused by concat or reshape-related operations.

**Planning Blocks (op IDs ~2600-end):**

- After ~2600, L1 drops sharply to near zero, marking the end of a major compute block and tensor release phase.
!memory_usage.png

- **DRAM:** Gradually increases up to ~1.8 MiB due to intermediate tensor spill, but remains relatively low overall.
- **L1:** Moderate repeated spikes (~8 MiB), mainly from ResNet50 convolution outputs.
- **Observation:** Efficient L1 utilization with limited DRAM pressure. No persistent memory buildup.

- **Peak Allocation:** ~9.77 MiB in both DRAM and L1.
- **Major Contributors:**
`ttnn.to_memory_config`, `ttnn.concat`, `ttnn.linear`, `ttnn.layer_norm`, `ttnn.reshape`
- **Behavior:** Frequent large spikes caused by temporary tensor materialization (e.g., attention maps, BEV grids, concat outputs).
- **Observation:** Most memory-intensive stage; dominated by large transient tensors rather than cumulative growth.

- Sharp drop in both DRAM and L1 usage.
- Only small, sporadic allocations remain.
- Indicates transition to lighter post-processing/decoding stages.

This document explains the visualization and real‑time inference tooling around SSR/BEV‑style perception using nuScenes. It covers:

- **Bird's‑eye‑view (BEV)** rendering of map layers, ego pose, and 3D boxes.
- Projecting ***planned trajectories*** (TTNN output) into BEV and front‑camera views.
- Composing a ***six‑camera grid***+ BEV into a single frame and exporting ***PNG/MP4***.
- The real‑time loop that runs Torch vs. TTNN inference and (optionally) live‑renders results.

---

```
+----------------------+ +----------------------+
| Results from model | | GT from NuScenes |
| • TTNN plan (BEV) | | • Map layers (BEV) |
| | | • 3D boxes (ego) |
+----------------------+ +----------------------+
 | |
 +-----------+---------------+
 v
 +---------------------------+
 | visualize_sample(...) |
 | • Map layers (BEV) |
 | • 3D boxes (ego frame) |
 | • TTNN plan (BEV) |
 +---------------------------+
 |
 +------------------+-------------------+
 | |
 v v
render_sample_data(...) Front/Rear camera loops
(BEV image) (project TTNN plan to CAM_FRONT)
 \ /
 \ /
 v v
 Compose 6 cams + BEV
 |
 v
 vis.png + vis.mp4
```

- TTNN outputs are post‑formatted as below and contain per‑sample planned trajectories and command logits.

```
bevformer_results = {
 'plan_results_ttnn': {...}, # Results from model, if output from Torch model, key name will beplan_results_torch
 'plan_gts': {...} # Ground truth
}
```

- `plan_gts` is a dictionary keyed by the sample ID (matching the one in `plan_results_ttnn`), where each value is a list containing a ground truth coordinates array (`numpy.float32`, shape `[steps, 2]`).

```
'plan_gts': {
 'sample_id': [
 array([[x1, y1], [x2, y2], ...], dtype=float32), # ground truth trajectory
 ]
```

- The code renders GT 3D boxes in BEV and overlays TTNN plan as green polylines.

- We convert all geometry through two chained transforms. First, we map **Global coordinates** to the **Ego frame** using the ego pose at the sample timestamp.
- For BEV, a convenience `world_to_bev(xy)` converts world XY into ego‑aligned BEV axes:

 - `x_BEV = -y_ego`, `y_BEV = x_ego` (so forward = +Y\_BEV, left = +X\_BEV).
- A local patch (≈ 30 m radius + padding) around the ego pose is fetched from nuScenes HD map (**lane**, **road\_divider**, **lane\_divider**).
- Map polylines are converted to BEV and drawn with **LineCollection** for anti‑aliased, rounded joins.
- Ground‑truth boxes near the ego (|x|,|y| ≤ 30 m) are transformed into ego and drawn as *red polygons*.
- A simple ***vehicle footprint*** and centerline are drawn for orientation cues.
- TTNN ***planned trajector**y* is discretized to many short segments (for smoothness) and drawn *green*.

- Centerlines*:* "lane" (blue-gray dashed, thin) and "lane\_connector" (lavender dashed, thin). These are *mid-line references* for routing and are optionally buffered into filled "lane areas" so ego boxes visibly sit *inside* lanes (not on a line).
- Boundaries/markings*:* "lane\_divider" (blue), "road\_divider" (orange), "stop\_line" (dark gray). These are the visible edges/markings of lanes/roads.
- Areas (polygons)*:* "drivable\_area", "road\_segment", "road\_block", "walkway", "carpark\_area", "ped\_crossing". All are filled with a very light pastel so lines and boxes remain readable.
- *Constants used by the map renderer:*

 - ***RESOLUTION\_M**:* sampling resolution (m) for parametric centerlines (smaller → smoother lines).
 - ***DENSIFY\_STEP\_M**:* re-sampling step for line layers to avoid tiny gaps on render (smaller → smoother).
 - ***LANE\_HALF\_WIDTH\_M**:* half width used to buffer centerlines into lane areas (e.g., 1.8 m for a ~3.6 m lane).
 - *LANE\_FILL\_COLOR:* light color to fill lane areas.

- Build **lidar2cam** from the sensor‑to‑top‑LIDAR transform (via **obtain\_sensor2top**), then compose with *camera intrinsics* to get a lidar2img matrix (to convert BEV trajectory to Camera trajectory).

```
plan_vecs = get_plan_vecs(
 plan_traj, lidar2img_rt
)
def get_plan_vecs(plan_traj, lidar2img_rt):
 ...
 plan_traj = lidar2img_rt @ plan_traj.T # convert BEV trajectory to Camera trajectory
 ...
```

- The front‑camera view (**CAM\_FRONT**) receives the same planned trajectory, but projected through **lidar2img** and drawn as green lines on the image.
- The planned trajectory (six steps) starts as *per‑step offsets* in LIDAR/ego; it's *cumulative‑summed*, lifted to homogeneous coordinates, then projected to pixels and rasterized as dense short line segments.
- Each camera pane gets a channel label.
- The six panes are arranged into 2×3 grid and horizontally concatenated with the BEV render to produce a single *widescreen* frame per sample.

- Per‑sample:

!vis.png
- Running stream (this video was run by offline visualization):

!vis_.mp4

Here is 1-min demo video visualization for SSR (latest update): 20250817\_SSR\_Visualization.mp4

---

The *inference runner* can render while iterating the data loader. Use:

```
python $WORKING_DIR/scripts/inference_visualize.py \
 --data-root /workspace/bos_model/SSR/data/dataset/nuscenes \
 --data-version v1.0-trainval \
 --max-batches 2 \
 --realtime
```

This will:

1. Load Torch and TTNN models, plus a nuScenes **DataLoader**.
2. For each batch, run TTNN and (optionally) Torch.
3. If **--realtime**, just run and format TTNN outputs and call **visualize(...)** to emit MP4 video in:

```
 /workspace/bos_model/SSR/generated/visualization/realtime/videos/
 ├── realtime_{time_stamp_1}.mp4
 ├── realtime_{time_stamp_2}.mp4
 ├── .....
 ```

Before visualizing, you must first run `inference_visualize.py` to generate the output pickle files. These files store **two separate sets of results**:

- **Torch** model outputs
- **TTNN** model outputs

```
cd /path/to/SSR/
python tools/inference_visualize.py \
 --data-root /workspace/bos_model/SSR/data/dataset/nuscenes \
 --data-version v1.0-trainval \
 --max-batches 2
```

This will save result files under:

```
/path/to/generated/visualization/SSR_e2e/{date_store}/pts_bbox/

pts_bbox/
├── torch_results_nusc.pkl
└── ttnn_results_nusc.pkl
```

Once the results are generated, run the visualization script:

```
python $WORKING_DIR/scripts/analysis_tools/visualization.py \
 --result-path /path/to/generated/visualization/SSR_e2e/{date_store}/pts_bbox \ # Folder contains pickle files output
 --save-path /path/to/generated/SSR_e2e/visualization/offline
 --data-root /path/to/data/dataset/nuscenes \
 --data-version v1.0-mini
```

**Argument:**

- `--result-path`: Path to the `.pkl` file containing the model outputs.
- `--save-path`: Directory where the visualization video will be saved
- `--data-root`: nuScenes dataset directory.

- `inference_visualize.py --realtime` enables live rendering during the TTNN run.
- Default output for the realtime demo is hardcoded to:
`/path/to/SSR/generated/test/visualization/`
- You can adapt or pass your own `out_path` when calling `visualize(...)` directly.

---

[#todo: !review and refine this]

*Time visualize:* Post-process takes 9.822845458984375e-05, and the visualize takes ~0.8s, need to more optimize for visualization.

- Writing the Matplotlib figure to PNG and reading it back with OpenCV adds blocking disk I/O and PNG (de)compression time.
- Time spikes around `fig.savefig(...)` and `cv2.imread(...)`; overall CPU low while waiting on I/O.
- Render the Matplotlib canvas directly to a NumPy array (no temp file).
- **Solved,** time visualize reduce to ~0.7-0.6 seconds and beside save the output image in `png`, save it in `jpg` and compress will decrease the output size, and execute time.

- Buffering thousands of centerline points into polygons (for lane fills) is CPU-heavy; doing it every frame repeats identical work.
- CPU pegged in Shapely during lane/connector fill; time scales with number of lanes in view.
- Cache buffered polygons once per *scene* and per *token*; per frame only do *clip to patch* and transform.
- `(token, RESOLUTION_M, LANE_HALF_WIDTH_M)` so cache invalidates when resolution/width changes.
- A few tens of MB per busy scene is typical; clear cache between scenes.

- Each Matplotlib artist has overhead (layout, z-order, antialias setup). Hundreds of tiny collections add up.
- Lots of `Axes.add_collection(...)` calls in logs/traces; profiler shows time in Matplotlib draw pipeline.
- Accumulate segments per *layer* and draw a single `LineCollection` per layer per frame.

- Smaller step → more points → heavier Shapely ops and more segments to draw, even on straight roads.
- Centerline arrays with thousands of vertices on long straights.
- Use 0.7–1.0 m on straights; keep 0.3–0.5 m only near high curvature (you can pre-classify lanes by curvature with `arcline_path_utils` and store a per-lane resolution).
- *Trade-off:* Slightly less smooth visuals on long straights; indistinguishable at normal zooms.

section_title: Sequential reads of six camera JPEGs
content:
- Blocking disk I/O for 6 large images back-to-back.
- Frame time dominated by `cv2.imread`; CPU underutilized (I/O bound).
- Threaded `imread` with a small pool (e.g., 6 workers), or prefetch next frame while rendering current.

---

- **nuScenes**: dataset access (samples, boxes, poses, calibrated sensors) and HD map API.
- **pyquaternion.Quaternion**: coordinate transforms among global/ego/sensor frames.
- **matplotlib**/**cv2**: plotting and image/video writing.
- **mmcv**, **torch**, **ttnn**: loading models, data iteration, TTNN device execution.
- **shapely**/**LineCollection**: vectorized map geometry drawing.

`visualize(bevformer_results_ttnn, out_path, nusc)`

- Iterates `sample_token`s in `bevformer_results_ttnn['plan_results_ttnn']`. For each token it renders a BEV map + ego + planned trajectory, loads six camera frames, overlays the CAM\_FRONT trajectory, composes a `2133×800` camera panel, concatenates with the `800×800` BEV to a `2933×800` visualization, writes a compressed `vis.jpg` (via `save_small`), and (optionally) appends frames to an MP4 writer (currently commented). Returns the last image path.

`render_bev_fast(nusc, nusc_map, sample_token, bevformer_results_ttnn, size=800, draw_lane_fill=False, lane_half_width_m=1.8, draw_bboxes=True)`

- High-level BEV composer. Rasterizes map layers (fills + lines), draws ego footprint, overlays the planned trajectory from `bevformer_results_ttnn` (ego-frame deltas), and optionally draws annotated 3D boxes from NuScenes. Returns an `HxWx3` BGR image (`H=W=size`).

`draw_map_fast_cv2(nusc, nusc_map, sample_token, size=800, draw_lane_fill=False, lane_half_width_m=1.8)`

`draw_map_fast_cv2(nusc, nusc_map, sample_token, size=800, draw_lane_fill=False, lane_half_width_m=1.8)`

- Fast BEV map rasterizer using OpenCV. Draws filled map layers (drivable, walkway, etc.) with alpha blending; overlays lane/connector centerlines (optional "painted" lane band), and road/stopping dividers with dashed/solid styles. Uses ego pose to set the BEV viewport. Returns a white-background BEV tile.

`traj_to_dense_uv(traj, sx, sy, size, n_segments=6, samples_per_seg=51)`

- Converts per-step ego-frame displacements `(dx,dy)` into a dense polyline in BEV pixel space: zeroes tiny deltas, cumulatively sums to positions, densifies each segment into `samples_per_seg` pieces, maps to pixel coords (`sx/sy/size`), and returns as a CV2 polyline contour.

`obtain_sensor2top(nusc, sensor_token, l2e_t, l2e_r_mat, e2g_t, e2g_r_mat, sensor_type='lidar')`

- Computes rotation/translation that maps *camera (or other sensor) coords* to the top LiDAR frame at a given sample time. Uses NuScenes calibrated sensor + ego pose records to assemble the compound transform.

`get_plan_vecs(plan_traj, lidar2img_rt)`

`get_plan_vecs(plan_traj, lidar2img_rt)`

- Projects a planned ego-frame trajectory to image space. Steps: zero small values, cumulatively sum deltas, create homogeneous `[x,y,z=-1,w=1]` points, prepend an offset seed, apply LiDAR→image transform, perspective-divide, and split into short 2-point segments with further interpolation (≈50 per segment).
`draw_ttnn_trajectory(cam_img, sample_token, cam, bevformer_results_ttnn, lidar2img_rt)`

- If `cam == 'CAM_FRONT'`, extracts the commanded trajectory from `bevformer_results_ttnn['plan_results_ttnn'][sample_token]`, projects it to CAM\_FRONT pixels (via `get_plan_vecs`), and draws the polyline in green. For other cams, it returns the image unchanged.

`save_small(img, path, fmt='webp', max_long_edge=1920, target_kb=600, quality=80)`

- Utility to quickly write compact images. Ensures 8-bit 3-channel, optional downscale to `max_long_edge`, encodes to WebP/JPEG/PNG with a small quality ladder until under `target_kb`. Returns the actual file path used and the final dimensions/size.

- *nuScenes dataroot/version*: Ensure they point to a valid dataset split; missing map layers or samples will raise errors.
- *Plan tensors on device*: If you pass GPU tensors, make sure to .cpu() before converting to NumPy for OpenCV.
- *Video codec*: cv2.VideoWriter\_fourcc('m','p','4','v') requires a compatible system codec. If MP4 writing fails, try AVI ('M','J','P','G') and .avi.
- *Performance*: Rendering every sample with dense line segments can be CPU‑bound. Reduce segment density if needed.
- *Predicted boxes*: The sample code overlays *GT boxes*. To show predicted boxes, pass your predictions through the same transform stack and draw them with a different color.

**SSR Performance Report**

The full pipeline runs in **221.3 ms**, with the **BEVEncoder** dominating the computation at **190 ms** (**85.9%** of total latency). The **Backbone** contributes **26 ms** (**11.7%**), while the **Scene Token Learner**, **Planning Decoder**, and **Trajectory module** together add only **5.3 ms** (**2.4%**). (Reference data: 20251107\_ssr\_e2e\_blocks.xlsx

| **Module** | **Device Time (ms)** | **Percentage (%)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Backbone (Resnet50 + FPN) | 26 | 11.7 |
| BEVEncoder | 190 | 85.9 |
| Scene TokenLearner + Planning Decoder + Planning Trajectory | 5.3 | 2.4 |
| **Total** | **221.3** | **100** |

**SSR Performance Report**

!memory_usage.png

- **DRAM:** Gradually increases up to ~1.8 MiB due to intermediate tensor spill, but remains relatively low overall.
- **L1:** Moderate repeated spikes (~8 MiB), mainly from ResNet50 convolution outputs.
- **Observation:** Efficient L1 utilization with limited DRAM pressure. No persistent memory buildup.

- **Peak Allocation:** ~9.77 MiB in both DRAM and L1.
- **Major Contributors:**
`ttnn.to_memory_config`, `ttnn.concat`, `ttnn.linear`, `ttnn.layer_norm`, `ttnn.reshape`
- **Behavior:** Frequent large spikes caused by temporary tensor materialization (e.g., attention maps, BEV grids, concat outputs).
- **Observation:** Most memory-intensive stage; dominated by large transient tensors rather than cumulative growth.

- Sharp drop in both DRAM and L1 usage.
- Only small, sporadic allocations remain.
- Indicates transition to lighter post-processing/decoding stages.

This section divided into 2 major bottleneck blocks including Backbone and BEV Encoder; common patterns are listed in the last subsection.

| **Category** | **Items** | **Key Issues / Actions** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Memory Layout & Op Config** | Backbone (`ResNet50 + FPN`) | - Residual connection, Maxpool2d, Conv2d 3x3 with high L1 peak. - Dimension conversion in the first Conv2d adds extra data movement. - Large input increase DRAM access, `ttnn.Conv2dSliceConfig` required in `ttnn.conv2d` or apply **batch splitting** method |
| Encoder (`BEVEncoder`) | - **Point sampling:** Many **DRAM ops** (e.g. matmul with large input) around this block. - **Long-life large intermediate tensors** at transformer layers (e.g. sampling locations, image features, etc.). - **Novel optimizations:** 1. Optimum no. of valid BEV queries (nonzero) 2. SSR Deformable Attention |
| Other Blocks (`TokenLeaner`, `PlanningDecoder`, `PlanningTrajectory`) | - Memory usage and runtime is the lowest. - Not main bottleneck blocks. |
| **Op Fusing** | TTNNFusing | - Many fusing patterns are required. |
| **Support new ops** | TTIR/TTNN dialects | - More than **10 ops** are not supported by TT-MLIR. - `ttnn.bos_ssr_deformable_attention` is considered as a fusing op specified for SSR operation patterns. |

| **Memory Management** | Fragmentation (Global) | Many long-lived intermediate tensors leading to **memory** **fragmentation** ***→ topic to discuss*** |
| **Control Flow** | SSR Implementation | Contains dynamic control flow; must do more research (MLIR Language Reference ***→ topic to discuss*** |

> Optimization strategy for memory layout is: "**Maximize the locality (L1 usage), reduce data movement (DRAM access)**".

![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Forge on Resnet50 with input shape [6, 3, 640, 384]

**SSR Performance Report**

We don't have a way to export this macro.

Below bottleneck patterns are referenced from ResNet50 - Model Decomposition:

1. **Dimension conversion for first Conv2d:** `ttnn.conv2d` requires input as in `NWHC`, a dimension conversion with large activation that cannot fit into L1 memory will hurt the model performance.
2. **Residual joins:** The fork-join structure creates repeated *L1 tensor peak usage* across 16 bottleneck blocks.
3. **Conv3x3 layers:** Their *spatial compute* and *halo exchange* behavior make them highest *circular buffer usage*.

Below bottlenecks exist for SSR Resnet50 only:

Below bottlenecks exist for SSR Resnet50 only:

4. **High frequency of DRAM access:** Due to the large input shape, if we let the input as it original shape, a sequential of multiple ops might perform *DRAM input - DRAM output*. We have 2 levels to optimize this:
**- Level 1 - Local Optimization (Layout and Op Config Propagation):**DRAM slice per op via slice config if supported (e.g. ttnn.Conv2dSliceConfig. With this slice config, runtime library will automatically slice the DRAM input tensors into L1 slices, output slice will write back to DRAM if not fit on L1. **- Level 2 - Global Optimization (Batch Tiling):***loop optimization* using batch tiling across multiple ops.

![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)What is Loop Optimization

> ![](https://bos-semi.atlassian.net/wiki/plugins/servlet/confluence/placeholder/error?i18nKey=editor.placeholder.error&locale=en_US&version=2 "Cannot invoke \"String.indexOf(int)\" because \"href\" is null") Loop Optimization**:** transforms nested loops to iterate over smaller blocks, improving spatial and temporal locality, which reduces the need to fetch data from slow main memory.
>
> In a deep learning computational graph, we can view this as **batch tiling**:
>
> - Step 1: **split** the *DRAM input* tensor by its *batch* into *L1 slices*.
> - Step 2: for each *L1 slice,* feed through a same *subgraph* of model where the**locality is maximized,**execute each slice in a **sequential or parallel (if mesh device) fashion** and write to *output slices*.
> - Step 3: **concat** the *output slices* across some dimensions to produce the final *output* as desired.
>
> An extension to this method is to execute them in a **distributed parallel fashion** in step 2.
>
> We don't have a way to export this macro.
>
> **Read more:** Batch Tiling on Attention

5. **Concat after Batch Tiling**: after applying above technique, *some concats have potential to perform on L1*, we must handle this properly through memory pressure tracing.

```
 /-> maxpool2d -> to_layout(DRAM) -\
 conv1 -> slice(DRAM) -< >-> concat(DRAM)
 \-> maxpool2d -> to_layout(DRAM) -/
 ```

| **Pattern (Before Fusion)** | **Fused Form** | **TT-MLIR supported** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Conv2d → ReLU` | `ttnn.conv2d(activation='relu')` | ✅ | ReLU fused into convolution kernel to eliminate intermediate tensor. |
| `Conv2d → BatchNorm` = `Conv2d → Multiply → Add` | (weights/bias folded) | ✅ | BatchNorm parameters pre-folded into conv weights and bias at compile time (inference fusion) |
| `Add → ReLU` | `ttnn.add(activations=[RELU])` | ✅ (BOS) | ReLU fused into add kernel to avoid extra allocation and memory movement |

**a. Matmul in point sampling** perform for reference point on DRAM since input is too large `[4, 1, 5, 10000, 4]`. Ref: encoder.py

| **Tensor Name** | **Shape (Runtime)** | **Lifetime** | **Used In** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Previous BEV Embedding** | `[1, 10000, 256]` | Entire encoder forward + reused next forward | Temporal self-attn | Persistent state across frames |
| **BEV Query** | `[1, 10000, 256]` | Entire encoder forward | All transformer layers | Core BEV feature map |
| **Key** | `[6, 240, 256]` | Entire encoder forward | Cross-attention | Multi-camera features |
| **Values** | `[6, 240, 256]` | Entire encoder forward | Cross-attention | Split camera groups |
| **BEV Positional Encoding** | `[1, 10000, 256]` | Entire encoder forward | Self-attention | Positional encoding |
| **Reference points 2D** | `[1, 10000, 128]` | Entire encoder forward | Self-attention | 2D sampling reference |
| **Reference points 3D** | `[10, <=10000, 128]` | Entire encoder forward | Cross-attention | Sparse 3D sampling points |
| **BEV Mask** | `[6, 1, 10000, 4]` | Entire encoder forward | Cross-attention | Camera validity mask |

3. **Novel Optimizations:**
**a. Maximal optimum number of valid BEV queries** **Problem:** In **spatial cross-attention**, valid BEV queries per camera are obtained via `nonzero`, but TT-Metal requires static output shapes. Since ttnn.nonzero returns the same shape as its input, this causes redundant memory allocation, reducing memory efficiency and impacting downstream ops like deformable attention. **Solution:** To address this bottleneck, a statistical approach was adopted to estimate an upper bound on the expected non-zero output size in **offline**, **prior to model execution**. A custom operator, `ttnn.bos_nonzero`, is introduced to control this bounded shape allocation. **References:** Optimum Query Search Space Method, Optimum fixed non-zero outputs' size

We don't have a way to export this macro.

We don't have a way to export this macro.

**b. Deformable Attention** **- DRAM Sampling Locations:** This op is heavy memory-bounded due to large **sampling locations** with maximum size can be up-to`100x100xC`(mostly at Spatial Cross Attention). Without above method applied, **this sampling locations should be placed on DRAM**. **- Interleaved Image Features causes dataflow across cores:** due to the nature design of this operation, image **cannot be sharded but interleaved**, reduce locality, increase dataflow along NOCs during feature sampling.
