In a hardware–software architecture that includes a Host PC and an NPU connected via PCIe, software at multiple levels (libraries, core runtime system, driver, low-level kernels, etc.) is required for the system to operate optimally.

!image-20260119-035445.png

High-level NN ops built (convolution, matrix multiplication…) on top of TT-Metalium, abstracts device-specific complexities and provides a tensor-based programming interface with automatic sharding, parallelization and memory management.

---

Tensor:

- Shape: Multi-dimensional (1 → 8) shape (with padding support).
- Data Type: bfloat16, bfloat8\_b, bfloat4\_b, float32, uint32, etc.
- Layout: Support Row major layout and Tile layout
- Memory Config: Location L1/DRAM and sharding configuration

 - Sharding configuration: Currently sharding only support L1 tensors. Tensor will be split across a distributed space. TTNN supports three primary sharding strategies:

 - Height sharded: Used when parallelizing over batch/spatial dimensions
 - Width sharded: Used when parallelizing over channel dimension.
 - Block sharded: Used for 2D parallelization across both spatial and channel dimmension.

---

MLIR (Multi level intermediate representation)-based compiler repository coordinate many sub-projects (TT-XLA, TT-Forge-FE), TT-MLIR compiler. Implements a classic compiler stack architecture: Model (framework layer), frontend ingestion (frontend layer), intermediate compilation (compiler layer), hardware execution

!image-20260119-043848.png

- Recommended framework: ONNX, TensorFlow, PaddlePaddle
- Support single-chip only

!aa4b9f84-5ee8-437b-80e7-b98906505ec4.png

- Recommended framework: Pytorch, JAX
- Support both single-chip and multi-chip

!image-20260119-063139.png

Deprecated !(blue star)

- Only support old version PyTorch 2.x, ONNX.

!image-20260119-063600.png

---

A MLIR based compiler infrastructure provides a high-level summary of the compilation flow from ML framework to hardware execution through three major stages:

- Frontend Lowering: StableHLO → TTIR
- Backend Lowering and Optimization: TTIR → TTNN
- Code generation → Flatbuffer Binary (.ttnn), EmitC (C++ source), EmitPy (Python Source)

---

No longer active development !(blue star) Maybe because BUDA is execution-centric and single-chip oriented and limited graph-level optimization and scalability.

Translating model from many popular frameworks (Pytorch Tensorflow, ONNX) to optimized executables. Follows a multi-stage compilation pipeline:

- Front end compilation: PyTorch/TensorFlow models → PyBuda module
- Graph Generation: Creates initial computational graph.
- Graph Optimization: Applies passes like pattern matching, op fusion, …
- Balancing: Selects optimal op models and grid shapes.
- Placement: Assign op to location on hardware grid.
- Netlist Generation: Create hardware specific representation (netlist).
- Backend Compilation: Netlist → executable image.

---

It is a low-level programming framework aimed at accelerating both ML and non-ML workloads on hardware, acting as a middleware layer between the host system and the hardware. It provides a C++ API for writing kernels that run on Tensix, configuring and executing kernels, controlling data movement, SRAM/DRAM usage, NoC communication, and execution order.

!image-20260119-023644.png

---

Device class manage device life cycle - 4 steps:

- Initialization
- Command Queue Setup: Create queue for host side and device ( queue memory for host and command queue for device)
- Fabric Initialization
- Closure

---

A physical chip is managed by Device class while MeshDevice wrap multi Device instances and provides a unified device interface which apply operations to all devices in the mesh. Device ids will be mapped with coordinates. MeshDevice class will fast dispatch across mesh through distributed command queues.

---

Just-In-Time (JIT) build system compiles device kernels and firmware for TT-Metal hardware. The system dynamically compiles RISC-V code for different processor types (Tensix, Ethernet, etc.) based on runtime configuration and caches compiled binaries to avoid redundant compilation. This page covers the build environment setup, compilation process, caching mechanisms, and integration with the runtime system.

---

In compilation process, op placement (why it here?)

- Temporal: All available resources used by one op, ops are placed one after the other.

 - Pro: Ops run fast
 - Cons:

 - High DRAM overhead in read/write (5 ops → need to read/write 5 times)

 - If have to, please use SRAM/L1 because they fast
 - Reconfigure between ops

 - If have to, please use fast dispatch.
 - Small ops waste resource

 - If have to, please fusing ops.
- Spatial: Ops got fixed resources according to their size and run as a pipeline

 - Pro: Local mem reuse because intermediate data travels on NoC, high utilization of resource
 - Cons:

- Pro: Local mem reuse because intermediate data travels on NoC, high utilization of resource
 - Cons:

 - Large models require large number of devices.
 - Pipeline overhead for fill/drain (place ops, complete ops)
- Hybrid: Model is divided into epochs, each epoch fits available hardware.

 - Pro: Fits hardware, high utilization
 - Cons: Complicated.

---

The dispatch system uses three circular queues in host huge-page memory to manage command flow between host and device.

- Issue Queue: contains the actual command data written by the host.
- Fetch Queue: parallel data structure, stores the size of each command in the Issue Queue.
- Completion Queue: enables the device to signal back to the host when operations complete.

**Slow dispatch**: Host directly writes launch message worker cores. Simple, high latency.

- Slow dispatch forces the host CPU to actively manage all operations
- the CPU must wait for each operation to complete before proceeding to the next instruction.

**Fast dispatch**: Host writes to issue queue and prefetcher/dispatcher process commands. Low latency.

**Fast dispatch**: Host writes to issue queue and prefetcher/dispatcher process commands. Low latency.

!image-20260120-080642.png

---

It tracks DRAM and on-device SRAM (L1) memory, assigns addresses to buffers by initialized class. This allows buffers to be split into low-address regions (bottom-up) for heavy data such as user data, and high-address regions (top-down) for lighter objects such as program binaries and SRAM buffers. This approach helps reduce memory fragmentation caused by mixing large and small allocations.

---

TT-Metalium's behavior is controlled by 80+ environment variables, which is included Watcher system, debugging print, hardware config, etc…

---

It provides a unified C++ API for single-chip and multi-chip systems, enabling applications to communicate with the chips using a cluster-centric model that treats the entire system as a single logical device. This abstracts away hardware complexity, which is handled by the TT-KMD layer.

---

DPRINT supports host-side print or file logging through API calls on device to show variables, addresses, Circular buffer data.

---

The Watcher consists of two components:

- instruments the firmware and kernels to catch common programming errors.

 - If a programming error is encountered, the Watcher stops the program and reports the error.
- launches a thread on the host which periodically monitors the status of the device.

 - If a hang occurs, the data in the log shows the state of the device at the time of the hang.

Watcher functionality includes:

- Logged "waypoints" indicate which piece of code last executed on each RISC V
- Sanitized NOC transactions prevent transactions with invalid coordinates or addresses from being submitted to the hardware and stops the offending RISC V via a soft hang
- Memory corruption detection at address 0 of L1
- Kernel paths and names of the currently executing kernel
- Flags which indicate which RISC Vs are executing in the current kernel

---

It is a Linux kernel driver that enables user space to communicate with Tenstorrent hardware. The driver automatically discovers devices through the PCI subsystem and, upon detection, creates a character device (e.g., `/dev/tenstorrent/N`), which serves as the user-space interface for accessing the hardware.

---

Although it is referred to as a low-level kernel, within the NPU it is considered a software layer, as it encapsulates operations on Tensix to improve efficiency and reusability. The kernel takes L1 memory addresses as input and produces L1 memory addresses as output and participates in two main kernel types: compute kernels and data movement kernels. TT-LLK provided:

- **Software interface** to program computer kernel and data-movement kernel.
- **Kernel Abstraction** encapsulates unpack, compute, pack stages into reusable building blocks.
- **Control and scheduling** data movement between DRAM, L1 and neighboring Tensix core.
- **Synchronization primitives** coordinate compute pipelines and data transfers between L1 memory and multiple RISC cores, as well as across the RISC cores themselves.

---

section_title: 1. Tensor Data Layout: Tile vs. Row-Major
content: Before talking about hardware, we must understand **how data (tensors) are laid out in memory**.

- Used by C/C++ and most CPUs
- Data is stored **row by row**, continuously in memory
- Example: one full row is stored, then the next row, and so on

!image-20260209-045208.png

**Why this is not ideal for accelerators:**

- Hardware often wants to process **blocks of data at once**, not single elements
- Accessing small pieces scattered across memory leads to inefficient bandwidth usage
- Memory access becomes the bottleneck instead of computation

---

- Data is organized into **fixed-size tiles**, typically **32 × 32 elements**
- Each tile is a compact block that hardware can process efficiently

!image-20260209-060228.png

**Intuition:**

- The hardware is designed to compute on one tile at a time

**Padding and Redundancy:**

- If tensor dimensions are not multiples of 32, unused space appears
- This space is filled with zeros or duplicated values (padding)
- This is **not a weakness**, but a trade-off that enables simpler and faster hardware

!image-20260119-034012.png

**Key idea:**

> Tenstorrent trades a small amount of extra storage for much higher compute efficiency.

---

section_title: 2. Where Data Is Stored: DRAM vs. SRAM
content: Now that we know **how** data is laid out, let's see **where** it lives.

- Large capacity (typically **8–16 GB**)
- Lower bandwidth and higher latency
- Shared by the entire NPU chip
- Accessed through multiple DRAM controllers

- Much smaller (about **1–2 MB per Tensix core** )
- Extremely high bandwidth and low latency
- Each Tensix core has **its own private L1 SRAM**

---

- The host CPU communicates with the NPU card via **PCIe**
- Data is typically:

1. Loaded from host memory
2. Placed into NPU DRAM
3. Moved into L1 SRAM (in Tensix Core) for computation

!data_flow_from_cpu-20260121-042041.png

The following image shows the NoC grid of the Tenstorrent Wormhole™ processor (D = DRAM, T = Tensix, E = Ethernet, A = ARC/management, P = PCIe).

---

- Performance comes from **massive multithreading**
- Thousands of threads execute the same instruction

- Performance comes from **explicit data movement** and **structured parallelism**
- The chip is organized as a **grid of Tensix cores**
- Each core works relatively independently with its own local memory

**Key idea:**

> GPUs hide latency with threads; NPUs eliminate latency with data locality and scheduling.

---

section_title: 4. Tensix Core: The Fundamental Building Block
content:
Each Tensix core contains:

- **5 small RISC-V "baby" cores**
- ~ **1.5 MB L1 SRAM**
- **2 Network-on-Chip (NoC) interfaces**

There is:

- One shared DRAM for the chip
- Many Tensix cores, each with private L1

---

!image-20260119-034354.png

Instead of one complex CPU, Tenstorrent splits work into **different classes of tasks**:

- Data movement
- Computation
- Packing and unpacking data formats

These tasks are:

- Mostly independent
- Able to run in parallel

This reduces waiting and improves throughput.

---

Inside each Tensix core, computation is organized into **kernels**:

- Read kernels
- Compute kernels
- Write kernels

Each kernel runs on a specific baby core.

---

- Data in L1 is accessed via **circular buffers**
- Naturally FIFO (first-in, first-out)
- Ideal for producer–consumer patterns between cores

Users can define **up to 32 circular buffers**:

- `c0 – c15`: typically inputs
- `c16 – c31`: typically outputs

!image-20260119-034450.png

---

section_title: 6. The Five Baby Cores and Their Roles
content:
A simplified functional split:

1. **Data Move Core 0**

Moves data from L1 SRAM into registers

2. **Data Move Core 1**

Moves data between memory structures or NoC

3. **Math Core**

Performs numerical computation

4. **Unpack Core**

Converts data from memory format into compute-ready format

5. **Pack Core**

Converts compute results back into storage format

---

!image-20260119-034525.png

1. Load tile from DRAM into L1 SRAM
2. Unpack tile into registers
3. Perform math operations
4. Pack results
5. Write results back to L1 or DRAM

All of this happens **in a pipelined and parallel fashion** (While a baby is doing this, other babies can do their own task...).

---

Instructions are broadly divided into:

- **`cfgexec` (configuration execution)**

Controls compute engines (unpack → math → pack)

- **`syncexec` (synchronization execution)**

Handles background tasks and coordination

This separation allows computation and coordination to overlap.

---

!image-20260119-034607.png

- Optimized for **tile-based 2D tensor operations**
- Used for:

 - Matrix multiplication
 - Pooling
 - Reductions (sum, mean, etc.)
 - Uses MAC units and partial sums
 - Maximizes data reuse without reloading

**Key idea:**

> One instruction triggers a full, fixed dataflow computation on a tile.

---

!image-20260119-034708.png

Used for **element-wise and special functions**:

- ReLU, GELU, exp, log, etc.
- SIMD-style engine
- 32 lanes total
- Organized as 4 rows × 8 lanes
- Single instruction controls all lanes
- Operates on registers for fast local computation

---

From smallest and fastest to largest and slowest:

1. **Registers**

Each RISC-V core contains 32×32-bit general-purpose registers (GPRs) that are directly used by the core for instruction execution. These are the fastest storage, accessible within a single cycle by the executing core, and are used for operand storage, address calculations, and intermediate results during RISC-V instruction execution.

2. **Register Files**

- The Tensix coprocessor contains specialized register files for AI computation:
- SrcA and SrcB: 64×16 (equal a 32 x 32 tile) entries each, 19-bit precision, serving as input registers for the Matrix Unit
- Dst: 1024×16 entries, 16/32-bit precision, serving as accumulator registers shared across execution units

> These register files are larger than GPRs but smaller than L1, optimized for the parallel data access patterns required by matrix and vector operations.

- SFPU have SIMD architecture using (a single) LReg. A LReg has 32 lanes and it is considered as 17 registers per lane. 8 of them are used for general purpose (LReg[0]..LReg[7]). A LReg is like a 2D array of register with lane index (17 x 32 = 544). Using for general compute purpose: 8 x 32 = 256.

3. **L1 SRAM**

Shared by the 5 baby cores within one Tensix core

4. **DRAM**

Shared by all Tensix cores on the chip

---

- Unlike CPU L1 cache:
- No automatic cache hits/misses
- No hardware-managed replacement
- All data movement is **explicitly controlled by software**

- A Tensix core can reuse its local L1 data across multiple operations
- Efficient data placement dramatically improves performance

- Data can remain in SRAM across operations
- Avoids repeated DRAM access
- Reduces bandwidth pressure and latency

---

- Multiple DRAM controllers exist
- Each controller manages a **distinct slice of DRAM**
- No two controllers access the same memory block

This parallelism improves throughput and avoids contention.

---

- Tenstorrent is built around **tile-based computation**
- Performance comes from:
- Explicit data movement
- Local SRAM usage
- Specialized compute engines
- The architecture favors **predictability and efficiency** over general-purpose flexibility

This design is particularly well-suited for modern ML and AI workloads.

!image-20260119-034903.png
> Note: CfgExu perform config task: address, stride, size, format,... while SyncExu build the schedule (synchonize). This 2 execution unit control TDMA to perfomr data movement. Data computation is handle in the compute engine: FPU and SFPU.

---

section_title: 13. Tenstorrent and BOS Products: Tensix Neo Core
content:
So far, we have described the **classic Tensix core** as the fundamental building block. Modern Tenstorrent and BOS products extend this idea further with the concept of **clusters** and the **Tensix Neo Core**.

This section connects the earlier concepts to real products and recent architectures.

---

section_title: 13.1 Motivation: Why Move Beyond a Single Tensix Core?
content:
In earlier designs, each Tensix core:

- Owns its own L1 SRAM
- Handles data movement, compute, and packing internally

This works very well, but as models grow:

- Data movement between cores becomes more expensive
- Some workloads are dominated by matrix math, others by vector math

**Key insight:**

> Not every part of the workload needs the same type of compute or control.

---

A **cluster** is a group of closely connected cores that:

- Share data more efficiently
- Coordinate computation at a finer granularity
- Reduce unnecessary data movement across the chip

Instead of treating every Tensix core as fully independent, clustering allows:

- Faster local communication
- Better reuse of intermediate results
- Higher overall throughput

You can think of a cluster as:

> A small neighborhood of cores that collaborate tightly before talking to the rest of the chip.

---

The **Tensix Neo Core** evolves the original Tensix idea by **separating responsibilities more clearly**.

!image-20260119-035004.png

Rather than each core doing everything, the Neo design emphasizes:

- **Matrix-focused compute cores**
- **Vector-focused compute cores**
- Optimized data-movement paths between them

This matches modern ML workloads, where:

- Large matrix multiplications dominate attention layers
- Vector operations dominate activation functions, normalization, and element-wise logic

---

section_title: 13.4 Matrix Engine Cores vs Vector Engine Cores
content:
Within a cluster:

- **Matrix Engine Cores**

 - Specialize in tile-based matrix math
 - Run high-throughput MAC operations
 - Maximize data reuse inside the matrix pipeline
- **Vector Engine Cores**

 - Specialize in SFPU-style operations
 - Handle activations (ReLU, GELU, exp, log, etc.)
 - Execute SIMD-style vector instructions efficiently

By separating these roles:

- Each core is simpler and more efficient
- Less control logic is duplicated
- Data flows directly to where it is needed

---

Clustering reduces data movement in three main ways:

1. **Locality of Intermediate Results**

Partial results stay inside the cluster instead of going back to DRAM

2. **Specialized Pipelines**

Matrix results flow directly into vector engines without repacking

3. **Fewer Cross-Chip Transfers**

Only final or high-level outputs leave the cluster

**Key idea:**

> Move computation to data, not data to computation.

---

Earlier in this seminar, we introduced:

- Tile-based data layout
- Explicit L1 SRAM usage
- Separate matrix (FPU) and vector (SFPU) engines

Clusters and Tensix Neo cores are a **natural extension** of these ideas:

- Tiles still define the unit of compute
- SRAM locality is pushed even further
- Data movement is more structured and predictable

---

section_title: A Tenstorrent chip is composed of several major subsystems:
content:
- **Tensix cores**: the main compute engines used for tensor and vector operations.
- **PCIe interface**: connects the chip to the host CPU for control-plane communication.
- **DRAM (GDDR) controllers**: provide off-chip memory bandwidth.
- **Ethernet interfaces**: enable direct chip-to-chip communication.
- **NoC (Network-on-Chip)**: the internal communication fabric connecting all on-chip components.

!image-20260119-074829.png

---

The **NoC** is the internal routing fabric of the chip. All on-chip components — Tensix cores, SRAM, DRAM controllers, PCIe, and Ethernet — are connected through the NoC.

- The NoC handles **all internal data movement**.
- Ethernet is **built on top of the NoC** as a NoC-attached endpoint.
- Tensix cores do **not** access Ethernet directly; all Ethernet traffic is injected and received through the NoC.

In short:

- **NoC = on-chip routing fabric**
- **Ethernet = off-chip forwarding fabric**

Ethernet is used exclusively for **inter-chip communication**, while the NoC handles both:

- data movement *inside* the chip
- data injection to / extraction from Ethernet.

---

Although a Tenstorrent chip contains **many physical Ethernet interfaces**, each Ethernet interface has a **static output connection** to a neighboring chip.

!image-20260119-074917.png

Logically, Ethernet links are grouped into **four abstract directions**:

- **East (E)**
- **West (W)**
- **North (N)**
- **South (S)**

Important notes:

- These directions describe **topology and data movement**, not physical placement on the die.
- Ethernet blocks are physically clustered for layout and power reasons.
- The logical direction (E/W/N/S) is assigned by board wiring and configuration, not by physical orientation.

Routing is **static and deterministic**:

- Paths are fixed by topology.
- Traffic patterns change dynamically based on workload.

---

Because Ethernet is designed as a **direct chip-to-chip fabric**, scaling does not require:

- external Ethernet switches
- NICs
- hierarchical network layers

!image-20260119-075004.png

A **Galaxy server** connects chips into a fixed **8 × 4 grid**:

- 32 Tenstorrent chips per server
- Roughly equivalent to an **8-GPU class server** in intent

Chips at the edges of the grid expose their Ethernet ports externally, allowing servers to connect directly to each other.

---

Multiple Galaxy servers can be connected together using the same Ethernet fabric:

- Each server acts as a **cell in a larger grid**
- No distinction between "inside a server" and "between servers"
- Results in a **flat mesh / torus topology**

This design enables scalable performance without introducing network switches or bottlenecks.

---

Within the chip:

- **NoC communication between on-chip components is bidirectional**
- **Chip-to-chip communication via Ethernet is directional per hop**

Routing decisions (e.g., E → S turns) occur at the **chip level**, not inside Ethernet hardware.

---

Blackhole introduces a **general-purpose RISC-V control core** ("R core") that performs chip-level orchestration:

- Fabric and Ethernet management
- Routing table programming
- Runtime coordination
- Error handling and monitoring
- Reduced dependency on host CPU

This significantly reduces control traffic over PCIe and improves scalability.

Because of the R core:

- **All Tensix cores are available for compute**
- No Tensix cores are reserved for system tasks

---

Blackhole adds two additional Ethernet directions:

- **Up**
- **Down**

This extends the fabric from:

- **2D topology (E/W/N/S)**
to:
- **3D topology (E/W/N/S/Up/Down)**

A 3D mesh or torus:

- Reduces hop count
- Improves bisection bandwidth
- Scales better for large clusters

---

- **80 Tensix cores physically**
- **72 Tensix cores available for user workloads**
- **8 Tensix cores reserved for system / fabric helper tasks**
- No dedicated on-chip control CPU
- Heavily dependent on host CPU over PCIe for orchestration
- Supports **2D topology only (E/W/N/S)**

---

- NoC is the internal routing backbone; Ethernet is a NoC-attached off-chip fabric.
- Ethernet directions are logical abstractions, not physical layout constraints.
- Static routing + scalable fabric enables switchless multi-chip systems.
- Blackhole improves scalability with a dedicated control CPU and 3D topology support.
- Wormhole relies on reserved Tensix cores and host orchestration, limiting scalability.

---

From classic Tensix cores to Tensix Neo clusters:

- The direction is toward **less data movement**
- **More specialization** in compute engines
- **Stronger locality** and collaboration between cores

All of this serves one goal:

> Deliver predictable, scalable performance for modern AI workloads.

Các câu lệnh trong CPU được thực hiện theo trình tự: Fetch → Decode → Execute. Lặp đi lặp lại. Vậy làm sao thứ tự thực hiện được duy trì? Người ta sẽ dùng clock:

Clock có thể tạo ra nhiều loại sóng, tuy nhiên ở đây ví dụ với square wave:

- bao gồm hai giá trị 0 hoặc 1. Khi giá trị giữ nguyên, hệ thống không có gì để phản ứng.
- Khi một giá trị thay đổi ta có một cạnh (edge) sẽ được edge detector phát hiện.

 - Rising edge: 0 → 1
 - Failling edge: 1 → 0
- Một clock cycle sẽ là giữa 2 lần rising edge.

!image-20260118-025652.png

Với cách thiết kế cùng flip flop sẽ có thể tạo ra một bộ đếm nhị phân xoay vòng: 00 → 01 → 10 → 11 → 00 … Từ đó có thể sử dụng để quản lý trình tự thực hiện trong các câu lệnh

!image-20260118-084202.png

Khi điện đi đến A, giá trị điện tại B bị delay một khoảng (bởi đi qua 3 cục phủ định hay 6 cục transistor) <=> Output sau một khoảng delay sẽ thay đổi, tùy vào cấu hình output có failing hay rising edge detector.

Tuân theo thiết kế bộ xử lý đi kèm với tập nhiều các câu lệnh điều khiển cho phép thực hiện nhiều phép toán trong một lệnh. Vd: load data → calculate → store. Mục tiêu để giảm số instructions một chương trình cần, từ đó việc lập trình dễ hơn. Ví dụ: x86

!image-20260117-082640.png

Trong thực tế, bộ nhớ sẽ có dữ liệu đến và đi, quá trình này tạo ra các ô dữ liệu không được sử dụng. Các memory allocation methods là phương pháp cách chọn vị trí để lưu dữ liệu có size n vào bộ nhớ.

- First-fit: Selects the first memory block whose size is greater than *n*.

 - Pros: Fast, Easy to implement
 - Cons: Causes external fragmentation (memory is split into many small, non-contiguous blocks)
 - Suitable for: Systems that prioritize speed.
- Best-fit: Selects the smallest memory block whose size is greater than *n*.

 - Pros: Minimizes wasted space for each allocation
 - Cons: Slow (must search through all blocks), Creates many small fragments that are hard to reuse
 - Suitable for: Systems that focus on memory efficiency and have a low number of allocation requests.
- Worst-fit: Selects the largest memory block whose size is greater than *n*.

 - Pros: Keeps medium-sized blocks available for future requests
 - Cons: Wastes memory, Low efficiency
 - Usage: Rarely used in practice 🙂

---

- Tensors are stored in a 2D memory space known as a `buffer`.
- An N-dimensional tensor is represented as a 2D memory object by combining the outer dimensions into a single dimension while keeping the inner dimension as the second dimension.

Example: a tensor with dimensions `[2x3x2x4]` will be represented in memory as a `[12x4]` tensor, where 2x3x2 is squeezed into 12.

!image-20251231-071012.png

At the most granular level, the smallest blocks that constructs of a tensor are call **pages**.

**Page** is the **smallest unit** of a Tensor handled by TT-software

!image-20251231-071950.png

Tensors are allocated in Tenstorrent device by different hierarchical concepts:

- **Memory banks**: **DRAM** and **L1 buffer**.
- **Memory layouts:** **Interleaved** and **Sharded**.
- **Tensor layouts**: **Row-Major** and **Tile layout**.

---

- One bank per DRAM channel
- Example architectures:

 - Wormhole: 12 banks (~1GB each)
 - Blackhole: 8 banks (~4GB each)
- A portion of each bank is reserved for synchronization barriers

SRAM banks differ depending on the core type:

!image-20260122-041643.png

- **Worker (Compute) Core:**

 - 1 SRAM (large) bank per core
- **Storage Core**

 - `N` smaller SRAM banks
 - `N = SRAM_per_core / storage_core_bank_size`

---

This describes how *elements* within a tensor are mapped to pages, with each page representing the granularity of the tensor in memory. Pages are stored consecutively within a buffer. Depending on tensor layout, page has different sizes:

- **Row-Major layout:** page\_size = 1 \* W (one stick)
- **Tile layout**: page\_size = TILE\_SIZE (currently support size of 32x32)

Each row of a 2D tensor corresponds to a single page in our buffer.

e.g. with a 64x64 tensor, each row is associated with a separate page, resulting in a total of 64 pages in the buffer.

!image-20250602-014903.png

In a tiled tensor, pages are represented as 2D tiles, with the default tile size being 32x32. For example, with a 64x64 tensor, this results in 4 tiles, each of size 32x32.

!image-20250602-015121.png

The hardware architecture supports tile shapes of `32x32` , `16x32` , `4x32`, `2x32`, `1x32`. **However currently TT-Metalium only supports 32x32, with limited functionality for 16x32 in some ops such as matmul.**

section_title: 3.2.1 Faces and Tile Organization in HW-Level
content:
Data inside the tile **isn't contiguous**. Each tile is split into faces ("sub-tiles"). By default, tile size is 32x32, and face size is 16x16. 4 faces per tile and each tile lies one after another contiguously in memory in row-major fashion (i.e., face0->face1->face2->face3).

The reason for using faces is that **the matrix engine natively multiplies 16x16 matrices**, and tile multiplication is decomposed into multiple face multiplications.

!image-20260122-081020.png

---

By spreading memory addresses evenly across memory banks. Using round-robin, with n banks, memory location i resides in bank i mod n. This led to:

- Contiguous memory reads and writes- using each memory bank in turn, instead of using the same one repeatedly -> reduced waiting for memory banks to become ready for the operations.

!Interleaving.gif

Ex: Interleaved memory layout 12 pages across 16 cores

!image-20250601-113417.png

However, interleaved memory does not always provide optimal performance for all kernels. It can lead to bottlenecks since Although data is located in short time, but it need to move to another location for computing → Long data movement + bottlenecks on a specific buffer (buffer used here instead of bank in case data is moved to DRAM).

!image-20260122-063654.png

Sharded memory is used for kernels that require more control over data placement and access patterns. Sharded memory allows you to specify which tiles should store which data flexing between these schemes:

Ex: 3 x 4 pages need to be allocated

!image-20260122-071552.png

Data is partitioned across tiles based on the *height dimension* → All page in row-oriented must be respectively placed in the same core.

!image-20260122-072323.png

!image-20260122-070323.png

Data is partitioned across tiles based on the *width dimension* → All page in col-oriented must be respectively placed in the same core.

!image-20250601-135305.png

Data is partitioned across tiles on both width and height dimensions → All pages in one block must be in the same core (missing pages will be padding with 0):

!image-20260122-071905.png

ND sharding preserves the original tensor dimensionality and can shard across any combination of dimensions (batch, sequence length, channels, etc.) Interactive Visualization

**Example**: 3D Sharding

!image-20260122-073627.png

Sometimes, data could be evenly divided between cores so padding with 0 strategy is used, this section is intentionally placed here to make the document longer.

!image-20260122-075012.png

---

- Unary operations: relu(), sin(), cos(), …
- Elementwise binary operations: x^2, a+b, …
- Tensor manipulation operations

 - Resharding
 - Permutation
 - Concatenation
 - Splitting

Can be executed locally *without needing full data movement* if layout is compatible and data is already on SRAM

**Example**: Tensor A [1, 1, 128, 96] is tile layout, height sharded (shape [32, 96]) on 4x4 grid row major performs a unary operation (power 2) results output with same memory configuration.

!image-20260122-062914.png

=> For unary operation, tensor can be either sharded or interleaved, since the **unary operation is layout-invariant**, that is the computation does not depend on how data is placed across banks

With L1 sharded tensor layout, eltwise binary operation takes 2 tensors and performs computation directly *without any data movement*.

**Example**: Tensor A already lives in L1, move Tensor B to L1 and then add C = A + B.

!image-20250601-141148.png

In certain scenarios, it's necessary to **change the shard configuration** of a tensor — for example, converting a tensor from **width-sharded** to **height-sharded** layout.

- **Resharding** involves **data movement** and **communication between cores**.
- It is required when the current shard layout is incompatible with the needs of a downstream operation or kernel.

**Example**: width-sharded → height-sharded

!image-20250601-142739.png

Resharding is just one example of a broader class of Tensor Manipulation Operations (TMs), which also includes:

- **Permutation**: Reordering tensor dimensions.
- **Concatenation**: Combining tensors along a specified axis.
- **Splitting**: Breaking a tensor into smaller shards or tiles.

**The workload computes**: Y=ReLU((A+B)×W)

**Compared Settings:**

- **DRAM**: Inputs and intermediate tensors are allocated in DRAM.
- **L1 Interleaved**: Inputs and/or intermediates are allocated in L1 with interleaved layout.
- **L1 Sharding**: Inputs and/or intermediates are allocated in L1 using **three sharding strategies**

 - Row sharding
 - Column sharding
 - Block / 2D sharding

**Metrics**:

- **End-to-end latency** for the full pipeline
- **Per-operator latency breakdown**: Add, MatMul, ReLU.
- **Memory metrics**:

 - Peak memory usage per tier (L1 vs DRAM),
 - Memory traffic (bytes read/write) and/or achieved bandwidth,

| | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| | | |
| | | |

---

---

The **Allocator** is the software engine responsible for managing on-device memory by defining which regions of memory are **available**, **reserved**, or **in use**. It assigns memory addresses to requesting components and maintains bookkeeping structures to track allocation and deallocation events.

The Allocator operates purely at the **address management level**. It does **not** directly interact with hardware memory nor perform data movement. Instead, it provides other system components with valid memory regions that can later be accessed by DMA engines or compute kernels

In Tenstorrent, memory storage units are referred to as **buffers**.

> **Buffer**: A buffer is a contiguous region of memory allocated by the Allocator to store data such as tensors, activations, weights, metadata, or control structures.

!image-20260122-041316.png

---

Different categories of buffers serve different roles in the system and therefore rely on distinct allocation policies. Each buffer type is associated with a corresponding allocator and **bank manager**, which is initialized once during device startup.

| Buffer Type | Purpose | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **DRAM** | Stores large tensors, model weights, or datasets that exceed on-chip capacity | High capacity; higher latency and lower bandwidth compared to L1 |
| **L1** | Holds intermediate tensors, activations, and circular buffers | On-chip SRAM with low latency and high bandwidth; limited size |
| **L1\_SMALL** | Stores control and metadata for core-level operations | Reserved sub-region of L1; size is dynamic and user-configurable via `l1_small_size`. Used in ops such as convolution and halo exchange |
| **TRACE** | Execution tracing and debugging data | *To be documented* |

---

- **DRAM** (high capacity)
- **SRAM (L1)** (low latency)

!image-20260122-041009.png

Although memory can be allocated in different directions, **all address bases originate from the lowest address**. The allocation direction only affects how subsequent memory regions grow.

- **DRAM**: Allocated **bottom-up** (low → high address)
- **SRAM (L1)**: Allocated **top-down** (high → low address)

This hybrid allocation strategy allows multiple buffer classes to coexist without fragmenting one another.

---
SRAM commonly hosts **circular buffers (CBs)**, which are used for streaming data and inter-core communication. There are two conceptual classes:

- Scoped to a program or operator execution
- Lifetime follows program execution (similar to local variables in a function)
- Typically managed by the program, not the Allocator
- Allocated **bottom-up**

- Managed by the Allocator
- Lifetime may span multiple operators
- Accessible by multiple cores
- Used for inter-core data exchange and shared state
- Allocated **top-down** to avoid collision with local CBs

!image-20260122-041221.png
> Before any program executes, the system performs a **validation step** to ensure that global and local CB regions do not overlap, preventing memory corruption.

!image-20260122-042352.png

There are some resolve strategies for this problem:

1. **Reduce Circular Buffer Size**

 - Decrease local or global CB capacity
 - Adjust operator tiling or streaming parameters
2. **Restructure Program Execution**

 - Split large operators into multiple execution stages
 - Reduce concurrent CB lifetimes
 - Reorder operations to shorten live ranges

---

- One bank per DRAM channel
- Example architectures:

 - Wormhole: 12 banks (~1GB each)
 - Blackhole: 8 banks (~4GB each)
- A portion of each bank is reserved for synchronization barriers

SRAM banks differ depending on the core type:

!image-20260122-041643.png

- **Worker (Compute) Core:**

 - 1 SRAM (large) bank per core
- **Storage Core**

 - `N` smaller SRAM banks
 - `N = SRAM_per_core / storage_core_bank_size`

section_title: SRAM banks ↔︎ Compute and Storage core
SRAM banks ↔︎ Compute and Storage core
SRAM banks ↔︎ Compute and Storage core
content:
→ Bank id is not mapping to the core respect/follow the core coordinate order in the core grid, this mapping can be config manually or using system random (shuffle). Since the data flow in the core grid is very large, shuffle to reduce the locality of the bank could help reduce the hotspot traffic over the NoC links.

---

Buffers are not placed directly into banks as monolithic blocks. Instead, data is divided into **pages**, a virtual-memory abstraction whose size depends on tensor shape and layout.

!image-20260122-041824.png

Conceptually:

1. A buffer is divided into pages
2. Pages are mapped across banks
3. Each bank receives its own address allocation

The allocation is take care of address\_limit to avoid conflict with the reserve space in L1. Also if there is "dependent allocator", then it can not allocate normally but find a region which is in **available range,** avoid conflict on other allocator (possibly in subdevice context).

---

!image-20260122-041848.png

The allocator uses a **free list–based memory management system** to track free and occupied address ranges. This avoids costly linear scans by maintaining linked structures of available memory regions and enables efficient first-fit allocation and deallocation.

| Strategy | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **First-Fit** | Selects the first free memory block that is large enough to satisfy the allocation request | - Fast allocation time due to minimal search - Simple to implement with a free list - Good performance under frequent allocations and deallocations | - Can lead to external fragmentation over time - Early memory regions tend to become fragmented |
| **Best-Fit** | Selects the smallest free memory block that can satisfy the allocation request | - Reduces wasted space within allocated blocks - Can lower internal fragmentation for same-size buffer classes | - Slower allocation due to full free-list scan - Increases external fragmentation by creating small unusable holes - More complex bookkeeping |

**Discussion:** Although best-fit strategies can reduce internal fragmentation for homogeneous allocation patterns, they do not account for fragmentation introduced by mixing different buffer classes or allocation directions. In contrast, first-fit allocation provides predictable performance and works efficiently with hybrid top-down and bottom-up allocation schemes, as used by the Tenstorrent allocator.

At normal allocation, the free list actually have (sub) Best fit strategy by divide the memory into multiple class of blocks, sort by range. Then it perform First-fit search among ascending order class range.

(Find more at: tt-metal/tt\_metal/impl/allocator/algorithms/free\_list\_opt.cpp at main · tenstorrent/tt-metal

---

To simplify address calculation across multiple banks, Tenstorrent employs a **lockstep allocation mechanism**.

**Definition**: Lockstep allocation enforces that **each bank reserves the same amount of memory for a given buffer**, even if the actual data stored in some banks is smaller.

!image-20260122-041925.png

Key properties:

- The first page of every buffer is always placed in **Bank 0**
- All banks mirror Bank 0's allocation pattern
- Enables simple, uniform address computation across banks
- Supports both interleaved and sharded tensor layouts

→ Allow **fast addressing** and management, support for **scale** up system

**Trade-off**: Lockstep allocation can introduce **internal fragmentation**, where allocated memory exceeds actual data size due to alignment and mirroring constraints.

We could check on example at **Appendix – Buffer Size Calculation**.

---

Occurs when allocated memory blocks are larger than the data they store, commonly due to:

- Alignment requirements
- Lockstep allocation
- Page padding

Occurs when memory is broken into many small, non-contiguous free regions after deallocations, even though total free memory is sufficient.

!image-20260122-042011.png

Tenstorrent employs several techniques to reduce external fragmentation:

- **Shift / reallocation** of buffers (to the top) where feasible

!image-20260122-042217.png

- **Pre-allocation** of long-lived buffers

!image-20260122-042240.png

- **In-place allocation**, allowing reuse of memory for input/output and CB buffers

---

The Allocator in Tenstorrent provides a robust, hardware-aware memory management layer that:

- Abstracts physical bank constraints
- Supports heterogeneous buffer lifetimes
- Enables efficient addressing via lockstep allocation
- Balances flexibility with performance and safety

This design is essential for scaling execution across many cores while maintaining predictable memory behavior.

---

Since lockstep allocation increases access speed by reducing address metadata and enabling easier scale-up, the trade-off is that it eliminates the flexibility required by a spatial execution model.

Because spatial execution relies on fine-grained, non-uniform placement and addressing, the uniform and synchronized nature of lockstep allocation makes a true spatial execution model impractical (or largely impossible).

!image-20260122-073712.png

!image-20260122-074103.png

One solution is that we will separate cores from global allocator so lockstep couldn't affect memory in L1. Separated cores now have to allocate memory itself, this allows us to asynchronously run programs on different sub-devices at the same time, instead of having to run all programs sequentially.

But synchronization across sub-devices can easily lead to stalls or system hangs due to incorrect or circular dependencies, especially when long-running programs depend on signals from other sub-devices or programs.

A SubDevice class represents a logical partition of a physical device, defined by their positions (logical coordinates of the specified core type of the full chip), their new allocator, and their new id. This is SubDeviceManager class, this class is used to manage sub\_devices,

Limitations:

- Sub-devices within a manager cannot share cores
- Dispatch cores cannot be assigned to sub-devices.
- No custom ID assignment
- …

!image-20260122-080339.png

---

We will shrink the global allocator by the user-specified size, and that size will be used for the local sub-device allocators. Users can specify a size of zero for the local allocator size, in which case no sub-device allocators will be created, and all memory will remain managed by the global allocator.

→ Memory Isolation.

!image-20260122-085334.png

---

By default, all read, write, and program execution stall until all previously issued programs on the device have completed. (see temporal ops placement. Stall groups enable synchronization across multiple sub-devices. When a stall group is set, command queue operations wait for all sub-devices in the group to complete before proceeding.

By limiting the stall group to only the relevant sub-devices, reads, writes, and program launches can avoid unnecessary blocking. This allows operations to wait only on producer sub-devices rather than all sub-devices.

→ Enable spatial ops placement.

---

Since local semaphores enable synchronization and communication but are not visible to programs that run before/after it. Sub-devices need to execute series of programs, so we need global semaphores that won't be deallocated when programs executed.

---

With the same reason, local CBs cannot be directly used reading/writing between different cores. Global circular buffers reserve a persistent L1 memory region that remains valid across programs until explicitly destroyed. Enable producer–consumer communication between different cores.

---

The key idea is to present a multi-device multi-host system as:

- a large virtual device that has
- a distributed shared memory space
- a set of programmable Tensix cores.

Unlike conventional architectures, programming cores distributed over multiple devices or multiple host machines will not fundamentally alter the programming model.

Ex: As device number increased - a 64-device TGG cluster, the host creates 192 threads, forcing multiple threads per CPU core and leading to performance issues.

!image-20260123-021205.png

TT-Distributed exposes two 4x2 and a single 4x4 Cluster as the same Virtual Mesh, this approach solve scalability issues seen with the TT-NN virtualization layer.

- **Introduce Unified Distributed Memory across the entire Virtual Mesh** (including Multi-host systems)

- **Introduce Unified Distributed Memory across the entire Virtual Mesh** (including Multi-host systems)

 - Introduce MeshBuffer – a buffer that is allocated across a Virtual Mesh and can be allocated in lock-step across physical devices
 - MeshBuffer across a mesh of devices is an extension of Buffer across a mesh of cores
 - Any core on any each physical device within the Virtual Mesh can Read/Write to any other address in the Mesh (Unified Distributed Memory)

!image-20260123-021808.png

TT-Mesh, hmm, like above section, this pic says what i want to.

!image-20260123-022649.png
Buffer size changes based on **data type** and **memory configuration**.

```
buffer_size = tensor_volumn x element_size(byte)
```

Ex: Buffer Size of Tensor A:

- shape: [4, 256, 256]
- dtype: BFLOAT16

```
tensor_volumn = 4 x 256 x 256 = 262144
element_size = 2(byte)
=> buffer_size = 524288(byte)
```
Ex: Block Float (BFP) must be used with Tile Layout (32x32):

```
bfloat8b_buffer_size = 32 x 32 x 1 (Data byte) + 4 x 16 (Exponent byte)
=> bfloat8b_buffer_size = 1088 (byte)
bfloat4b_buffer_size = 32 x 32 x 0.5 (Data byte) + 4 x 16 (Exponent byte)
=> bfloat4b_buffer_size = 576 (byte)
```

| **Dtype** | **Size (Byte)** |
| --- | --- |
| BFLOAT16 | 2 |
| FLOAT32 | 4 |
| INT32/UINT32 | 4 |
| UINT16 | 2 |
| UINT8 | 1 |
| BFLOAT8\_B | 1 per datum + 4 × 16B shared exponent |
| BFLOAT4\_B | 0.5 per datum + 4 × 16B shared exponent |

```
page_size = page_shape x element_size
```

Ex: Continued example: shape: [4, 256, 256], dtype: BFLOAT16.

- Row-major Layout:

```
page_size = 256 x 2 = 512 (byte)
```

- Tile Layout:

```
page_size = 32 x 32 x 2 = 2048 (byte)
```

| **Layout** | **Page Shape** |
| --- | --- |
| Row-Major Layout | 1 x W (width - shape's last element) |
| Tile Layout | 32 x 32 |

section_title: Step 3: Calculate buffer size per bank
content:
```
ALIGNMENT_BYTES = 16 (I don't know, it's fixed)
```

Number of pages: (floor was used because we only count fully allocated page)

```
num_pages = floor(buffer_size / page_size)
```

section_title: Step 3: Calculate buffer size per bank
content:
Pages per bank: (ceil was used because lockstep allocation is used)

```
num_distributed_pages = ceil(num_pages / num_cores)
```

Bytes per bank:

```
buffer_size_per_bank = num_distributed_pages x round_up(page_size, ALIGNMENT_BYTES)
```

Round up mean round page\_size to the nearest multiple of ALIGNMENT\_BYTES

Ex: Continued example:

- buffer\_size: 524288 (byte)
- page\_size: 2048 (byte)
- num\_cores: 8

```
num_pages = floor(524288 / 2048) = 256
num_distributed_pages = ceil(256 / 8) = 32
buffer_size_per_bank = 32 x round_up(2048, 16) = 32 x 2048 = 65536 (byte)
```

| **Buffer Type** | **Memory Location** | **Use Case** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| DRAM | Off-chip banks | Large tensors, weights, activations | high capacity with higher latency and lower bandwidth |
| L1 | On-chip SRAM | Intermediate values, kernel I/O | low latency and high bandwidth; limited in size. |
| L1\_SMALL | On-chip SRAM | Small frequent allocations (ex: control metadata for core-level operations.) | Reserved region of L1 |
| SYSTEM\_MEMORY | Host memory | DMA staging | |
| TRACE | Device memory | Program trace recording | |

Visualizer here (Arena Allocator, Stack Allocator, Free-list Allocator, Pool Allocator Visualized, Buddy Algorithm

- **Freelist**: free blocks are stored in a list; allocation searches the list base on allocation policies; free inserts back, coalescing (optional).
- **Linear**: allocate by incrementing a pointer; free is "reset the whole arena" (or rewind to a marker).
- **Pool**: carve memory into equal-sized objects; free objects tracked (often via a freelist inside the slab).
- **Buddy**: memory managed in power-of-two blocks; split/merge "buddies".

| **Strategy** | **Pros** | **Cons** | **Best for** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Freelist | - Good space efficiency (can handle variable sizes). - Can be fast if segregated by size class. | - External fragmentation risk (free space exists but not in useful shapes). - Search complexity: O(n). - Coalescing can add overhead. | Variable-size allocations where you want a reasonable balance of speed and memory use. |
| Linear | - O(1) allocate, minimal metadata. - Great cache locality. - No fragmentation *within* an arena lifetime. | - Can't free individual objects. - Memory can balloon if you need multiple lifetimes. (me too) | Short-lived allocations with clear phases. |
| Pool | - alloc/free fast: O(1) complexity. - Minimal fragmentation for that object size. - Great cache locality | - Not flexible for variable sizes (needs many pools / size classes). - Can waste memory if your live-set distribution shifts. | Many allocations of the same size/type |

| Buddy | - alloc/free and coalescing efficient: O(log N) complexity. - Coalescing is straightforward and deterministic. - Good for page-level / kernel allocators. | - Internal fragmentation - Poor fit for many odd-sized small allocations unless layered with caches. | Systems memory management, large blocks, page allocators, predictable merges. |

Normally, you have to By connecting unallocated regions of memory together in a linked list (first word of each unallocated region as a pointer to the next), a freelist operates dynamic memory allocation, and most suitable for allocating a memory pool where all objects have the same size.

- Free a region → Link to the free list.
- Allocate a region → Remove region from the end of the list and use it.

Allocation policies (also called placement policies) determine how an allocator selects a free block to satisfy an allocation request. Common placement policies include first-fit, best-fit, and next-fit, each offering different trade-offs between allocation speed and fragmentation.

- First-fit: Selects the first memory block whose size is greater than *n*.
- Best-fit: Selects the smallest memory block whose size is greater than *n*.
- Worst-fit: Selects the largest memory block whose size is greater than *n*.

| **Policies** | **Pros** | **Cons** | **Usage** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| First-fit | Fast, Easy to implement | Causes external fragmentation | Systems that prioritize speed. |
| Best-fit | Minimizes wasted space for each allocation | Slow (must search through all blocks), Creates many small fragments that are hard to reuse | Systems that focus on memory efficiency and have a low number of allocation requests. |
| Worst-fit | Keeps medium-sized blocks available for future requests | Wastes memory, Low efficiency | Rarely used (smile) |

---

- Tensors are stored in a 2D memory space known as a `buffer`.
- An N-dimensional tensor is represented as a 2D memory object by combining the outer dimensions into a single dimension while keeping the inner dimension as the second dimension.

Example: a tensor with dimensions `[2x3x2x4]` will be represented in memory as a `[12x4]` tensor, where 2x3x2 is squeezed into 12.

!image-20251231-071012.png

At the most granular level, the smallest blocks that constructs of a tensor are call **pages**.

**Page** is the **smallest unit** of a Tensor handled by TT-software

!image-20251231-071950.png

Tensors are allocated in Tenstorrent device by different hierarchical concepts:

- **Memory banks**: **DRAM** and **L1 buffer**.
- **Memory layouts:** **Interleaved** and **Sharded**.
- **Tensor layouts**: **Row-Major** and **Tile layout**.

---

- One bank per DRAM channel
- Example architectures:

 - Wormhole: 12 banks (~1GB each)
 - Blackhole: 8 banks (~4GB each)
- A portion of each bank is reserved for synchronization barriers

SRAM banks differ depending on the core type:

!image-20260122-041643.png

- **Worker (Compute) Core:**

 - 1 SRAM (large) bank per core
- **Storage Core**

 - `N` smaller SRAM banks
 - `N = SRAM_per_core / storage_core_bank_size`

---

This describes how *elements* within a tensor are mapped to pages, with each page representing the granularity of the tensor in memory. Pages are stored consecutively within a buffer. Depending on tensor layout, page has different sizes:

- **Row-Major layout:** page\_size = 1 \* W (one stick)
- **Tile layout**: page\_size = TILE\_SIZE (currently support size of 32x32)

Each row of a 2D tensor corresponds to a single page in our buffer.

e.g. with a 64x64 tensor, each row is associated with a separate page, resulting in a total of 64 pages in the buffer.

!image-20250602-014903.png

In a tiled tensor, pages are represented as 2D tiles, with the default tile size being 32x32. For example, with a 64x64 tensor, this results in 4 tiles, each of size 32x32.

!image-20250602-015121.png

The hardware architecture supports tile shapes of `32x32` , `16x32` , `4x32`, `2x32`, `1x32`. **However currently TT-Metalium only supports 32x32, with limited functionality for 16x32 in some ops such as matmul.**

section_title: 3.2.1 Faces and Tile Organization in HW-Level
content:
Data inside the tile **isn't contiguous**. Each tile is split into faces ("sub-tiles"). By default, tile size is 32x32, and face size is 16x16. 4 faces per tile and each tile lies one after another contiguously in memory in row-major fashion (i.e., face0->face1->face2->face3).

The reason for using faces is that **the matrix engine natively multiplies 16x16 matrices**, and tile multiplication is decomposed into multiple face multiplications.

!image-20260122-081020.png

---

By spreading memory addresses evenly across memory banks. Using round-robin, with n banks, memory location i resides in bank i mod n. This led to:

- Contiguous memory reads and writes- using each memory bank in turn, instead of using the same one repeatedly -> reduced waiting for memory banks to become ready for the operations.

!Interleaving.gif

Ex: Interleaved memory layout 12 pages across 16 cores

!image-20250601-113417.png

However, interleaved memory does not always provide optimal performance for all kernels. It can lead to bottlenecks since Although data is located in short time, but it need to move to another location for computing → Long data movement + bottlenecks on a specific buffer (buffer used here instead of bank in case data is moved to DRAM).

!image-20260122-063654.png

Sharded memory is used for kernels that require more control over data placement and access patterns. Sharded memory allows you to specify which tiles should store which data flexing between these schemes:

Ex: 3 x 4 pages need to be allocated

!image-20260122-071552.png

Data is partitioned across tiles based on the *height dimension* → All page in row-oriented must be respectively placed in the same core.

!image-20260122-072323.png

!image-20260122-070323.png

Data is partitioned across tiles based on the *width dimension* → All page in col-oriented must be respectively placed in the same core.

!image-20250601-135305.png

Data is partitioned across tiles on both width and height dimensions → All pages in one block must be in the same core (missing pages will be padding with 0):

!image-20260122-071905.png

ND sharding preserves the original tensor dimensionality and can shard across any combination of dimensions (batch, sequence length, channels, etc.) Interactive Visualization

**Example**: 3D Sharding

!image-20260122-073627.png

Sometimes, data could be evenly divided between cores so padding with 0 strategy is used, this section is intentionally placed here to make the document longer.

!image-20260122-075012.png

---

- Unary operations: relu(), sin(), cos(), …
- Elementwise binary operations: x^2, a+b, …
- Tensor manipulation operations

 - Resharding
 - Permutation
 - Concatenation
 - Splitting

Can be executed locally *without needing full data movement* if layout is compatible and data is already on SRAM

**Example**: Tensor A [1, 1, 128, 96] is tile layout, height sharded (shape [32, 96]) on 4x4 grid row major performs a unary operation (power 2) results output with same memory configuration.

!image-20260122-062914.png

=> For unary operation, tensor can be either sharded or interleaved, since the **unary operation is layout-invariant**, that is the computation does not depend on how data is placed across banks

With L1 sharded tensor layout, eltwise binary operation takes 2 tensors and performs computation directly *without any data movement*.

**Example**: Tensor A already lives in L1, move Tensor B to L1 and then add C = A + B.

!image-20250601-141148.png

In certain scenarios, it's necessary to **change the shard configuration** of a tensor — for example, converting a tensor from **width-sharded** to **height-sharded** layout.

- **Resharding** involves **data movement** and **communication between cores**.
- It is required when the current shard layout is incompatible with the needs of a downstream operation or kernel.

**Example**: width-sharded → height-sharded

!image-20250601-142739.png

Resharding is just one example of a broader class of Tensor Manipulation Operations (TMs), which also includes:

- **Permutation**: Reordering tensor dimensions.
- **Concatenation**: Combining tensors along a specified axis.
- **Splitting**: Breaking a tensor into smaller shards or tiles.

**The workload computes**: Y=ReLU((A+B)×W)

**Compared Settings:**

- **DRAM**: Inputs and intermediate tensors are allocated in DRAM.
- **L1 Interleaved**: Inputs and/or intermediates are allocated in L1 with interleaved layout.
- **L1 Sharding**: Inputs and/or intermediates are allocated in L1 using **three sharding strategies**

 - Row sharding
 - Column sharding
 - Block / 2D sharding

**Metrics**:

- **End-to-end latency** for the full pipeline
- **Per-operator latency breakdown**: Add, MatMul, ReLU.
- **Memory metrics**:

 - Peak memory usage per tier (L1 vs DRAM),
 - Memory traffic (bytes read/write) and/or achieved bandwidth,

| | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| | | |
| | | |

---

---
Buffer size changes based on **data type** and **memory configuration**.

```
buffer_size = tensor_volumn x element_size(byte)
```

Ex: Buffer Size of Tensor A:

- shape: [4, 256, 256]
- dtype: BFLOAT16

```
tensor_volumn = 4 x 256 x 256 = 262144
element_size = 2(byte)
=> buffer_size = 524288(byte)
```
Ex: Block Float (BFP) must be used with Tile Layout (32x32):

```
bfloat8b_buffer_size = 32 x 32 x 1 (Data byte) + 4 x 16 (Exponent byte)
=> bfloat8b_buffer_size = 1088 (byte)
bfloat4b_buffer_size = 32 x 32 x 0.5 (Data byte) + 4 x 16 (Exponent byte)
=> bfloat4b_buffer_size = 576 (byte)
```

| **Dtype** | **Size (Byte)** |
| --- | --- |
| BFLOAT16 | 2 |
| FLOAT32 | 4 |
| INT32/UINT32 | 4 |
| UINT16 | 2 |
| UINT8 | 1 |
| BFLOAT8\_B | 1 per datum + 4 × 16B shared exponent |
| BFLOAT4\_B | 0.5 per datum + 4 × 16B shared exponent |

```
page_size = page_shape x element_size
```

Ex: Continued example: shape: [4, 256, 256], dtype: BFLOAT16.

- Row-major Layout:

```
page_size = 256 x 2 = 512 (byte)
```

- Tile Layout:

```
page_size = 32 x 32 x 2 = 2048 (byte)
```

| **Layout** | **Page Shape** |
| --- | --- |
| Row-Major Layout | 1 x W (width - shape's last element) |
| Tile Layout | 32 x 32 |

section_title: Step 3: Calculate buffer size per bank
content:
```
ALIGNMENT_BYTES = 16 (I don't know, it's fixed)
```

Number of pages: (floor was used because we only count fully allocated page)

```
num_pages = floor(buffer_size / page_size)
```

section_title: Step 3: Calculate buffer size per bank
content:
Pages per bank: (ceil was used because lockstep allocation is used)

```
num_distributed_pages = ceil(num_pages / num_cores)
```

Bytes per bank:

```
buffer_size_per_bank = num_distributed_pages x round_up(page_size, ALIGNMENT_BYTES)
```

Round up mean round page\_size to the nearest multiple of ALIGNMENT\_BYTES

Ex: Continued example:

- buffer\_size: 524288 (byte)
- page\_size: 2048 (byte)
- num\_cores: 8

```
num_pages = floor(524288 / 2048) = 256
num_distributed_pages = ceil(256 / 8) = 32
buffer_size_per_bank = 32 x round_up(2048, 16) = 32 x 2048 = 65536 (byte)
```

| **Buffer Type** | **Memory Location** | **Use Case** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| DRAM | Off-chip banks | Large tensors, weights, activations | high capacity with higher latency and lower bandwidth |
| L1 | On-chip SRAM | Intermediate values, kernel I/O | low latency and high bandwidth; limited in size. |
| L1\_SMALL | On-chip SRAM | Small frequent allocations (ex: control metadata for core-level operations.) | Reserved region of L1 |
| SYSTEM\_MEMORY | Host memory | DMA staging | |
| TRACE | Device memory | Program trace recording | |

Visualizer here (Arena Allocator, Stack Allocator, Free-list Allocator, Pool Allocator Visualized, Buddy Algorithm

- **Freelist**: free blocks are stored in a list; allocation searches the list base on allocation policies; free inserts back, coalescing (optional).
- **Linear**: allocate by incrementing a pointer; free is "reset the whole arena" (or rewind to a marker).
- **Pool**: carve memory into equal-sized objects; free objects tracked (often via a freelist inside the slab).
- **Buddy**: memory managed in power-of-two blocks; split/merge "buddies".

| **Strategy** | **Pros** | **Cons** | **Best for** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Freelist | - Good space efficiency (can handle variable sizes). - Can be fast if segregated by size class. | - External fragmentation risk (free space exists but not in useful shapes). - Search complexity: O(n). - Coalescing can add overhead. | Variable-size allocations where you want a reasonable balance of speed and memory use. |
| Linear | - O(1) allocate, minimal metadata. - Great cache locality. - No fragmentation *within* an arena lifetime. | - Can't free individual objects. - Memory can balloon if you need multiple lifetimes. (me too) | Short-lived allocations with clear phases. |
| Pool | - alloc/free fast: O(1) complexity. - Minimal fragmentation for that object size. - Great cache locality | - Not flexible for variable sizes (needs many pools / size classes). - Can waste memory if your live-set distribution shifts. | Many allocations of the same size/type |

| Buddy | - alloc/free and coalescing efficient: O(log N) complexity. - Coalescing is straightforward and deterministic. - Good for page-level / kernel allocators. | - Internal fragmentation - Poor fit for many odd-sized small allocations unless layered with caches. | Systems memory management, large blocks, page allocators, predictable merges. |

Normally, you have to By connecting unallocated regions of memory together in a linked list (first word of each unallocated region as a pointer to the next), a freelist operates dynamic memory allocation, and most suitable for allocating a memory pool where all objects have the same size.

- Free a region → Link to the free list.
- Allocate a region → Remove region from the end of the list and use it.

Allocation policies (also called placement policies) determine how an allocator selects a free block to satisfy an allocation request. Common placement policies include first-fit, best-fit, and next-fit, each offering different trade-offs between allocation speed and fragmentation.

- First-fit: Selects the first memory block whose size is greater than *n*.
- Best-fit: Selects the smallest memory block whose size is greater than *n*.
- Worst-fit: Selects the largest memory block whose size is greater than *n*.

| **Policies** | **Pros** | **Cons** | **Usage** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| First-fit | Fast, Easy to implement | Causes external fragmentation | Systems that prioritize speed. |
| Best-fit | Minimizes wasted space for each allocation | Slow (must search through all blocks), Creates many small fragments that are hard to reuse | Systems that focus on memory efficiency and have a low number of allocation requests. |
| Worst-fit | Keeps medium-sized blocks available for future requests | Wastes memory, Low efficiency | Rarely used (smile) |

section_title: Matrix Multiplication Optimization for Edge AI
content:
Matrix multiplication is a fundamental operation in deep neural networks. As artificial intelligence and machine learning workloads increasingly move toward edge devices, optimizing matrix multiplication under strict hardware constraints becomes a key challenge. Efficient implementations of this operation are critical to achieving acceptable performance while minimizing resource usage such as memory bandwidth, storage capacity, and compute power.

This report demonstrates how matrix multiplication can be optimized through different loop orderings and data access patterns, providing insights into how these choices affect hardware efficiency.

The animations in this document show matrix `A` in **red** and matrix `B` in **blue**. The elements of `A` and `B` accessed during code execution are highlighted in darker shades. Each element of the output matrix `C` is the accumulation of `K` partial sums and is shown in shades of **green**.

!image-20260202-065330.png

---

**(Check at 3m25s)**

In this naive implementation, data access is **temporally isolated**. Each element of `A` and `B` is loaded repeatedly without reuse across iterations. As a result, memory bandwidth usage is high and hardware efficiency is low.

---

section_title: Loop Permutations with `K` as the Outer Loop (Efficient method?)
Loop Permutations with `K` as the Outer Loop (Efficient method?)
content:
**(Check at 6m00s)**

With this loop order (`k → m → n`), each column of matrix `A` is reused across all rows of `B`. This reuse significantly reduces memory access cost and improves hardware efficiency.

From a higher-level perspective, this version accesses matrix `A` **column by column** and matrix `B` **row by row**, which is the reverse of the naive implementation.

---

From the visualization, we observe that reading a column of `A` and a row of `B` produces an **outer product**, which contributes directly to the final output matrix `C`. Each outer product generates a full matrix of partial sums that are accumulated across iterations.

This approach enables parallel execution, where multiple cores or threads can process independent row–column pairs. However, unlike the naive method that updates a single element of `C` at a time, the outer product generates a full `M × N` partial result. This requires significantly more on-chip storage to hold partial sums and intermediate data.

This leads to a critical question:

**How can we apply the outer product method when the matrix size (and a pair of column in A, row in B) exceeds available hardware storage?**

---

When matrix sizes are too large to fit in on-chip memory, we apply a **divide-and-conquer** strategy known as **tiled matrix multiplication**.

The key idea is that to compute a **tile** (block) of the output matrix `C`, only a corresponding stripe of matrix `A` and a stripe of matrix `B` are required. The computation proceeds iteratively by accumulating partial results from smaller sub-blocks.

Each tile of `C` is computed as the sum of inner products between blocks from a row stripe of `A` and a column stripe of `B`. For these smaller block multiplications, the outer product approach becomes feasible again because the data fits within hardware constraints.

This tiling strategy allows scalable matrix multiplication while preserving data reuse and parallelism.

!image-20260202-064701.png

!image-20260202-064701.png

For an output of size ***Mtile × Ntile***, the computation is performed by iteratively multiplying a tile from the **A** **stripe** in row order with a tile from the **B** **stripe** in column order. Each multiplication spans the inner dimension *Ktile*. The choice of *Ktile* depends on the characteristics and capabilities of the system's computation engine

---

section_title: Matrix Multiplication with Reuse in tt-metal
content:
In the tt-metal library, matrix multiplication is implemented using the hardware-efficient matrix multiply unit with a fixed tile size of **32 × 32**. To avoid confusion with this hardware tile, we use the term **block matrix multiplication** to describe the higher-level decomposition strategy discussed here.

---

We define the inner (share) dimension Ktile in the Tile Outer Product as `k`

Given an output matrix of size **Mt × Nt**, the matrix is decomposed into multiple **blocks**.

Each block has size **Mpc × Npc**, and **each block is assigned to exactly one core**. Then a core will use the Outer Product strategy of matrix multiplication to optimize reuse.

Thus, the computation is distributed spatially across the core grid, where each core is responsible for computing one output block of matrix `C`.

This block-level decomposition enables parallel execution across the 2D core grid and determines how the workload is mapped onto the available hardware resources.

---

Although a single core is responsible for computing one block of size *Mpc × Npc*, once a column tile **Co** from **A** (of size *Mpc × k*) and a row tile **R** from **B** (of size *k × Npc*) are loaded, the product **Co × R** can, in principle, be computed in various ways. However, for efficient execution, a decomposition strategy is required to fully utilize the matrix computation engine (for example, when the destination registers can hold up to 8 tiles).

To achieve this, each core further decomposes its assigned block into smaller subblocks.

The subblock size is selected to align with the hardware's efficient execution granularity, specifically the maximum number of output tiles that can be simultaneously held in destination registers (e.g., 8 tiles).

At the subblock level, computation is performed to produce a subblock-sized output matrix, which is then written to an intermediate buffer. For each pair of **Co** and **R**, this process contributes a partial result to the final output matrix **C**.

Note that, for each core, the block size (*Mpc × Npc*) as well as the sizes of **Co** and **R** must be carefully specified to ensure that all required data can be stored simultaneously in the core's L1 memory.

The choice of subblock height and width is **not arbitrary**.

Subblock dimensions are selected from a small set of supported shapes that due to the storage of the destination register.

Importantly:

- The **selection of subblock size happens at runtime on the host**,
- but the **kernel executed on the core is compiled with fixed subblock dimensions**.

This design allows the kernel to:

- Fully unroll inner loops
- Allocate registers statically for accumulators

Using runtime-variable subblock sizes inside the kernel would significantly reduce performance due to loss of unrolling, increased branching, and poor register utilization.

---

section_title: Core Grid Mapping and Block Assignment
content:
The assignment of blocks to cores is not a simple row-major split.

Instead, tt-metal constructs a **2D (sub)grid of cores** that:

- Can collectively cover all output blocks.
- Respects on-core memory constraints
- Allows blocks to share the same compile-time kernel configuration (especially subblock size).

This more complex mapping ensures that:

- Most cores execute the same optimized kernel variant.
- Slow fallback cases (such as 1×1 subblocks) are minimized.

---

In tt-metal, matrix multiplication with reuse is achieved through a hierarchical decomposition:

1. **Block level**:

The output matrix (Mt × Nt) is partitioned into blocks (Mpc × Npc), with each block assigned to a core.

2. **Subblock level**:

Each core further decomposes its block into subblocks sized to utilize hardware register.

3. **Kernel specialization**:

Subblock sizes are chosen at runtime but enforced as compile-time constants inside the kernel, preserving high performance.

This design balances flexibility and performance, allowing tt-metal to efficiently map matrix multiplication onto the underlying hardware while maximizing data reuse.

content: > Click here for viewing the visualization of matmul process and data loading with example.
